charts.chart_read: Three bars show the same model given proper worked examples, no examples, or scrambled nonsense examples. Taller is better (more programs fully fixed). Falling bars mean the examples were genuinely being used.
charts.chart_takeaway: Scrambled examples score far below even having none, proving the model relies on coherent examples rather than ignoring them.

qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.concept_primer: Imagine a student who reviews only their own past correct solutions. That self-study could pay off two ways: nailing familiar problems on the first try, or finally cracking harder ones they never solved before.
qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.plain_question: If a small AI reviews its own correct answers, does it just get faster at what it already knew, or can it start solving problems it never could?
qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.plain_answer: Both. After the model studied its own verified correct answers, it solved easy problems on the first try far more often (60% to 80%) and tripled how many harder problems it could ever solve (15% to 45%) — beating the do-nothing option of just retrying the original model.
qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.why_it_matters: If you plan to let a model make several attempts anyway, first have it study its own past correct solutions: the ceiling of what it can solve rises, not just its speed. But self-study cannot teach a difficulty it never once solved.

key_numbers.label: Easy problems solved on first try
key_numbers.sub: faster at what it already knew (self-study)

key_numbers.label: Harder problems ever solved
key_numbers.sub: tripled — cracked ones it never had before

key_numbers.label: Hardest problems solved
key_numbers.sub: no self-solved examples to learn from

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-study helps easy problems on first try, harder problems overall
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by problem difficulty (level 1 easiest to level 4 hardest). Within each group, four bars: the original model's first try, the original with many tries, the self-taught model's first try, and the self-taught with many tries. Taller means more problems solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: For easy problems the self-taught first-try bar rises toward the many-tries level; for the next-harder problems the self-taught many-tries bar triples above the original, while the hardest groups stay flat at zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: On harder problems, self-study solves far more at every number of tries

charts.chart_takeaway: The self-taught line sits well above the original at every try count and ends about three times higher, meaning it reaches harder problems the original never solved even with all 16 tries.

qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.verdict_tag: A smarter picker cannot beat just trying more
qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.concept_primer: Picture solving a puzzle by making many rough attempts, then choosing one to submit. Failing can mean the right attempt was never made at all, or it was made but never chosen.
qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.plain_question: When the model cannot solve a hard puzzle, is it because it never produces a correct attempt, or because it produces one but fails to pick it?
qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.plain_answer: It never produces one. Whenever a correct attempt does appear among many tries, a simple check always grabs it, so picking is free; the failures come entirely from the right attempt never being made.
qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.why_it_matters: Building a cleverer picker is wasted effort here, since picking already works perfectly. To solve harder puzzles, the model must be steered to produce the right attempt in the first place, using better tools or lessons from past solutions.

key_numbers.label: One try vs many tries (medium puzzle)
key_numbers.sub: share solved when you make many attempts instead of one

key_numbers.label: Extra gain from a smarter picker
key_numbers.sub: a simple check already grabs every correct attempt made

key_numbers.label: Deep puzzles, even with many tries
key_numbers.sub: a correct attempt almost never appears no matter how many you make

charts.chart_plain_title: On one puzzle machine, every way of picking lands at the same height
charts.chart_read: Bar height is the share of puzzles solved, taller is better; each cluster is a puzzle depth from easy to hard. The four bars are one try, two smarter picking methods, and the best-possible ceiling; the three picking bars sit at identical heights.
charts.chart_takeaway: By depth 3 and 4 every bar collapses to near zero together, showing the right attempt is simply never made no matter how you pick.

charts.chart_plain_title: On the other machine, picking many attempts far beats one try
charts.chart_read: Bar height is the share of puzzles solved, taller is better; each cluster is a puzzle depth. The single-try bar is short while the two picking bars and the best-possible ceiling all rise together and match exactly.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three picking bars tower over the single-try bar yet land exactly on the ceiling, meaning picking adds nothing beyond simply making more attempts.

charts.chart_plain_title: More attempts help on easy puzzles but stall on deep ones
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many attempts you make, the vertical axis is the chance a correct attempt appears, higher is better; each line is a puzzle depth, shallow on top and deep at the bottom.
charts.chart_takeaway: The deep-puzzle lines stay flat and near the floor however many attempts you make, so extra tries cannot conjure a correct attempt that never shows up.

qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.verdict_tag: Following steps holds everywhere; mental tracking varies
qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.concept_primer: Picture the model as a cook working three ways with recipes: following a written one to the letter, cooking a dish in its head without touching ingredients, and guessing a recipe from the finished plate.
qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.plain_question: Are the model's strengths and weaknesses the same across genuinely different kinds of puzzles, or specific to just one kind?
qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.plain_answer: It depends. Following written steps stays near-perfect and guessing hidden rules stays near-impossible across every puzzle type, but its head-only accuracy swings wildly by task, from near-zero on text to strong on compact numbers.
qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.why_it_matters: Don't spend an outside helper tool on steps the model already follows flawlessly. Add one only when it must juggle a bulky, hard-to-hold state like text, and never expect it to invent rules from examples alone.

key_numbers.label: Following written steps
key_numbers.sub: correct code, every puzzle type, every depth

key_numbers.label: Doing it in its head, deepest puzzles
key_numbers.sub: text falls to zero, compact numbers stay strong

key_numbers.label: Guessing the hidden rule, deeper puzzles
key_numbers.sub: collapses in all three puzzle types

charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines run flat along the ceiling, so faithfully following given steps is effortless no matter the puzzle type or how many steps pile up.

charts.chart_plain_title: Doing it in its head: accuracy depends entirely on the puzzle type
charts.chart_read: Left to right stacks more steps; height is how often the model got the right answer computing purely in its head. One line per puzzle type; higher is better. The compact-number line stays high while the text line hugs the floor.
charts.chart_takeaway: The lines fan far apart, with compact number states near the top and text at zero, so head-only tracking hinges on how bulky the state is.

charts.chart_plain_title: Guessing the hidden rule collapses toward random in every puzzle type
charts.chart_read: Left to right stacks more steps; height is how often the model reverse-engineered the hidden rule from examples alone. One line per puzzle type; higher is better, but all lines start moderate and plunge toward the bottom.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line dives to the floor as steps stack up, showing the model can run rules it cannot invent, uniformly across all three puzzle types.

qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.verdict_tag: External step-search wins on every puzzle type
qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.concept_primer: Think of each puzzle as flat-pack furniture: getting the right sequence of assembly steps (the blueprint) is the hard part, while plugging in the exact screws (the numbers) is easy once the blueprint is set.
qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.plain_question: Does the trick of searching for the right sequence of steps still work on completely different kinds of puzzles?
qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.plain_answer: Yes. On all three puzzle types, the model on its own almost never found the right blueprint, but an external search that tries blueprints and plugs in the numbers solved nearly every task, far beating the model's near-zero.
qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.why_it_matters: For a model this size, don't expect it to plan multi-step solutions on its own. Pair it with an external tool that proposes candidate blueprints and checks them; the numbers fall into place easily once the sequence is right.

charts.chart_read: Bars group four approaches (the model's first guess, whether its own steps match the needed sequence, filling numbers into a correct given sequence, and an external search over sequences), colored by puzzle type; taller means more puzzles solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two left bars sit near the floor while the two right bars nearly touch the ceiling on every puzzle type: the model alone fails, but supplying or searching the step-sequence solves almost everything.

charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines climb only modestly and stay far below the near-100% of the correct-sequence search, confirming the exact order of steps, not the numbers, is the real obstacle.

qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.verdict_tag: Search crosses the wall, planning doesn't
qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.concept_primer: Faced with a hard three-step puzzle, the model can guess the whole answer in one leap, or break it into single moves and let a calculator check each move before the next.
qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.plain_question: Can breaking a hard multi-step task into single checked steps let this small model solve puzzles it fails to crack in one leap?
qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.plain_answer: It depends. Breaking the task into checked steps solves about three times more of the hardest puzzles than one leap does, but a blind step-by-step search does just as well, so the win comes from the step-by-step structure plus the calculator, not the model's own judgment.
qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.why_it_matters: When a small model stalls on multi-step problems, wrap it in a step-by-step search with an external checker instead of trusting one big attempt, then train it on the solutions found to lock in modest gains.

key_numbers.label: Genuinely deep tasks solved
key_numbers.sub: one leap never solved these; step search sometimes did

charts.chart_takeaway: Both step-search bars roughly triple the one-leap bar on the hardest tasks, but blind search matches the model-guided one, so the structure does the work.

charts.chart_takeaway: The guided line climbs fast then flattens early while blind search keeps rising to catch or pass it, meaning guidance saves effort but hits a ceiling.

charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare the untrained model against two self-trained runs across four one-shot solving measures; taller is better everywhere.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every self-trained bar sits above the untrained one, showing that practicing the found solutions modestly lifts the model's own one-shot solving.

charts.chart_read: Two bars show the step-search solve rate on genuinely deep tasks versus tasks that only looked deep but really need fewer steps; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The look-deep bar is full while the truly-deep bar is short, so most of the headline win came from tasks that were easier than their label suggested.

qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.verdict_tag: The bottleneck is missing clues, not strategy
qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.concept_primer: Picture a game of 20 questions to identify a hidden rule: a fair player picks the smartest next question without knowing the answer, while a cheating player who already knows the answer sets the ceiling.
qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.plain_question: When the model struggles to pin down a hidden rule, is it because it lacks a clever questioning strategy, or because it just needs more clues?
qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.plain_answer: It needs more clues. In the hardest setup the fair player scores only 4% while the cheating player hits 74%, but simply allowing more questions lifts the fair player to 86%, nearly matching the cheat and closing most of the gap.
qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.why_it_matters: When a model fails at this kind of deduction, spend effort feeding it more evidence or more questioning steps rather than hunting for a cleverer questioning strategy; the fair strategy is already close to the best possible.

key_numbers.label: Fair player, more questions allowed
key_numbers.sub: same 4 clues but 10 questions allowed

key_numbers.label: Fair player, more starting clues
key_numbers.sub: 16 clues up front, still only 3 questions

key_numbers.label: Cheating player's ceiling
key_numbers.sub: in the hardest setup, knowing the answer in advance

charts.chart_plain_title: Fair player nearly catches the cheat once given more clues or questions
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare the fair player against the cheating player who already knows the answer, across three setups from hardest to easier. Height is how often the hidden rule is correctly identified; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: In the leftmost hardest setup the fair player's bar is tiny next to the cheat's, but in the other two setups the two bars stand almost level, showing more clues or questions closes the gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: More questions steadily lift the fair player toward the ceiling
charts.chart_read: Two lines track identification accuracy as the number of allowed questions rises left to right, holding starting clues fixed. One line is the fair player, the other the cheating player; higher lines are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fair player's line climbs steadily from bottom to top while the cheat's line flattens early, meaning extra questions keep helping the fair player long after the cheat has maxed out.

charts.chart_read: Two lines show identification accuracy as the number of clues shown up front increases left to right, with the number of questions held low. One line is the fair player, the other the cheating player; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fair player's line rises steeply and converges toward the cheat's line, showing that each batch of extra clues shrinks the gap between them from wide to nearly nothing.

qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.verdict_tag: More worked examples keep lifting the ceiling
qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.concept_primer: Think of teaching by worked examples: the model couldn't solve a certain three-step chained puzzle on its own, so we trained it on solved cases that a separate search program found, then tested it on brand-new puzzles.
qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.plain_question: When a small model fails a hard three-step puzzle, is that a permanent limit, or does feeding it more worked examples keep helping?
qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.plain_answer: It keeps helping. Solving these new puzzles was a data problem, not a hard ceiling: as we added more worked examples, the share of new puzzles solved rose steadily with no sign of leveling off, and the skill became usable in a single try.
qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.why_it_matters: When a small model stalls on hard multi-step tasks, don't assume it can't. Feed it more verified worked examples from a search tool and the skill grows, eventually working in one shot.

charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by how many worked examples the model trained on, from none up to the most. Within each group, colored bars show different solving styles; taller means more new puzzles solved. Right and taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bars climb steadily from left to right with no flattening, and the one-try 'no scratch-work' bars appear only at the largest training amount.

charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many attempts the model gets per puzzle; the vertical axis is the share of new puzzles solved. Each line is a training amount. Higher and to the right is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained line lies flat on zero, while each higher training amount fans out above it, the top line reaching over a third of new puzzles solved.

qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.verdict_tag: More varied practice keeps paying off
qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.concept_primer: Picture teaching the model to solve multi-step puzzles by having it practice on worked examples that a simple search program discovered on its own. More examples, and more varied ones, means more practice.
qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.plain_question: Does feeding the model ever more varied self-found practice examples keep improving it, or does it soon hit a ceiling?
qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.plain_answer: Yes, it kept improving. Doubling the variety of practice examples lifted the solve rate from 38% to 54% with no ceiling in sight, and controls show the boost came from more variety, not just more repetition of the same examples.
qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.why_it_matters: When self-training a small model, invest in gathering more genuinely different solved examples rather than just running more training passes over the same few. Variety is what actually installs new skill.

charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many different problem types the model practiced on; the vertical axis is its solve rate. The upper line allows several attempts, the lower line takes the single best answer. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines keep climbing to the right with no flattening, meaning more varied practice keeps adding skill with no ceiling reached yet.

charts.chart_plain_title: The gain comes from variety, not from more repetition
charts.chart_read: Three training setups shown as bars, taller is better: a little practice, then a much larger amount repeating just 40 problem types, then that same large amount spread across 640 different types.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bar jumps only when practice covers more different problem types, showing variety, not extra repetition, drives the improvement.

charts.chart_takeaway: Adding harder examples nearly triples the several-attempts bar, but the single-answer bar stays flat, so the new skill installs only weakly.

qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.verdict_tag: It can only follow recipes, not infer them
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.concept_primer: Think of a multi-step puzzle as two separate jobs: figuring out the recipe of steps just from before-and-after examples, versus following a recipe someone hands you. This study pulls those two jobs apart.
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.plain_question: When this small AI hits a wall on multi-step puzzles, is it bad at carrying out the steps, or bad at figuring out which steps to use?
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.plain_answer: It is the figuring-out. Handed the recipe, the model carries out even four-step puzzles near-perfectly; left to infer that recipe from examples, it fails past two steps. The wall is inference, not execution, the opposite of the expected story.
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.why_it_matters: For a fixed small model, hand it the plan and let cheap external search tools find the steps. It executes given recipes almost for free but cannot reliably discover multi-step ones on its own.

key_numbers.label: Solving when told the recipe

key_numbers.label: Solving when it must infer the recipe
key_numbers.sub: drops to nothing past two steps

key_numbers.sub: one transformation, done reliably

charts.chart_takeaway: The handed-the-recipe bars stand at full height across every depth while the other two bars sit near the floor, so the failure is inferring the recipe, not running it.

charts.chart_takeaway: Every line plunges from high at one step to the floor by three steps, and the lines overlap, showing the number of steps drives failure while the operation type barely matters.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even pre-chopped steps and yes/no checks fade as puzzles lengthen
charts.chart_read: Bars group tasks by depth and operation type. One bar is solving when each step is handed separately; the other is accuracy at judging whether a stated recipe matches the examples. Higher is better; half-height means pure guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars sink toward the floor and the yes/no check drops to coin-flip at the deepest puzzles, showing the trouble is running steps in its head, not just searching.

charts.chart_plain_title: The old wall was mismeasured: search only worked on shortcut tasks
charts.chart_read: Two bars compare an external search tool's success on two kinds of nominally three-step tasks: ones that secretly collapse to fewer steps, and genuinely three-step ones. Bar height is the share solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The shortcut-task bar is full while the genuine three-step bar is tiny, revealing that earlier apparent successes rode on tasks that were shallower than labeled.

qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.concept_primer: Picture a capable student who fails a problem only because they tried one approach and stopped; ask them for several different attempts and one usually works.
qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.plain_question: When our small model fails a coding task, is it because it truly can't do it, or just because it didn't try enough different approaches?
qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.plain_answer: Mostly the latter. A handful of tries solved 70% of tasks; asking for many varied attempts lifted that to 89%, rescuing most earlier failures. The ability was there — it just needed more varied shots.
qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.why_it_matters: Before retraining a small model to fix failures, first spend effort on diverse retries — much of the apparent weakness is untapped and cheaply recoverable. Re-editing wrong answers, by contrast, added nothing here.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of tasks solved as retries grow more varied
charts.chart_read: Bars left to right go from a few tries to many varied tries; height is the share of tasks solved, and taller is better. The rightmost bars include a step that re-edits wrong answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars climb steadily from 70% to 89%, but the last two are identical — more varied sampling keeps helping while re-editing wrong answers adds nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Originally-missed tasks rescued by each retry strategy
charts.chart_read: Each bar counts how many of the 24 originally-failed tasks a strategy rescued; taller is better. The middle bars are single decoding styles, the right bars combine several.
charts.chart_takeaway: Combining three decoding styles rescues 13 tasks versus 10 for the best single style, showing different styles rescue different tasks rather than the same ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks solved versus total computing effort spent
charts.chart_read: The line moves right as more computing effort is spent and up as more tasks get solved; up-and-to-the-right is better. It traces the no-training ceiling to beat.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line rises then flattens, so extra effort keeps solving more tasks but with clearly diminishing returns at the high end.

qwen35_4b_error_localization.verdict_tag: Its confidence dips right where it slips
qwen35_4b_error_localization.concept_primer: When a model reasons in several steps and gets the answer wrong, a useful thing would be to know WHICH step went wrong -- so you can redo just that step instead of the whole chain. This tests whether the model's own confidence pinpoints the failing step.
qwen35_4b_error_localization.plain_question: When the model makes an error partway through a multi-step calculation, does its confidence reveal WHICH step went wrong?
qwen35_4b_error_localization.plain_answer: Yes. As the model works step by step, we can read how confident it is in each step from its output probabilities. When it makes a slip, its confidence dips -- and the dip lands exactly on the step where it first went wrong (high just before, lowest right at the error, recovering after). We had to be careful: the model's confidence naturally rises over the course of a chain, so we removed that trend first; the pinpointing survives. When the model slips exactly once, reading its confidence finds the bad step about 56% of the time -- roughly triple random guessing and well above guessing from step-position alone -- so you can redo just from there instead of the whole chain (cheaper). The one limit: if the model slips several times in a chain, its confidence flags a bad step (76% of the time) but not always the FIRST one.
qwen35_4b_error_localization.why_it_matters: It extends the earlier finding (the model knows when it's wrong, in its output probabilities) from single answers to multi-step reasoning, and makes it actionable: read the per-step confidence to find and re-try just the failing step -- targeted repair instead of redoing everything. Combined with the confidence-select result, it suggests a cheap self-repair loop for a small model, with no verifier and no execution.

qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.verdict_tag: Runnable-fix training transfers, but needs worked steps
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.concept_primer: Instead of describing a fix in words, the model is trained to rewrite the broken formula as a runnable mini-program the computer then checks by actually running it on real cases.
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.plain_question: Can the model learn to fix broken mini-programs by rewriting them, and still succeed on kinds of problems it never trained on?
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.plain_answer: Mostly. After training it fully solved unseen problem types about three-quarters of the time, versus a third with no worked-through steps in the prompt, but only when those matching steps were present, and one problem type stayed stubbornly hard.
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.why_it_matters: Training a small model to emit runnable fixes can carry over to new problem types, but only if the prompt shows matching worked-through steps. Missing or scrambled steps erase most of the gain, so keep them aligned.

qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.verdict_tag: Cheap rule knows when extra effort pays
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.concept_primer: Picture cleaning a messy spreadsheet: the model can give a quick answer, or run five slower step-by-step programs. This tests a cheap rule that decides when those slow programs are worth the extra effort.
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.plain_question: Can a simple rule tell when it is worth running the slow, expensive table-fixing programs instead of just taking the quick answer?
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.plain_answer: Yes. A one-line rule that runs the slow programs only when the quick answer drops columns reached the best accuracy any program could, 58% correct versus 42% for the quick answer alone, at a fraction of the always-run cost.
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.why_it_matters: You do not need a trained judge to decide when to spend expensive compute. A simple check on the shape of the quick answer routes the effort to the few cases that need it and skips the rest.

charts.chart_takeaway: The cheap-rule bar ties the best-possible bar and clears the quick-answer bar, so the simple rule captures every available gain.

charts.chart_read: The rising line shows accuracy as you force more slow programs to run on every table; the flat line is the cheap rule that runs them only when needed. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rising line levels off just below the flat rule line, so no amount of always-on effort reaches what the selective rule gets.

charts.chart_read: Paired bars for each table type show the quick answer versus the cheap rule; taller is better. Most pairs match, meaning the rule leaves them unchanged.
charts.chart_takeaway: Two table types jump from zero to most-correct with the rule while every other type is untouched, so gains are concentrated and nothing is broken.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trying one program first barely helps
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per program strategy compare running that one strategy outright versus first testing it and escalating only on disagreement. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two bars are nearly identical for every strategy and all sit below the cheap rule's level, so the escalate-later trick adds almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Requiring programs to agree lowers accuracy
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars at each effort budget compare committing to the first workable program versus demanding two or three programs agree first. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The agreement bars sit below the first-commit bars at every budget, so insisting programs match each other consistently costs accuracy.

charts.chart_takeaway: The tightest trigger's bar is a small fraction of the always-run bar despite matching its accuracy, so the strict rule buys the same result cheaply.

qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.verdict_tag: Asking directly beats the specialized tool
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.concept_primer: Imagine reshaping messy spreadsheet tables into a clean format. You can use a pre-built converter that only knows a fixed set of moves, or just ask the AI to write out the finished table itself.
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.plain_question: Does the pre-built table converter beat simply asking the model to write out the reshaped table itself?
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.plain_answer: No. Asking the model directly got 55% of tables exactly right, far ahead of the pre-built converter's 18%. The converter added only a tiny extra slice of cases the model missed on its own.
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.why_it_matters: For reshaping messy tables, reach for the model directly rather than building a specialized converter; keep the tool only as a small backup for the few cases the model misses.

qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.verdict_tag: Writing a throwaway program did not help
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.concept_primer: Instead of reshaping a messy spreadsheet by hand, the model can write a tiny throwaway script that does the reshaping, test it on one worked example, then run it on the real data.
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.plain_question: Does having the model write and test a small reshaping script beat just having it reshape the spreadsheet directly?
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.plain_answer: No. Answering directly got about 55% of tables exactly right, while the script route managed only 15%. Even combining both perfectly would reach just 59%, barely above answering directly alone.
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.why_it_matters: On this kind of table-reshaping task, let the model answer directly rather than routing through a self-written script. Passing one worked example is a weak safety check and lets wrong scripts through.

qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.verdict_tag: Fixed toolkit too narrow for outside tasks
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.concept_primer: Think of a fixed toolkit of table-reshaping moves, like a small recipe book, that tries to rebuild each messy spreadsheet into the desired clean layout using only the recipes it already knows.
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.plain_question: Can a fixed toolkit of table-reshaping moves handle a broad outside collection of real spreadsheet cleanup tasks?
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.plain_answer: No. The fixed toolkit rebuilt only 18% of the outside transformation tasks, failing 82% because it simply lacks the needed moves, not because it chose the wrong recipe or saw too few examples.
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.why_it_matters: Before investing in smarter recipe-picking, first widen the toolkit itself. Coverage was all-or-nothing by task type, so the gap is missing building blocks, not better selection.

charts.chart_read: Each line tracks the share of tasks solved as the number of shown example pairs rises from one to five. Higher is better. Two lines stay flat; the top line only falls to meet them.

qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.verdict_tag: Making programs agree beats no simpler pick
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.concept_primer: Instead of answering directly, the model writes small programs that reshape a messy table, and each program can be checked against one worked example before you trust its answer.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.plain_question: When the model writes several table-reshaping programs, does requiring them to agree beat just using the first one that matches the worked example?
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.plain_answer: No. Requiring several programs to agree was safer on each pick but far too cautious; simply using the first program that matched the worked example solved the most tables, 52% versus 44% for asking the model directly.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.why_it_matters: Prefer the simplest reliable filter — trust the first program that reproduces the worked example. Demanding agreement across programs raises confidence per answer but skips too many solvable tables to be worth it.

key_numbers.sub: share of tables solved by asking the model for the answer

key_numbers.label: First matching program
key_numbers.sub: use the first program that reproduces the worked example

key_numbers.label: Requiring agreement
key_numbers.sub: make at least two programs agree first — fewer tables solved

key_numbers.label: Best any program could do
key_numbers.sub: ceiling if you always picked a correct program when one existed

charts.chart_plain_title: How many tables each answering strategy solves
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a way of picking the final answer, from asking directly, through the two agreement rules, to using the first program that matches the example, ending at a best-possible ceiling; the height is the share of tables solved exactly, and taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first-matching-program bar is the tallest usable strategy, standing above both agreement bars and above asking directly.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tables newly fixed versus newly broken by each strategy
charts.chart_read: For each picking strategy, one colored bar counts tables it newly gets right compared with asking directly, and the other counts tables it newly gets wrong; more fixes and fewer breaks is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first-matching-program strategy fixes 23 tables while breaking only 4, a far bigger net gain than either agreement rule.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often a committed program is actually correct
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a picking strategy, and the height shows how often the program it commits to turns out to be right; taller means more trustworthy picks.
charts.chart_takeaway: Requiring stricter agreement lifts per-pick correctness to 84% from 73%, but that small edge does not make up for the many tables it skips.

charts.chart_plain_title: Programs that match the example, before and after one fix
charts.chart_read: Each group is one of three prompt styles for writing the program, with one bar for programs that matched the example on the first try and another for extra matches gained after a single fix attempt; more matches is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: One fix round nearly doubles the matches for the middle prompt style while barely helping the other two.

qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.verdict_tag: Repair adds coverage but not a replacement
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.concept_primer: Instead of typing the answer by hand, the model writes a small runnable recipe that reshapes the table, tries it on a solved worked example, and fixes wherever the result differs.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.plain_question: Does letting the model write a small conversion recipe, test it on a worked example, and fix its mistakes beat just asking for the answer directly?
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.plain_answer: It depends. On its own the recipe path solved far fewer tables than answering directly (25% vs 55%), but fixing the recipe against the worked example and then keeping whichever path won caught 18 extra tables, reaching 62%.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.why_it_matters: Treat the recipe path as a complementary second opinion, not a replacement for direct answering, and never auto-trust a recipe just because it matches the worked example.

key_numbers.label: Recipe correctness after fixing
key_numbers.sub: share of tables the recipe got right on real data, up after fixing rounds

key_numbers.label: Tables solved when both paths combine
key_numbers.sub: extra tables caught by adding the recipe path to direct answering

key_numbers.label: Recipes that look right but aren't
key_numbers.sub: passed the worked example yet still wrong on the real data table

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy of each approach on the real data
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one strategy; height is the share of tables transformed exactly right on the unseen real data. The left bars are single strategies and the rightmost combines both paths; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe-only bars sit well below direct answering, yet the combined bar is the tallest, showing the two approaches solve different tables.

charts.chart_plain_title: How each fixing round pays off
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the fixing round; the lines count tables. One line is tables still being fixed, one is tables now matching the worked example, and one is tables that also match the real data. Bigger gains per round are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-on-real-data line jumps by 40 tables in the first round then adds only a handful more, so fixing helps most early and fades fast.

charts.chart_plain_title: How trustworthy is passing the worked example?
charts.chart_read: Bars count tables among recipes that passed the worked example: the total that passed, how many were truly right on real data, and how many were secretly wrong. A small wrong bar is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Of 78 recipes that passed the example, 16 were still wrong on real data, so passing the example does not guarantee a correct answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Warning the model not to copy the example's answer
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare two instructions across three measures; one color is the plain instruction and the other explicitly forbids copying the example's output. For the two correctness measures higher is better, and for the misleading-pass measure lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The stricter instruction doubled recipe correctness and nearly halved the misleading passes, so telling the model not to memorize the example helped.

qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.verdict_tag: Executable recipes help but cost too much
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.concept_primer: Instead of asking the model to write out the fixed table directly, you can have it write a small step-by-step recipe, run that recipe, and keep only recipes that reproduce the example you were shown.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.plain_question: Does having the model write and test small table-fixing recipes beat just asking it for the finished table directly, on new kinds of tables?
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.plain_answer: It depends. Writing and testing recipes lifted exactly-correct tables from 42% to 48% with the chosen rule, and up to 56% with a simpler keep-first-working rule, but at roughly twelve times the computing cost.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.why_it_matters: Spend recipe-writing effort only where it pays off: a few well-structured table types gain a lot while others just burn compute for nothing. Route effort adaptively and prefer keeping the first recipe that works.

charts.chart_read: Each bar is one way to produce the table, from asking directly on the left to the best possible pick on the right; bar height is the share of 50 unseen tables made exactly right. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The direct bar is lowest and the best-possible bar highest; the chosen recipe rule sits only slightly above direct, while a simple keep-first-working rule nearly reaches the best possible.

charts.chart_plain_title: How each recipe-keeping rule trades fixes against mistakes
charts.chart_read: Three rules for deciding when to keep a recipe are compared. Colored bars per rule show recipes kept, previous misses newly fixed, and previously-correct tables accidentally broken. Many fixes with few breaks is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The keep-first-working rule commits the most recipes and fixes eight misses while breaking just one, showing the cautious rules leave easy fixes unused.

charts.chart_read: Two bars show total computing used on the same 50 tables: asking directly on the left, the five-recipe approach on the right. Shorter is better because it is cheaper.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe bar towers about twelve times higher than the direct bar, so the accuracy gain comes at a steep, not-yet-efficient cost.

qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.verdict_tag: Simple check helped, stress-test did not
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.concept_primer: For each table task the model can either write the answer straight out, or write a tiny program to build it; you can then run that program on the shown examples to see if it reproduces them.
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.plain_question: Does falling back to a table-transforming program beat answering directly, and does stress-testing that program make the choice safer?
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.plain_answer: It depends. Trusting the program whenever it reproduced the shown examples raised accuracy from 55% to 62% with zero cases broken, but the extra stress-test on made-up inputs helped nothing and slightly hurt.
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.why_it_matters: A cheap correctness gate, does the program match the examples you were shown, can be a reliable trust signal. Adding elaborate self-consistency stress-tests can cost effort and quietly reject good answers.

charts.chart_takeaway: The rightmost bar, trusting any program that reproduces the shown examples, is tallest, beating direct answers and every stress-test-gated rule.

qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.verdict_tag: Discovered recipes unlock no new tables
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.concept_primer: Imagine asking the model to write down a few general recipes for reshaping messy tables, learned from practice examples, then checking whether those recipes help it fix brand-new tables.
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.plain_question: Can the model discover reusable table-reshaping recipes that let it solve new tables it otherwise could not?
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.plain_answer: No. The discovered recipes nudged accuracy from 42% to 46%, but they solved zero new tables beyond a ready-made stockpile of solutions, which on its own reached 58%.
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.why_it_matters: Letting the model invent general strategies sounds appealing, but here it only reshuffled what it could already do. Prefer curating a proven library of solutions over auto-discovered recipes.

qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.verdict_tag: Broad self-practice moved the held-out benchmark
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.concept_primer: We built a private training playground of twelve invented task worlds — tracking caravan cargo, probing hidden glyph machines, fixing tiny programs, navigating burrows — each with an automatic grader. The model practiced on its own verified successes from ten of the worlds, and we tested it on the two worlds it never practiced plus a completely separate held-out benchmark it has never seen any content from.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.plain_question: Does practicing many different agent-style tasks at once teach the model something general, or does each skill stay stuck to the task it was learned on, as every earlier experiment found?
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.plain_answer: It generalizes — once the training signal points at the right spot. Practicing on its own correct answers verbatim changed nothing. The winning tweak taught the model to finish thinking and commit to a short final answer, even when its reasoning gets cut off mid-thought. Playground scores nearly quadrupled, the two never-practiced worlds improved just as much, and the held-out benchmark jumped from about 0.14 to 0.36-0.45 on two fresh test sets — roughly seven times the success bar we pre-registered. A separate hard-won lesson: our fast evaluation engine had been silently ignoring the trained weights, so two whole rounds initially looked like failures when the measurement itself was broken.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.why_it_matters: This is the first time any training method in this project has been shown to improve the blackbox benchmark at all, and it breaks the running rule that installed skills never transfer. It also establishes the working recipe — practice broadly, grade strictly, and concentrate learning on the moment of commitment — plus a permanent warning to verify that trained weights are actually loaded before trusting any evaluation.

qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.verdict_tag: Self-checking works, but only when it thinks
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.concept_primer: Think of a student who writes a coding answer, then proofreads their own attempts to spot which one is actually right—without running the code. This is the model grading its own work.
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.plain_question: Can the model reliably tell which of its own coding answers are right, just by reading them rather than running them?
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.plain_answer: Yes, once it reasons first. Given time to think, the model grades its own answers correctly about 83% of the time and picks its best of eight tries at 86%—near the 89% best-possible—versus mostly rubber-stamping everything without thinking.
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.why_it_matters: Rather than training a separate judge, let the model reason before grading its own drafts. It becomes a strong self-critic for free, cheaply lifting answer quality by choosing its best attempt.

charts.chart_read: Each bar shows how often the chosen answer is correct; taller is better. Left is a random pick of eight tries, then the model's own check without and with thinking, then the unbeatable best-possible pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: The thinking-check bar sits just below the best-possible bar and well above random, so reasoned self-checking recovers most of the available headroom.

charts.chart_plain_title: Thinking turns the model into a real critic of its own work
charts.chart_read: Paired bars show how well the model separates its right answers from its wrong ones on two scoring measures; higher is better. Light bars are without thinking, dark bars with thinking.
charts.chart_takeaway: The dark thinking bars tower over the light ones on both measures, meaning reasoning first sharply improves telling right from wrong.

charts.chart_plain_title: Without thinking, the model rubber-stamps nearly everything as correct

charts.chart_takeaway: The no-think bar sits far above the true-correct level while the thinking bar drops close to it, so thinking curbs the habit of approving everything.

qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.verdict_tag: Self-patching diagrams lose to plain one-liners
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.concept_primer: The model can write its solution two ways: as a compact one-line formula, or as a numbered wiring diagram whose steps you can check against failing examples and then patch.
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.plain_question: Does writing the program as a checkable step-by-step diagram and then letting the model patch its own mistakes solve more unseen tasks than a plain one-liner?
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.plain_answer: No. The plain one-liner solved the most unseen tasks, about 29%. The step-by-step diagram plus a self-patch step reached only about 24%, so the extra machinery did not pay off.
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.why_it_matters: A more elaborate, self-correcting format is not automatically better; here the simpler direct format won. Save the patch step for cases whose flaws resemble the ones it was actually trained to fix.

charts.chart_takeaway: Wins and losses flip by task type: the diagram aces one family the one-liner completely fails, but drops to zero on two families the one-liner fully solves.

qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.verdict_tag: Smart stopping did not improve accuracy
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.concept_primer: Picture a quiz-solver that drafts several candidate answers, then runs sample inputs through them and keeps the answer shared by the biggest group; the model's only job is to say "commit now" or "run one more sample input."
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.plain_question: Can the model boost accuracy by deciding when to stop running sample inputs versus running one more?
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.plain_answer: No. Every stopping strategy landed at the same 17% correct-answer rate, and letting the model spend more sample inputs never moved the pick onto a right answer, even though a correct answer was available for every single task.
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.why_it_matters: Deciding when to stop testing is a dead end when the sample inputs cannot tell right answers from wrong ones. Effort belongs in a better way to pick the winning answer, not in tuning when to quit.

charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-answer bar sits at the same low height for every strategy while availability stays at full height, meaning the pick almost never lands on the right answer that was there all along.

qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.verdict_tag: The HumanEval rerun confirms the judge-token signal
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.concept_primer: The MBPP code experiment found that the model can score its own code if we ask a yes/no correctness question and read the probability of the yes answer. This rerun checks the same idea on HumanEval, with no public tests available.
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.plain_question: When there are no tests to run, can the model still pick its best HumanEval code attempt by judging its own code?
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.plain_answer: Yes. Across all 164 HumanEval tasks, the model picked better code when it used the one-token 'this is correct' score than when it picked a random sample or trusted the average probability of the whole code completion. The best possible pick among the 8 samples was 0.87; the self-judge reached 0.84, close to that ceiling, without running tests.
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.why_it_matters: This separates the deployment rule from the benchmark: when tests exist, run them. When tests are missing, use the model's own one-token correctness score to choose among samples or decide whether to route the problem elsewhere.

charts.chart_takeaway: The one-token self-judge score beats the other no-test selectors and lands close to the best possible pick among the 8 attempts.

qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.verdict_tag: Teaching the model how to search does not break the depth wall
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.concept_primer: Two earlier findings pointed in opposite directions. One: the model cannot invent the multi-step structure of an unfamiliar transformation (its oldest, most replicated failure). Two: the model CAN be taught a general 'guess, check against the examples, revise' routine that works on a held-out puzzle family. This experiment forces the two findings to fight: teach the routine on the exact substrates where the structure failure lives, and see if the failure moves.
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.plain_question: If we teach the model a systematic guess-and-check search routine, can it finally figure out three-step transformations it has never been able to crack?
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.plain_answer: No -- and the way it fails is the insight. The model learned the routine perfectly: on two-step transformations (the depth it practiced on), its success at proposing the right structure DOUBLED (0.37 to 0.70 on lists), with flawless formatting and no loss of old skills. But on three-step transformations -- one step deeper than its practice -- it gained nothing at all. The routine also does not travel: the earlier experiment's trained adapter, re-verified as working on its own puzzles, scored near zero here and even hurt performance. Teaching HOW to search only helps at the depth where the search was practiced.
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.why_it_matters: It pins down what the model's hardest limit actually is: not a missing method, but depth itself. Answers, data variety, and now explicit search procedures all fail to carry the model one composition step past its training. Practical rule: use procedure-training to consolidate depths the model can already touch, and use external brute-force search for anything deeper.

key_numbers.label: two-step structure proposal after procedure training (lists)
three-step (one deeper than practice), all three arms vs base
prior adapter transferred to new substrate

qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.verdict_tag: It recognizes rules, it doesn't invent them
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.concept_primer: In-context learning is the headline trick of modern AI: show a model a few examples and it 'figures out' the pattern. But is it truly inferring a new rule, or just recognizing a rule it already knows? This experiment separates the two.
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.plain_question: When the model 'learns' a rule from a few examples, is it genuinely inferring a new rule, or just recognizing a familiar one?
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.plain_answer: Just recognizing familiar ones. Given examples of 'move forward k steps' in the normal 0-9 number order, the model figures out the rule about half the time. Given the SAME kind of rule but in a scrambled, unfamiliar order, it drops to chance -- it cannot infer it from any number of examples. Yet when you simply TELL it that scrambled rule instead of making it guess, it applies it almost perfectly (97%). So the model can run an unfamiliar rule flawlessly; it just can't DISCOVER one from examples. Its 'in-context learning' is recognizing structure it already knows, not inventing new structure.
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.why_it_matters: This explains the whole research arc in one line: the model is an executor and retriever of familiar structure, not an inventor of new structure. It reframes what 'in-context learning' buys you -- point the model at a pattern it already knows (it will recognize and apply it), but don't expect it to reverse-engineer a genuinely novel pattern from examples, no matter how many you provide. More examples don't help; they slightly hurt.
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.why_it_matters_short: ICL recognizes familiar rules; it can't invent new ones.

qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.verdict_tag: Fixed toolkit misses most new tasks
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.concept_primer: Think of a pre-stocked toolbox of generic coding building blocks, assembled and locked before any test. A task counts as covered if some single tool from the box already solves it.
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.plain_question: Can one fixed, general-purpose toolbox of coding building blocks solve a useful share of brand-new programming tasks?
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.plain_answer: No. The locked toolbox solved only about 14% of brand-new tasks, down from 37% on the familiar tasks it was shaped around, too few to justify building anything on top of it.
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.why_it_matters: A generic prebuilt toolbox does not stretch to unseen problems. Reusable coverage has to be proven on fresh tasks before investing in tooling; a passing sample check is not proof.

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved from the toolbox
key_numbers.sub: familiar tasks vs brand-new tasks

key_numbers.label: Solved across three fresh task samples
key_numbers.sub: steady low level, not a fluke

key_numbers.label: Sample-passing matches that still fail

charts.chart_takeaway: The brand-new bar falls to less than half the familiar bar, showing the toolbox does not carry over to unseen problems.

charts.chart_read: Each bar is one random sample of brand-new tasks; height is the share the fixed toolbox solves. Taller is better.

qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.verdict_tag: Independent agreement never produced usable picks
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.concept_primer: Imagine asking several people who worked out an answer separately, then trusting only answers they all independently agree on, so you can pick a correct solution without having the answer key.
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.plain_question: Can several independently derived solutions, by agreeing with each other, tell us which answer is correct when we have no answer key?
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.plain_answer: No. Requiring independent solutions to agree committed an answer on zero of 24 hard problems, because the independent versions rarely converged; a looser close-match pool committed sometimes but mostly on wrong answers.
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.why_it_matters: Forcing variety among your reference solutions can backfire, making them less relevant and less likely to agree. Genuine independent agreement needs a richer, more relevant source library, not just diversification.

key_numbers.label: Committed answers, requiring independent agreement
key_numbers.sub: Independent solvers never agreed enough to pick anything

key_numbers.label: Problems the diversified pool could solve
key_numbers.sub: Diversifying lowered it below the close-match pool

key_numbers.label: Answers that looked right but were wrong (diversified pool)
key_numbers.sub: Most passing-looking candidates were actually incorrect

key_numbers.label: Best a perfect picker could reach
key_numbers.sub: Even the ideal pick tops out here

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each picking method commits, and whether it was right
charts.chart_read: Bars count how many of 24 hard problems each picking method committed an answer on; green is answers that were actually right, red actually wrong. Taller green and shorter red is better. The far-right bar shows the best any picker could do.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two independent-agreement bars sit flat at zero, so requiring independent solvers to agree never produced a single committed answer, right or wrong.

charts.chart_plain_title: Diversifying the source pool lowered its quality
charts.chart_read: For two ways of pulling reference solutions, one bar shows the share of problems the pool could actually solve (higher is better) and the other the share of answers that looked right but were wrong (lower is better). Compare the diversified pool against the close-match pool.
charts.chart_takeaway: The diversified independent pool has the lower solve share and the taller wrong-answer bar, so forcing variety made the source material worse, not better.

qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.verdict_tag: Training did not build a usable shortlist
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.concept_primer: Imagine building a small gadget that needs two parts chosen from a 512-item parts catalog: the model must shortlist a handful of likely parts for each slot so the correct pair is among them.
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.plain_question: Can extra training teach the model to shortlist the right parts from a 512-item catalog so the correct pair is included?
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.plain_answer: No. After training, the model's shortlist still contained the correct two-part combination 0% of the time, exactly matching the untrained and scrambled-catalog versions, so the extra teaching added nothing here.
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.why_it_matters: When a model cannot surface the right choice from a large catalog, adding more same-format training will not rescue it; invest instead in smarter search or better-designed test questions, which measurably narrowed the field.

key_numbers.label: Right pair found, before vs after training
key_numbers.sub: the shortlist never included the correct two-part combination

key_numbers.label: Options left after six test questions
key_numbers.sub: designed questions narrow the field far more than random ones

charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar sits flat at zero, so training left the model no better than the untrained or scrambled versions at surfacing the right pair.

charts.chart_takeaway: The designed-question bar is clearly taller, meaning well-chosen questions lead to the correct program more often, though it still fails most of the time.

charts.chart_takeaway: The line drops sharply and then flattens around a low level, confirming the model did learn the format, so the zero score is not a fitting failure.

qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.verdict_tag: Model can't read a reshuffled codebook
qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.concept_primer: Each question ships with a codebook that pairs every tool with a nickname, and the nicknames are reshuffled each time, so the model must actually read it. Its job: name two tools by their current nicknames.
qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.plain_question: Can the model name two tools correctly by looking each one up in a codebook that gets reshuffled for every question?
qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.plain_answer: No. The model never once produced the correct two-tool answer, in any version of the test. Training nudged single-tool guesses up slightly, but reshuffling the codebook barely changed that, so it wasn't truly reading the lookups.
qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.why_it_matters: Handing a model a large, reshuffled lookup table and asking it to name several items at once is too hard an interface; give it a short list of pre-filtered candidates to judge instead.

key_numbers.label: Both tools named right
key_numbers.sub: never once, in any version of the test

key_numbers.label: One tool named right, untrained then trained
key_numbers.sub: training nudged it up, but reshuffling the codebook kept the same score, so not real reading

key_numbers.label: Training error, start to end
key_numbers.sub: the model learned the answer format but not how to use the codebook

charts.chart_takeaway: The both-tool bar is flat at zero in every cluster, and the single-tool bars barely rise and don't fall when the codebook is scrambled, so the small lift isn't genuine reading.

charts.chart_plain_title: Designed clues narrow the possibilities, but the model can't cash it in
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many tools are in the codebook; the vertical axis is how many answers still fit the clues shown, so fewer means less guessing. Lines from designed clues sit below their random-clue counterparts. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Designed-clue lines run below random-clue lines at every codebook size, yet that smaller pool of possibilities never turned into a single correct model answer.

charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training progress; the vertical axis is the model's error while learning, where lower means a better fit to the training answers. The single line falls steeply at the start, then wanders in a middling band. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line plateaus in a mid-range band rather than near zero, matching a model that learned the answer format but not how to read the codebook.

qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.verdict_tag: It reasons but doesn't figure out rules
qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.concept_primer: There are two skills in multi-step reasoning: FOLLOWING a rule you're given, and FIGURING OUT a hidden rule from examples. A companion experiment showed this small model follows rules in plain English almost perfectly. This one tests the second skill: can it infer a hidden rule from examples?
qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.plain_question: In plain language, can the model FIGURE OUT a hidden multi-step rule from examples (not just follow one it's told)?
qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.plain_answer: Barely. Told the rule explicitly, it applies it correctly ~86% of the time. But asked to infer the same kind of hidden rule from worked examples, it is at chance (0%) in a single pass -- it cannot figure out even a one-step rule. Letting it think out loud only gets it to ~50% on the easiest case, and its reasoning is on the right track but error-prone. So the model can EXECUTE a rule it's handed but cannot reliably DISCOVER one -- in plain language just as in code.
qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.why_it_matters: Together with the companion result, this pins down exactly what the model can and can't do. Following steps (execution) is easy and gets easier in natural language. Discovering the rule (induction) is hard everywhere -- language doesn't rescue it. For practitioners: give the model the procedure to run; don't expect it to reverse-engineer the procedure from examples. This is the one limitation that holds even in the model's native domain.

key_numbers.label: INFER a hidden rule (1 step, single pass)
INFER a hidden rule (1 step, thinking)
following a rule in language (companion, C37)

qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.verdict_tag: The reasoning wall is formal, not linguistic
qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.concept_primer: The model fails at chaining 3+ steps of a hand-built symbolic operation (a 'compositional wall' this project mapped in depth). But it is a language model. This asks whether the same 3-step wall appears when the identical chaining task is posed in plain English instead of formal code.
qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.plain_question: Does the model's 3-step 'compositional wall' also appear when the same multi-step chain is presented as natural-language reasoning instead of formal code?
qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.plain_answer: No. Asked to follow a chain of made-up facts ('Kel is directly followed by Vor') three steps in plain English, the model is right ~99% of the time -- and still ~100% with a totally made-up relation word, so it is the language framing, not a memorized meaning, that carries it. That is the exact depth where formal composition collapses. So the 'wall' is a property of the formal/code modality, not of the model's ability to reason step-by-step. A striking side effect: handing the model the same facts as a Python dictionary makes it dump the dictionary back as code instead of reasoning -- the surface form decides whether it reasons or codes.
qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.why_it_matters: The whole project spent 36 findings proving this small model is bad at deep composition -- but that was measured only on formal symbolic tasks. In its native domain, plain language, the same model chains 3-4 reasoning steps almost perfectly. For practitioners: present a multi-step task in natural language, not as code/data structures, to elicit the model's reasoning rather than trigger a code-dump. And it relocates the research question -- the barrier is formal-symbol manipulation, not step-count.

qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.verdict_tag: Hidden answer only for simple problems
qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.concept_primer: Like a word on the tip of your tongue, we peeked at the model's internal activity to check whether it already knows an answer even when it can't say it out loud.
qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.plain_question: When the model can't solve a multi-step problem, does it secretly already know the answer inside its head?
qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.plain_answer: It depends on difficulty. On simple problems the answer is clearly present inside even when the model won't say it; on the hardest problems it's near random-guess level inside, meaning the model never actually worked it out.
qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.why_it_matters: Coaxing out hidden answers can help only on easy cases where the answer already exists inside. For truly hard problems, you must teach the model the missing skill, not just read its mind.

key_numbers.label: Says it out loud vs holds it inside, simplest
key_numbers.sub: the gap between what it knows and what it expresses

key_numbers.label: Answer readable inside, hardest problems
key_numbers.sub: barely above chance, so the model never figured it out

Height shows how often the first step is recovered: one bar reads it straight from the model's internal activity, the others show how often the model says or writes it; the lowest bar is the random-guess floor. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: For simple problems the internal-read bar towers over the spoken bars, but for the hardest problems every bar collapses to the random-guess floor.

charts.chart_takeaway: The simple-problem line climbs to near-perfect by the middle stages and stays flat, while the harder lines never lift off the floor.

qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.verdict_tag: Self-training installs the missing forward planning
qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.concept_primer: Think of solving a puzzle that needs three moves in a row. There is a difference between planning your first move toward a far-off goal and simply recognizing when you are one move from finished.
qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.plain_question: Can the model solve a three-move puzzle by planning one move at a time, and does training on its own solutions help?
qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.plain_answer: It depends. On its own the model can recognize a finish but cannot plan a first move toward a distant goal, so guiding itself does worse than random. But training it on its own solved puzzles installs genuine forward planning, lifting every step.
qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.why_it_matters: Do not expect the model to plan multi-step solutions unaided; it only recognizes near-finished states. Training it on its own verified solutions adds real planning that carries over to new roles.

The three colors are the untrained model versus two doses of self-training; a random guess lands near 3%.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained bar for the first move is almost invisible (about 1%, below random), while every trained bar rises at every distance, showing self-training adds real forward planning.

charts.chart_takeaway: At the same budget the untrained dot sits below the random line while the self-trained dot jumps far above it, showing self-training turns a harmful guide into a useful one.

qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.verdict_tag: Rewarding right-over-wrong backfires; practice more instead
qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.concept_primer: Think of a chef who can taste two dishes and pick the better one almost every time, yet that judging skill is separate from cooking a great dish from scratch on the first try.
qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.plain_question: Can the model get better at answering by learning to prefer its own correct attempts over its wrong ones?
qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.plain_answer: No. Training it to favor its correct answers over its wrong ones broke its ability to write answers at all, dropping its one-shot solve rate to zero. Simply practicing longer on the correct answers alone worked far better.
qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.why_it_matters: Being good at judging your own answers does not make you good at producing them. To lift real single-shot performance, just train longer on verified-correct examples rather than on correct-versus-wrong comparisons.

key_numbers.label: One-shot solve rate, practice longer

key_numbers.label: One-shot solve rate, reward right-over-wrong

charts.chart_takeaway: The train-twice-as-long bar is tallest on both measures, while the reward-correct-over-wrong bars shrink toward zero, meaning preference training loses to plain extra practice.

charts.chart_takeaway: The training lines rise slightly with more propping-up but stay below even plain practice, confirming the fix eases the collapse without recovering lost performance.

qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.verdict_tag: Learned test-picker helps on some sets
qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.concept_primer: After the model drafts many possible answers, it can ask one more test input to rule some out, like a game of twenty questions where a sharper question eliminates more wrong guesses at once.
qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.plain_question: Can the model learn to pick the single most useful follow-up test, the one that rules out the most wrong answers?
qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.plain_answer: It depends. With one extra test the trained picker beat a simple hand-coded rule on two of three problem sets, but not on the main set, and its picks ruled out under half as many wrong answers as the best-possible choice.
qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.why_it_matters: The model can learn to ask informative follow-up tests, but a picker trained on single best-first choices only partly beats a simple rule; a reliable gain likely needs training on its own multi-step choices.

qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.verdict_tag: Writing and running code recovers cases
qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.concept_primer: Faced with a table to reshape, the model can either answer from memory or write a small program, run it, and check the result — with a "decider" step picking which answer to submit.
qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.plain_question: Does letting the model write and run a small program, instead of only answering from memory, help it reshape tables correctly without making things worse?
qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.plain_answer: Yes. Writing and running a small program, with a decider choosing when to trust it, solved 2 of 12 new tables that answer-only got wrong, and never broke a case the model would otherwise have gotten right.
qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.why_it_matters: Adding a run-the-code check with a decider is a safe, cheap upgrade: it only recovers misses and causes no new mistakes. A trained decider reaches the same result with far less tool effort than always running code.

key_numbers.label: Tables solved: answer-only vs writing code
key_numbers.sub: cases recovered that answering from memory missed

key_numbers.label: Right answers broken by adding tool use
key_numbers.sub: no correct case was ever made wrong

key_numbers.label: Work to solve the same two tables
key_numbers.sub: always-run needs 12 programs plus 18 fixes; trained decider uses 6 programs, no fixes

charts.chart_read: Each bar is one answering strategy; height is how often it got the table exactly right on new, unseen tasks — taller is better. The far-left bar answers from memory only; the middle bars write and run a program; the scrambled-decider bar is a sanity check.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every write-and-run strategy clears the answer-only bar, which solves none, while the scrambled-decider control also drops to zero — running the code is what recovers the extra cases.

charts.chart_plain_title: Familiar practice tasks versus new tasks: the drop-off
charts.chart_read: Two bars per strategy: the lighter bar is familiar practice tasks, the darker bar is new unseen tasks; height is how often the table came out exactly right, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every strategy's new-task bar is far shorter than its practice bar — accuracy falls sharply on unfamiliar tables, and even ideal choices top out at 2 of 12.
