charts.chart_read: For strategies that all score the same on new tasks, the bars show tool effort on 12 tasks: one color is programs written, the other is fix attempts. Lower bars mean cheaper.
charts.chart_takeaway: Always-running code needs 12 programs plus 18 fixes, while the trained decider reaches the identical score with 6 programs and no fixes — a large cost cut for equal accuracy.

qwen35_4b_meta_induction.verdict_tag: General rule-finding IS teachable -- as reasoning
qwen35_4b_meta_induction.concept_primer: The model's core weakness (established across this project) is INDUCTION: figuring out a hidden rule from a few examples. This asks the biggest question -- can you TRAIN that missing skill into the fixed model so it works on rules it never saw?
qwen35_4b_meta_induction.plain_question: Can fine-tuning teach the small model the general skill of figuring out a hidden rule from examples -- lifting its central limitation?
qwen35_4b_meta_induction.plain_answer: Yes, if you teach it the right way. Training the model to answer directly barely works and wrecks its other skills. Training it to REASON through a hidden shift step by step lets it solve new shift puzzles perfectly -- but only shifts. The breakthrough: teach a GENERAL 'try each possibility and check which fits the examples' strategy across several DIFFERENT rule types, and the model then solves a rule type it was NEVER trained on just as well (91%, matching the trained types). So the model can genuinely learn general rule-finding -- but it lives entirely in its step-by-step reasoning: the SAME trained model is back at chance if forced to answer in one shot. Its central weakness is not missing knowledge, but an inability to run the computation in a single pass -- and a general reasoning strategy overcomes it generally.
qwen35_4b_meta_induction.why_it_matters: This is the most constructive result of the whole project: the model's central limitation -- it can't figure out hidden rules -- can be TRAINED AWAY, generally, by teaching a general hypothesize-and-verify strategy as chain-of-thought across diverse examples. The practical recipe: teach the general STRATEGY (not one specific rule) across varied cases, and always run it with room to reason step by step. Never fine-tune it to answer in one shot -- that forces the computation somewhere it cannot live and destroys skills it already had.

key_numbers.label: held-out rule family (never trained), via reasoning
same model forced to answer in one step (C44)

qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.verdict_tag: It knows in its logits, not its words
qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.concept_primer: A useful AI should know when it doesn't know -- so it can flag a guess instead of confidently making something up. This tests whether the model can tell, on tasks where we KNOW in advance which ones it can and can't do, whether it is about to be right or wrong.
qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.plain_question: Does the model know when it is about to fail -- and if so, can it tell you?
qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.plain_answer: It knows, but it can't tell you. When you look at the model's internal probability on the answer it gives, that number tracks its actual accuracy almost perfectly, and it predicts which specific problems it will get right about as well as a near-perfect detector -- far better than guessing from the surface of the problem. But when you simply ASK the model 'is your answer correct?' or 'how confident are you?', its answer is useless: it says it's confident about equally on the problems it aces and the ones it fails (and its verbal confidence is a flat 100 every time). So the model's uncertainty is real and readable -- but it lives in its output probabilities, not in anything it can say.
qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.why_it_matters: This is directly deployable and it unearths a genuinely useful hidden capability. If you want a small model to flag its own likely mistakes (to abstain, ask for help, or route to a bigger model), read its answer-token probability -- NOT its self-reported confidence, which is worthless. Filtering by that internal signal lifts accuracy on the problems it chooses to attempt from 23% to nearly 100%. The practical rule: trust the model's math (logits), never its self-assessment (words).

qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.verdict_tag: Fancier case-picking did not beat simple
qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.concept_primer: Think of a tutor giving a student extra practice problems to fix a weak spot. The problems can be a simple fixed set of tricky cases, or ones hand-picked to specifically trip up this student.
qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.plain_question: Does hand-picking practice cases that specifically trip up the model beat a simple fixed set of tricky cases, given the same training effort?
qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.plain_answer: No. Picking cases to specifically trip up the model did not beat a simple fixed set. Both lifted the model's weak spot from 64% to a perfect 100%, and the simple fixed set was actually a touch cleaner.
qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.why_it_matters: Spend effort on covering the tricky case types rather than on the fancier trip-it-up selection loop, at least until your test set is hard enough that the simple set stops solving everything.

key_numbers.label: Tricky-case success, before vs after adding practice
key_numbers.sub: getting the whole little program right on unseen inputs

key_numbers.label: Hardest case type, before vs after
key_numbers.sub: the one weak spot both practice sets fully repaired

key_numbers.label: Same practice with clues scrambled
key_numbers.sub: shows the model actually reads the worked-out steps, not just their presence

charts.chart_plain_title: Both practice sets fully fixed the model's weak spot
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each trained version gets the whole little program right on unseen inputs; taller is better. Three versions: no extra practice, simple fixed practice, and hand-picked trip-it-up practice. Each has a single-guess bar and a best-of-a-few-tries bar.
charts.chart_takeaway: The no-extra-practice bars sit around two-thirds while both practice versions reach the top, so extra practice, not which kind, drives the fix.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where practice helps: it repairs the one failing case type
charts.chart_read: Bars group by the three tricky case types; taller is better. Within each group, the no-extra-practice version sits beside the two practice versions, showing how often each fully solves that case type on unseen inputs.
charts.chart_takeaway: The no-practice version only clears the text-length case type a quarter of the time while both practice versions clear every type, pinpointing where the fix lands.

charts.chart_plain_title: The worked-out steps genuinely guide the answer
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often the model gets the whole program right when its prompt has correct worked-out steps, no steps, or scrambled steps; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy drops from near-perfect to about a third once the steps are scrambled, proving the model reads the steps rather than treating them as decoration.

qwen35_4b_neurosymbolic_repl_substrate.concept_primer: Picture a coder who writes a program, runs it, sees the wrong output, and edits it — instead of just rewriting from scratch a few times. This test lets the model do exactly that, using real results from running its code.
qwen35_4b_neurosymbolic_repl_substrate.plain_question: Does letting the model run its own code and fix its mistakes beat simply having it try several fresh times?
qwen35_4b_neurosymbolic_repl_substrate.plain_answer: No. Running the code and feeding back the real result barely helped (about 29% of tasks solved), while just trying five fresh independent times did better (about 34%). The extra tries were the smarter use of the same effort.
qwen35_4b_neurosymbolic_repl_substrate.why_it_matters: For a small model, don't burn effort on run-and-fix loops at answer time — plain independent retries do as well or better. Durable gains come instead from training it on the answers it can already verify are correct.

key_numbers.label: Run-and-fix loop
key_numbers.sub: tasks solved when the model runs its code and revises

key_numbers.label: Just try five fresh times
key_numbers.sub: tasks solved by independent attempts, same effort

key_numbers.label: Learning from its own wins
key_numbers.sub: single-shot accuracy after training on verified successes

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixing its own code barely beats a single try
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one strategy; bar height is the share of unseen tasks solved, and taller is better. Left to right: one try, run-and-fix using real results, run-and-fix told nothing, and five fresh independent tries.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rightmost bar (five fresh tries) is clearly tallest, while the run-and-fix bar sits barely above a single try — live feedback adds almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: The fix-it loop lands on or below the plain retry curve
charts.chart_read: The rising line shows accuracy on unseen tasks as the model makes more independent attempts; higher is better. The two separate dots are the run-and-fix loops, placed at how much effort they cost.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both fix-and-retry dots sit on or under the rising line, so the same effort spent on fresh tries does as well or better.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training on its own verified wins lifts accuracy
charts.chart_read: Two task groups (one-shot answers, and best-of-five coverage); paired bars compare the untrained model against one trained on its own verified solutions. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained bars are taller in both groups, and coverage rises rather than shrinking — a real gain with no loss of variety.

charts.chart_plain_title: Repeated self-training compounds but flattens out
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training rounds (0 = untrained); three lines track one-shot accuracy, best-of-five coverage, and the very hardest tasks on unseen problems. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The top lines climb then plateau by round three, while the hardest-task line stays flat near the bottom — the gains hit a ceiling.

qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.verdict_tag: Gentle nudge helps, but first tries suffer
qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.concept_primer: Imagine coaching someone by showing right answers next to convincing-but-wrong ones, so they learn the difference. This experiment gives the fixed model that kind of side-by-side coaching without retraining it from scratch.
qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.plain_question: Can a small coaching update help the model solve more coding problems without wrecking the quality of its very first answer?
qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.plain_answer: It depends. A gentle coaching update nudged the share of problems eventually solved from 62% up to 67%, matching what you'd get by just trying more often, at half the computing effort. But the first answer got worse, so the pre-set bar was not cleared.
qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.why_it_matters: A light-touch update can make a fixed model cheaper to squeeze answers from, but it shifts probability around rather than adding skill. Guard first-try quality explicitly, and never overtrain: heavy coaching broke the model completely.

key_numbers.label: Problems eventually solved
key_numbers.sub: gentle coaching vs the untrained model

key_numbers.label: Effort to reach that 67%
key_numbers.sub: vs solving more by simply trying more times

key_numbers.label: First answer correct
key_numbers.sub: dropped after coaching — the catch

key_numbers.label: Heavy coaching version
key_numbers.sub: overtraining broke every answer

charts.chart_plain_title: Problems solved versus first-try quality across coaching recipes
charts.chart_read: Each recipe has two bars: one is the share of problems solved after a few tries, the other is how often the first answer is right. Higher is better for both. The leftmost pair is the untrained model.
charts.chart_takeaway: The gentle-coaching recipe's solved-bar tops the untrained model while its first-try bar sits lower, and the two heavy-coaching recipes flatline at zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: Computing effort to solve the same share of problems
charts.chart_read: Two bars whose height is the computing effort used. Both recipes end up solving the same share of problems; a shorter bar is better because it means less effort.
charts.chart_takeaway: The gentle-coaching bar is under half the height of the try-more-often bar, so the same problems get solved for roughly half the effort.

charts.chart_plain_title: Usable answers per problem, by coaching recipe
charts.chart_read: One bar per recipe; height is how many usable, runnable answers it produces per problem. Higher is better. Two of the bars are the heavily coached versions.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two heavily coached bars drop to zero usable answers, while every light-touch recipe stays near three to four.

qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.concept_primer: Picture filling blanks in a recipe by trying every tool in a drawer. One blank is easy; two blanks means testing every tool-pair, and many pairs cook up identical-looking results.
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.plain_question: As the tool drawer grows from a handful to hundreds, can plain trial-and-error still pick the one correct program?
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.plain_answer: It depends. For one-blank programs, trying every tool still lands the right answer nearly every time. For two-blank programs it stalls: at 512 tools the correct pick lands under half the time, because too many tool-pairs look identical on the examples given.
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.why_it_matters: When you must fill two slots from a large drawer, brute force stops being enough. You need a smart shortlister to narrow candidates first, then a few clarifying checks to break remaining ties.

key_numbers.label: Two-blank programs picked correctly
key_numbers.sub: drops as the drawer grows from 8 to 512 tools

key_numbers.label: One-blank programs picked correctly
key_numbers.sub: stays reliable at every drawer size

key_numbers.label: Tool-pairs to test at 512 tools
key_numbers.sub: combinations checked per two-blank problem

key_numbers.label: Short list still holds the answer
key_numbers.sub: a small fixed candidate list stops containing the right pick

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct picks as the tool drawer grows
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many tools are in the drawer; vertical axis is how often the correct program is picked with no follow-up questions; higher is better. One line is easy one-blank programs, the other harder two-blank programs.
charts.chart_takeaway: The one-blank line stays near the top while the two-blank line sags toward half, showing two-blank search gets crowded as the drawer grows.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does a short candidate list still hold the answer?
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is drawer size; vertical axis is how often the right tool-pair still sits inside a fixed short list of candidates; higher is better. Each line is a different list length, for two-blank programs.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line dives toward the floor once the drawer passes a few dozen tools, so plainly capping the list length loses the answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: A few clarifying questions recover correct picks
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many clarifying questions are allowed; vertical axis is how often the correct two-blank program is then picked; higher is better. One line is a practical question-picking rule, the other an ideal best-case rule.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines climb steeply from under half toward two-thirds or more, showing a handful of questions breaks most remaining ties.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which two-blank recipe is the hard one
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by drawer size (128 and 512 tools); height is how often the correct program is picked with no questions; taller is better. One color is the arithmetic recipe, the other the comparison-gate recipe.
charts.chart_takeaway: The comparison-gate bars are far shorter, showing that low-information recipe is what drags overall correct picks down.

qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.concept_primer: Many operations take a list of numbers and return one number (add them, take the biggest, multiply them). They look identical from the outside, so you can only tell them apart by the answers they produce on example inputs.
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.plain_question: Can the model recover a hidden number-crunching operation by searching a toolbox, even when several operations look identical from the outside?
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.plain_answer: Yes, as long as the operation is in the toolbox. A limited three-tool set never found the missing operations, while a complete seven-tool set identified the right one on 93% of new tasks straight away, rising to every task after one or two follow-up checks.
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.why_it_matters: The bottleneck is toolbox coverage, not the search itself. Make sure candidate operations are stocked and add a couple of clarifying checks when several fit; searching harder on a missing tool cannot help.

key_numbers.label: New tasks solved, limited toolbox
key_numbers.sub: three-tool set never contains the answer

key_numbers.label: New tasks solved, complete toolbox
key_numbers.sub: seven-tool set, no follow-up checks

key_numbers.label: After one or two follow-up checks
key_numbers.sub: complete toolbox, every new task solved

key_numbers.label: Candidates weighed per task
key_numbers.sub: search stayed cheap

charts.chart_plain_title: Complete toolbox recovers operations a small toolbox never can
charts.chart_read: Bars compare two toolboxes on new tasks: a limited three-tool set versus a complete seven-tool set. One bar per group shows whether the right operation was even available, the other whether it was actually picked; taller means more tasks solved, so higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The limited-toolbox bars sit flat at zero while the complete toolbox reaches near-full height, showing the answer must be stocked in the toolbox to be found.

charts.chart_plain_title: A couple of follow-up checks solve every remaining new task
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many extra clarifying checks are run; the vertical axis is the share of new tasks solved, where higher is better. Two lines are strategies using the complete toolbox; the flat bottom line is the limited toolbox.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both complete-toolbox lines climb from about 93% to 100% within one or two checks, while the limited-toolbox line stays flat at zero no matter how many checks are run.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most operations are picked correctly nearly every time
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one operation; taller means a larger share of its example tasks were identified correctly, so higher is better. The first four are new operations and the last three are familiar ones.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most bars sit near the top, but gcd is lowest at about 77%, meaning it overlaps most with other operations and most often needs a follow-up check.

qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.verdict_tag: Hint fails to localize the correctness signal
qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.concept_primer: Picture two solutions that read the same until one fork, where a correct choice and a convincing wrong choice split apart; the question is whether handing a grader a retrieved recipe helps it pick the right side there.
qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.plain_question: Does giving the grader a retrieved recipe help it pick the correct side at the fork where good and bad code split?
qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.plain_answer: No. The recipe added essentially no help at those forks, no better than a scrambled, meaningless hint. It only boosted the grader when its own wording happened to overlap the code's surface text.
qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.why_it_matters: Before training a model to imitate its own best answers using a retrieved recipe, first check that the recipe helps at the exact spots where right and wrong code diverge; otherwise training just reinforces familiar wording, not correctness.

key_numbers.label: Extra help at the real decision points
key_numbers.sub: basically no change from giving no hint

key_numbers.label: Extra help from a scrambled fake hint
key_numbers.sub: the meaningless control scored the same

key_numbers.label: Extra help when hint wording matched the code
key_numbers.sub: but only surface overlap, not real correctness

key_numbers.label: Extra help from an answer-leaking hint
key_numbers.sub: and only because it contains the solution

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra help the hint adds at the real decision points
charts.chart_read: Bars show how much a hint changes the grader's preference for the correct branch versus giving no hint at all; the zero line means no added help, and taller is better. The four bars are the recipe hint, a scrambled fake hint, an answer-leaking hint, and cases where the hint wording overlapped the code.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe-hint bar sits at zero, level with the scrambled fake hint; only the wording-overlap bar towers up, so the hint helps by matching surface text, not by teaching correctness.

charts.chart_plain_title: How strongly each grader already favors the correct code
charts.chart_read: Bars show how strongly each grader already favors the correct branch over the wrong one; taller means a stronger, more confident preference. Left to right: no hint at all, the recipe hint, a scrambled hint, and an answer-leaking hint.
charts.chart_takeaway: All four bars are nearly the same height, meaning the grader already favored the correct branch on its own, so adding the recipe hint changes almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Broad extra weight the hint puts on individual code tokens
charts.chart_read: Bars show how much extra weight the hinted grader puts on individual pieces of code; taller means more upweighting. The groups compare the recipe hint on correct versus wrong pieces against a scrambled hint and an answer-leaking hint.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe-hint-on-correct-pieces bar is tallest, hinting at broad help, but this optimistic view collapses at the specific forks that actually separate right code from wrong.

qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.verdict_tag: Trained grader wins at home, not away
qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.concept_primer: Imagine several programs that all pass the checks you can see, but only some pass secret checks you cannot. The job is to pick the one that will pass the secret checks too.
qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.plain_question: Can we teach the model to reliably pick which surface-correct program will also pass the hidden checks?
qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.plain_answer: It depends. Training the model to judge candidates lifted the right-pick rate from 72% to 81% on familiar problems, but on a new problem set the untrained model judged slightly better (94% vs 90%). Both crushed naive picking.
qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.why_it_matters: Training a small model to grade its own candidate programs pays off when new work resembles the training problems; on unfamiliar problems the untrained model may already grade better, so test before trusting it.

key_numbers.label: Right pick, familiar problems

key_numbers.label: Trained grader vs naive picking
key_numbers.sub: grabbing the first surface-correct program vs the trained grader

key_numbers.label: Right pick, new problem set
key_numbers.sub: here the untrained grader edged out the trained one

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each way of picking lands a fully-correct program
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often the chosen program also passes the hidden checks (taller is better), across six ways of choosing. Two colors compare the familiar problem set against a new one; the last bar is the best-possible pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained grader's bar is tallest on the familiar set but slightly shorter than the untrained grader on the new set; every grader towers over naive picking.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most programs that pass visible checks still fail the hidden ones
charts.chart_read: For each problem set, one bar counts programs per task that pass the visible checks and a second, shorter bar counts how many of those also pass the hidden checks. A bigger gap means picking is harder.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the familiar set barely half the surface-correct programs survive the hidden checks, versus about three-quarters on the new set, so choosing well matters more there.

qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.verdict_tag: Better questions help, proposing them is the gap
qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.concept_primer: Picture a 20-Questions game where you must identify a hidden answer by asking a checker, but you can only pick from a short menu of pre-written questions. Better questions on the menu mean you solve more puzzles.
qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.plain_question: Can the model do better at guessing a hidden answer if it gets a smarter menu of questions to ask?
qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.plain_answer: It depends. A smarter question menu lifted success from 42.5% to 49.4%, and light training added a small real gain to 51.2%. But a best-possible chooser scanning every question hit 86.9%, so most headroom is in inventing good questions, not picking among them.
qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.why_it_matters: Effort is better spent generating high-information questions to ask than tuning how the model picks from a short fixed menu. On hard puzzles the winning questions already exist but never reach the menu.

charts.chart_takeaway: The two rightmost bars (best-possible choosers) tower over every learned bar, meaning most success is still left on the table by real players.

charts.chart_plain_title: Success rises with each extra question asked
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many questions the player is allowed (0 to 3); vertical axis is success rate at finding the hidden answer, higher is better. Each line is a different player, from simple rules to the best-possible chooser.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-possible-chooser line separates from the pack at the very first question, so the edge comes from asking better questions, not smarter later choices.

charts.chart_takeaway: On the hard puzzle every real player scores near zero while the full-list best-possible chooser reaches 73.8%, proving the winning questions exist but go unused.

qwen35_4b_oracle_process_grpo.concept_primer: Think of a game like Guess Who: you get only a few questions, each one rules out some suspects, and choosing questions wisely pins down the hidden answer faster.
qwen35_4b_oracle_process_grpo.plain_question: Can a small AI learn to ask smarter narrowing questions to identify a hidden answer, instead of just guessing?
qwen35_4b_oracle_process_grpo.plain_answer: Yes. Training taught the model to pick better questions, solving nearly half the puzzles versus about a third when untrained, closing roughly 78% of the gap to a perfect question-picker.
qwen35_4b_oracle_process_grpo.why_it_matters: You can train a small model to steer a search process rather than answer directly. Control runs with scrambled clues collapsed, confirming it learned real signal, not formatting tricks.

charts.chart_takeaway: Trained pickers rise well above random guessing and the untrained model, nearly matching the perfect picker; feed them scrambled clues or feedback and they collapse back toward guessing.

charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many questions are allowed, zero to three; vertical is share of puzzles solved. Every line starts together at zero questions, then higher lines are better. The top line is the perfect picker.
