charts.chart_read: Bars group two puzzle types: an information-rich one where questions reveal a lot, and a sparse one where they barely narrow the suspects. Height is share solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Training lifts scores sharply on information-rich puzzles but barely moves the sparse ones, where even a perfect picker solves under one in ten, a hard ceiling.

qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.verdict_tag: More thinking room, more reasoning payoff
qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.concept_primer: Before answering, the model can write out its working on a scratchpad. The test: does that genuine step-by-step reasoning actually help, or would the same benefit come from just having more blank space to scribble?
qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.plain_question: When the model is given far more room to think first, does its genuine reasoning stop mattering, outpaced by the sheer extra space?
qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.plain_answer: No. Giving the model more room to think makes its genuine reasoning matter more, not less. Its edge over the same words in scrambled order grew from about 11% to 15% as the room quadrupled, overturning the fear that longer thinking washes coherence out.
qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.why_it_matters: Let the model write out real, ordered reasoning and give it ample room. What lifts accuracy is the coherence of what it writes, not raw length or extra scratch time, and that payoff holds up as you extend it.

key_numbers.label: Reasoning's edge as thinking room grows
key_numbers.sub: how much genuine reasoning beats the same words scrambled, at short then long thinking

key_numbers.label: Payoff of meaningless filler
key_numbers.sub: padding the scratchpad with blank dots gave no gain over not thinking at all

key_numbers.label: Result of pasting a wrong problem's reasoning
key_numbers.sub: another task's thinking sends the model to the wrong answer almost every time

charts.chart_plain_title: Genuine reasoning's edge over scrambled thinking grows with more room
charts.chart_read: The line tracks how much more often the code passes with genuine reasoning versus the exact same words shuffled into random order. Left to right is more thinking room; higher means real reasoning matters more.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line climbs instead of falling toward zero, so more thinking room makes coherent reasoning matter more, refuting the idea that extra thinking washes its value out.

charts.chart_plain_title: Five kinds of pre-answer thinking compared across thinking-room sizes
charts.chart_read: Each line is one kind of thinking the model does before answering, plotted as room increases left to right; height is how often the code passed. Genuine reasoning sits on top, a foreign task's thinking at the bottom; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Genuine thinking stays far above scrambled words, blank filler and no-thinking (which cluster together), while foreign thinking sits near the floor, so coherent reasoning alone carries the gain.

qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.verdict_tag: The perfect pruning signal existed, but the model could not read it
qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.concept_primer: Imagine a maze with more than a million complete routes. A perfect map can keep only four promising routes at each step. This test asks whether the small model can imitate that map by judging unfinished routes.
qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.plain_question: Can the fixed small model recognize which unfinished program routes can still reach the right answer well enough to prune the search?
qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.plain_answer: No for this route description. The perfect checker reduced more than a million complete routes to four and kept a solution every time, but the model's thinking-based scores ranked sibling routes about as well as chance. A cheaper immediate score was slightly better, yet still far below the launch requirement. The planned full search and training follow-up were stopped.
qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.why_it_matters: A useful hidden signal is not automatically a usable model signal. Measuring rankings within the same problem prevented a misleading overall score from triggering a large, expensive search. It also showed that exact depth-five search already finishes quickly on this task family, so learned pruning must clear a real cost bar.

key_numbers.label: Perfect-checker development paths
key_numbers.sub: four retained leaves instead of 1,048,576; 262,144x compression

key_numbers.label: Thinking route ranking
key_numbers.sub: chance within each problem; cheaper immediate scoring reached 55.6%

key_numbers.label: Exact depth-five reference
key_numbers.sub: 60/60 pools contained a solution; visible-only choice succeeded on 56/60

charts.chart_plain_title: Ranking unfinished routes within each problem
charts.chart_read: For each method, the first bar measures whether viable routes rank above dead routes within the same problem; 50% is chance. The second bar measures how many viable children survive when only four are kept; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Thinking sits at chance and retains fewer viable children than the cheaper immediate score. Since neither clears the pre-set requirement, the experiment correctly stops before full search.

qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.verdict_tag: Coaching for coverage lost to plain sampling
qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.concept_primer: Ask the model for several draft solutions per coding problem; "coverage" is the share of problems where at least one draft actually works, like buying several lottery tickets to raise your odds.
qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.plain_question: Does lightly coaching the model to spread its drafts wider solve more coding problems than simply telling it to be more creative when it drafts?
qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.plain_answer: No. The coached model solved about 44% of unseen problems with four drafts each, worse than the 69% from simply asking the untouched model to draft more creatively, and even below its own uncoached 50%.
qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.why_it_matters: Before investing in extra training to widen a model's answers, first try just dialing up its creativity when it drafts; that cheaper knob beat the trained version here.

key_numbers.label: Problems solved with four creative drafts
key_numbers.sub: untouched model, most creative sampling

key_numbers.label: Problems solved after coaching
key_numbers.sub: the trained version, four drafts each

key_numbers.label: Change from coaching
key_numbers.sub: coaching lowered its own starting point

key_numbers.label: Training rounds that gave any signal
key_numbers.sub: most rounds were all-fail or all-pass

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of unseen problems solved by at least one draft
charts.chart_read: Bars compare four ways of drafting; height is the share of problems where at least one draft works, so taller is better. The coached model is the third bar; the other three are the untouched model at different creativity or draft counts.
charts.chart_takeaway: The coached bar is the shortest, below even the plain untouched model, while the most-creative untouched sampling stands tallest.

charts.chart_plain_title: First-draft accuracy and how different the drafts are
charts.chart_read: For each drafting method, one bar shows how often the very first draft is correct and another shows how different the drafts are from each other; higher is better on both. The coached model is the third group.
charts.chart_takeaway: The coached model has the lowest bars on both measures, so it also weakened first-draft quality and made drafts more alike.

charts.chart_plain_title: Useful learning signal across the training rounds
charts.chart_read: Each point is one training round; the line shows how many of eight drafts passed. Rounds sitting at zero (all failed) or at eight (all passed) teach the model nothing; only in-between rounds help.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most points sit flat at zero or eight, so only a handful of rounds gave any usable teaching signal, explaining why the coaching stalled.

qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.verdict_tag: Draft-first search does not beat plain sampling
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.concept_primer: Normally the model writes each solution from scratch, then checks which ones pass. This test instead hands it promising half-written code drafts to finish, asking whether starting from a good draft finds answers more cheaply.
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.plain_question: Does starting from promising half-written code drafts help the model solve more programming problems, for the same compute, than writing complete solutions from scratch?
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.plain_answer: No. Every draft-first approach solved the same share of problems as plain from-scratch writing, about 75%, and never more. Starting from good drafts changed how the model explored but produced no extra solved problems, so the idea was set aside.
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.why_it_matters: Before building machinery to score and pick promising code drafts, confirm it actually solves more problems. Here it did not: the drafts added variety but no new answers, so the added complexity was not worth it.

key_numbers.label: Problems solved, plain writing
key_numbers.sub: share of the 12 problems solved

key_numbers.label: Problems solved, best draft-first
key_numbers.sub: same share, no improvement

key_numbers.label: Compute per method
key_numbers.sub: units of compute; drafts cheaper but solve no more

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of problems solved by each search method
charts.chart_read: Each group is one search method: plain from-scratch writing versus five draft-first variants. Darker bars show the share of the 12 problems solved at all; lighter bars the share solved on a first pick. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every draft-first bar tops out at the same solved share as plain writing and none rises above it, so the drafts add no new solutions.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute used by each search method
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one search method; bar height is the total compute it burned, so shorter is cheaper. Plain from-scratch writing sits on the left; the draft-first variants follow.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best draft-first bar is about four times shorter than plain writing, so it is cheaper, but only because the answers were already known when picking that draft.

charts.chart_plain_title: How varied the generated programs are
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one search method; taller bars mean the finished programs differ more. Two shades: how differently the programs run, and how differently they are written. Neither direction is inherently better here.
charts.chart_takeaway: Draft-first bars are much taller for how-differently-they-run, meaning more varied behavior, yet that extra variety never converted into more problems solved.

qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.verdict_tag: The exact number is not hidden inside
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.concept_primer: The model solves each puzzle with a hidden rule that has a specific number in it, like "add 3." We test whether that number lives inside the model's mind or can just be read straight off the example inputs and outputs.
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.plain_question: When the model solves these puzzles with a hidden rule like "add 3," is the exact number stored inside its mind, or just readable from the examples?
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.plain_answer: Just readable. Simple arithmetic on the examples recovers the exact number as well as, or better than, peeking at the model's internal activity, so it is not hidden knowledge to unearth. The kind of action, by contrast, the model genuinely does work out internally.
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.why_it_matters: Before hunting for hidden ability in a model, try a dumb baseline: if plain arithmetic on the examples recovers a detail equally well, that detail is not locked in the weights. Spend effort only where the model truly computes something.

key_numbers.label: Reading the kind of action
key_numbers.sub: from inside the model vs from the examples — the model wins, so it is computed internally

key_numbers.label: Reading the exact number
key_numbers.sub: from inside vs from the examples — the examples win, so it is not stored inside

key_numbers.label: Puzzles solved once given the number
key_numbers.sub: handing the model the right number is what unlocks the puzzle

charts.chart_plain_title: Can you read the hidden rule from the model, or from the examples?
charts.chart_read: Each pair of bars shows how often a hidden-rule detail is correctly recovered from the model's internal activity versus from the examples alone; taller is better. Left group is the kind of action, right group is the exact number.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model bar tops the examples bar for the kind of action but drops below it for the exact number — so the action is computed inside, the number is merely read off the examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: Giving the model the exact number is what lets it solve the puzzles
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of puzzles solved under different hints; taller is better. One color is solved on the first try, the other solved within a few tries. Hints range from nothing, to the kind of action only, to the full rule including its number.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bar for the full rule with its number towers over the bar for the kind of action alone, and a deliberately wrong number solves nothing — the exact number is what unlocks solving.

qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.verdict_tag: Hints help, but not the decoded one
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.concept_primer: When solving a multi-step coding puzzle, the model internally forms a hunch about what the first step should be. This test reads that hunch out and writes it back into the instructions as a reminder.
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.plain_question: Can reading the model's internal hunch about a puzzle's first step, then writing it into the prompt, help it solve more?
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.plain_answer: It depends. Reminding the model of the complete first step, the action and its specific value, roughly six-tupled the first-try solve rate on medium puzzles. But the hunch we can actually read captures only the action, not the value, so it changed nothing on its own.
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.why_it_matters: Feeding the model a concrete first step in the prompt can unlock harder puzzles it otherwise fumbles, but only when the hint includes the specific value, not just the kind of action. The model's readable hunch stops short of that, and nothing helps once a puzzle is beyond its internal reach.

key_numbers.label: First-try solve, medium puzzles (full first step given)
key_numbers.sub: six times higher than no hint

key_numbers.label: Solve within several tries (full first step given)
key_numbers.sub: six times higher than no hint

key_numbers.label: The model's own read-out hint alone
key_numbers.sub: 5% → 6%, essentially no gain

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the complete first step lifts solving; a partial hint does nothing
charts.chart_read: Each bar group is a different prompt hint, from no hint through the true first step (its kind only, or kind plus its specific value) to a wrong step. Taller is better; one color counts first-try solves, the other solves within several tries.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the 'full first step' bars stand tall; the kind-only and the model's decoded-hunch bars stay as low as no hint, so the specific value is what unlocks solving.

charts.chart_plain_title: The hint works on medium puzzles but does nothing on the hardest
charts.chart_read: Bars compare the first-try solve rate on medium-depth puzzles against the hardest puzzles, across five hints, with the two colors marking the two difficulty levels. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar for the hardest puzzles stays flat near zero, so even handing the model the true first step cannot help when its internal reasoning never worked that step out.

qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.verdict_tag: Finding answers is the wall, not picking them
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.concept_primer: Think of the model writing several draft answers to a problem, then an automatic picker choosing one to submit. Success needs both: at least one correct draft must exist, and the picker must choose it.
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.plain_question: When the model writes several draft answers and a picker submits one, is the weak link writing a correct draft or picking it?
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.plain_answer: It's the writing. Once a correct draft exists, the picker reliably submits it, matching the best possible choice on both problem sets. The remaining misses on the harder set are cases where no correct draft was ever written.
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.why_it_matters: Effort is better spent helping the model produce a correct draft on hard problems than on building a smarter picker, since picking is already near-perfect at this scale.

key_numbers.label: Hard set solved when a right draft exists
key_numbers.sub: the picker captures this full ceiling

key_numbers.label: Easy set solved
key_numbers.sub: nearly every problem, at any picker

key_numbers.label: Easy-set drafts needed with smart stopping
key_numbers.sub: same score, far fewer tries

key_numbers.label: Hard set traded for cheaper stopping
key_numbers.sub: small accuracy given back to save tries

charts.chart_plain_title: When a right answer exists, the picker finds it
charts.chart_read: For two problem sets, taller bars mean more problems solved. The first bar is how often at least one correct try was written; the other bars are what different auto-pickers actually submitted. Bars level with the first are ideal.
charts.chart_takeaway: The smart picker's bar reaches the same height as the best-possible bar on both sets, so nothing correct was left on the table.

charts.chart_plain_title: Writing more tries helps the hard set only up to a point
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many tries the model was allowed; the vertical axis is how often at least one correct try appeared. Higher and rising is better. One line is the easy set, one is the hard set.
charts.chart_takeaway: The easy-set line starts high and flat while the hard-set line climbs then flattens at 60%, showing extra tries stop adding correct answers.

charts.chart_plain_title: Smart stopping uses far fewer tries for the same result
charts.chart_read: For each set, bars show the average number of tries used per problem; shorter is better. Bars compare always going to the limit, a perfect stop-when-done rule, and the learned stop rule.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned-stop bar is far shorter than the always-to-limit bar, especially on the easy set, meaning most tries were saved.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every picking method lands on nearly the same score
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each picking method submitted a correct answer; taller is better. Six methods are grouped for the easy set and the hard set.
charts.chart_takeaway: All bars sit at nearly equal height, so which picker you use barely matters once the tries are written.

charts.chart_plain_title: Stop rules keep almost all the accuracy
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often the submitted answer was correct under three stop-when-you-think-you're-done rules; taller is better, shown for both sets.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned stop rule matches the easy set fully and gives up only a sliver on the hard set versus the perfect stop rule.

charts.chart_plain_title: Runs quit early once a try passes the visible checks
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the try limit allowed; the vertical axis is how many tries were actually drawn on average; lower means it quit earlier. One line per set.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines fall below the limit at the high end, and the easy set stays lowest, because a passing try shows up sooner there.

qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.verdict_tag: Cheap reliability shortcuts all fell short
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.concept_primer: Think of a proofreader choosing which draft to trust. The test here: does handing them a specific worked example of a mistake help more than handing them the same notes randomly shuffled?
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.plain_question: Can a cheap trick — a worked failing example, or the model's own confidence — reliably pick the correct code before doing any training?
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.plain_answer: No. A real worked failing example pointed the model toward the fix no better than randomly shuffled notes, and ranking drafts by the model's own confidence picked fewer correct answers than simply taking the first draft that passed the visible checks.
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.why_it_matters: Before teaching a model to judge its own code, confirm the judging signal actually beats a shuffled control. Here it didn't, so the payoff lies in gathering independent behavioral evidence rather than another learned judge.

key_numbers.label: Nudge toward the fix from a real worked example
key_numbers.sub: real evidence nudged less than randomly shuffled fake evidence — so it added nothing genuine

key_numbers.label: Correct drafts picked by model's own confidence
key_numbers.sub: fewer than the 8 you get by just taking the first draft that passes

key_numbers.label: Wrong drafts mistakenly committed by that confidence rule
key_numbers.sub: more mistakes than the 6 from the simple first-pass rule

charts.chart_plain_title: A real worked example didn't help the teacher find the fix
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a kind of hint given to the teacher; height shows how much more it leans toward the correct fix. A bar only counts as real help if it clears the shuffled-evidence bar; the far-right bar shows what happens when the answer is essentially leaked.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real worked-example bar sits just below the shuffled-evidence bar and far below the answer-leaked bar, so genuine evidence added nothing the audit could trust.

charts.chart_plain_title: Ranking by the model's confidence picks worse than the simplest rule
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by selection method. The dark bar is drafts that were actually correct (taller is better); the light bar is drafts that were actually wrong (shorter is better). Compare model-confidence against just taking the first passing draft, and against a best-possible answer-key pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: Under model-confidence the correct bar shrinks and the wrong bar grows versus the first-pass rule, meaning confidence-ranking commits more mistakes, not fewer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Colder, less-random decoding didn't improve the fixes
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by how randomly the model writes code, coldest on the left. The dark bar is the share of tasks where a working draft existed; the light bar is the share solved on the first try. Higher is better on both; no setting clearly wins.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bars stay essentially level across temperatures — the coldest setting nudges first-try success up but drops coverage, so nothing beats the existing baseline.

qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.verdict_tag: Lookups help but trusting them fails
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.concept_primer: Think of handing the model a cookbook of already-solved problems: for a task it can't crack alone, it finds the closest recipe and tweaks it to fit the new problem.
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.plain_question: When the model gets stuck, does looking up a similar solved problem fix it, and can the system tell which fix to trust?
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.plain_answer: Partly. Looking up a similar solved problem rescued a third of the stuck tasks, double a random lookup. But no automatic check could reliably tell the good fixes from the wrong ones, so most couldn't be safely trusted.
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.why_it_matters: Retrieving and adapting past solutions is a real way to unstick a small model, but the payoff only lands if you can verify which candidate is right. Invest next in stronger checks, not more candidates.

key_numbers.label: Tasks the model solved on its own
key_numbers.sub: 56 of 80 problems

key_numbers.label: Stuck tasks rescued by looking up a similar solved problem
key_numbers.sub: 8 of 24 — double the 17% from random lookup

key_numbers.label: Best possible coverage if every good rescue were picked
key_numbers.sub: only with flawless picking

key_numbers.label: Committed answers that were actually wrong
key_numbers.sub: the shipped example checks couldn't separate right from wrong

charts.chart_plain_title: Meaning-matched lookup rescues the most stuck tasks
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a lookup strategy; bar height is the share of the 24 stuck tasks it rescued into the candidate pool, so taller is better. The meaning-matched lookup is tallest and plain copying is shortest.
charts.chart_takeaway: The meaning-matched bar reaches a third of stuck tasks, roughly double the random-lookup bar, showing that matching by meaning is what recovers stuck tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every automatic picker commits as many wrong answers as right ones
charts.chart_read: For each picking method there are paired bars, one for correct answers committed and one for wrong ones, out of 24 stuck tasks; fewer wrong is better. Only the last method, a cheat that peeks at hidden answers, has no wrong bar.
charts.chart_takeaway: The wrong-commit bar matches the correct-commit bar for every real method, meaning the checks cannot separate right from wrong; only the peeking baseline avoids mistakes.

qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.verdict_tag: Shippable schedulers didn't beat sampling more
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.concept_primer: Think of several dice, each weighted to favor different answers. A 'portfolio' rolls a few styles and a manager decides which style deserves more rolls on each problem, hoping to beat just rolling one die harder.
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.plain_question: Can mixing several answer-writing styles and picking the best one per problem solve more coding tasks than just generating more tries from a single style?
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.plain_answer: No. Every manager we could actually ship tied or fell behind simply rolling one style harder. Only a perfect-hindsight picker did better, solving 75% of problems versus 67% for sample-more, showing the payoff exists but isn't yet reachable.
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.why_it_matters: The room to improve is real but only a smarter style-picker can claim it. Invest in predicting which style will help each problem, not in more blind sampling or scaling one style.

key_numbers.label: Best a shippable manager reached
key_numbers.sub: problems solved — same as just sampling more, no gain

key_numbers.label: If you always picked the right style
key_numbers.sub: hindsight-only ceiling, not something you can deploy yet

key_numbers.label: Compute for that ceiling
key_numbers.sub: about 22k vs 44k units, cheaper than sampling more

charts.chart_plain_title: Which answer-writing style wins, per problem, across six approaches
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one approach for choosing generation styles; height is the share of 24 coding problems solved, so taller is better. The far-right bar is a perfect-hindsight picker; the others are approaches you could actually ship.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the rightmost hindsight bar rises clearly above the rest, while every shippable approach sits level with or below simply sampling more from one style.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving power of five style schedules on a larger 80-problem pool
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a schedule for spending generation effort; height is the share of 80 problems solved, taller is better. The far-right bar throws every candidate answer at each problem regardless of cost.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained-scheduler bar actually dips below the plain fixed schedules, and the towering far-right bar shows lots of solvable problems nobody's approach reached.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute spent by each approach on the 24-problem set
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one approach; height is the total compute it burned across 24 problems, so shorter is better. Read it alongside the first chart, which shows how many problems each solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The perfect-hindsight bar is one of the shortest, about half the compute of sampling-more, yet it solved the most problems, so its payoff is cheap not costly.

qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.verdict_tag: Repairing the core skill did not transfer
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.concept_primer: Picture the model as a bundle of separate habits, each tied to one shape of question and one shape of answer. Here the shaky habit was tracing a multi-step process in your head.
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.plain_question: If we perfectly fix a small model's ability to trace a process step by step, do the harder skills that seem to lean on it improve too?
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.plain_answer: No. We fully repaired the step-by-step tracing habit, yet every harder skill that appeared to depend on it stayed flat. The improvement stayed locked to the exact question-and-answer shape we trained, disproving the idea that fixing a foundation ripples outward.
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.why_it_matters: For a small fixed model, do not expect repairing a foundational skill to spread to related tasks. Training buys only the exact task shape you drill, plus close look-alikes. When a piece is missing, hand the model a tool instead.

key_numbers.label: Tracing accuracy, short vs long process
key_numbers.sub: one step chained versus four steps chained

key_numbers.label: Longest process: deliberate vs snap answer
key_numbers.sub: pausing to think more than doubles accuracy

key_numbers.label: Accuracy kept per added step
key_numbers.sub: each new step in the chain costs more than the last

charts.chart_plain_title: Tracing a process step by step gets worse as steps pile up
charts.chart_read: Moving right chains more steps into the process; height is how often the model traces the whole thing correctly, so higher is better. The upper line pauses to think first, the lower line answers immediately.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines fall steeply, from near-perfect at one step to about a third correct by four steps, so the tracing habit is genuinely broken on longer chains.

charts.chart_plain_title: Deliberating first generally beats answering immediately
charts.chart_read: Same left-to-right count of chained steps and the same higher-is-better correctness. Two lines pause to think, two answer immediately; within each pair, one chain includes steps that throw information away and one does not.
charts.chart_takeaway: The deliberate lines mostly sit above the snap-answer lines, and the snap-answer information-discarding chain sits lowest, so thinking helps rather than hurts the tracing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Each extra step costs more accuracy than the one before
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows how much of the previous length's accuracy survives when one more step is added to the chain; taller bars mean less was lost. Left adds a second step, right adds a fourth.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bars shrink from left to right, so damage from each added step keeps growing, meaning the failure is an accelerating collapse rather than one fixed error rate.

qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.verdict_tag: Only hand-written templates survive a new task
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.concept_primer: Think of a fill-in-the-blank blueprint: the system fills the blanks to build many candidate programs, then keeps the ones that run correctly. The test is whether the right program still lands in that candidate pile.
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.plain_question: When the task changes to need a genuinely new operation, does the correct program still show up among the candidates the system builds?
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.plain_answer: It depends on the blueprint. When the blueprint explicitly names the new operation, the correct program appears every time; when the blueprint is auto-generated it appears only 17% of the time, and a bare blueprint never finds it.
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.why_it_matters: Before building a smarter picker to choose among candidates, invest in blueprints that name and shape the new operations. A perfect picker cannot rescue a correct program that was never built in the first place.

key_numbers.label: New-operation task, auto-generated blueprint
key_numbers.sub: how often the correct program was even among the candidates

key_numbers.label: New-operation task, hand-written blueprint
key_numbers.sub: naming the operation in the blueprint fixes it completely

key_numbers.label: New-operation task, bare blueprint
key_numbers.sub: the correct program never appeared at all

charts.chart_plain_title: Does the right program survive when the task shifts?
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars across three task types (familiar task, same task just renamed, task needing a new operation). Within each, three bars for auto-generated, hand-written, and bare blueprints. Taller means the correct program was in the candidate pile more often; full height is best.
charts.chart_takeaway: Hand-written blueprint bars stay at full height everywhere, while the auto-generated bar collapses to a sliver once the task needs a genuinely new operation.

charts.chart_plain_title: Allowing more fill-ins per blank does not rescue auto blueprints
charts.chart_read: Lines show how often the correct program appears on the new-operation task as you allow more options per blank (left to right). Higher is better. One line for hand-written, one for auto-generated, one for bare blueprints.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hand-written line climbs to full, but the auto and bare lines stay flat along the bottom, because the new operation is simply never proposed no matter how many options each blank gets.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra check-queries sharpen a choice but cannot create a missing program
charts.chart_read: Lines show how often the chosen program is actually correct as the system spends more clarifying queries (left to right). Higher is better. Two lines have the right program available; one line (new operation, auto blueprint) does not.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two lines with the right program available climb to 100% within two or three queries, while the line missing that program stays flat, showing queries help only after the answer already exists.

qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.concept_primer: Think of a repair technician handed a broken recipe plus a few taste-tests showing how the dish should come out; the job is to rewrite the recipe so it matches. Here the model repairs a broken mini-program the same way.
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.plain_question: Does showing the model a few worked repair examples help it fix brand-new kinds of broken programs it never trained on?
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.plain_answer: It depends. Adding a handful of worked repair examples fixed every practiced problem type and roughly doubled success on brand-new unseen types, from about 20% to 44%, but that still leaves most new problems unfixed.
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.why_it_matters: A few worked examples reliably nail the familiar cases and clearly help on new ones, so they are worth adding, but do not expect them to carry the model across genuinely harder, unfamiliar problems on their own.

key_numbers.label: New unseen problems fixed
key_numbers.sub: without vs with worked examples

key_numbers.label: Practiced problems fixed
key_numbers.sub: worked examples saturate the familiar cases

key_numbers.label: Fixes when the example is removed
key_numbers.sub: success collapses without a correct worked example

charts.chart_plain_title: A few worked examples help on new problems, but only partway
charts.chart_read: Three training setups along the bottom: no worked examples, plus 60, plus 80. For each, two bars: problem types the model practiced versus brand-new unseen types. Taller means more programs fixed correctly; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Adding worked examples lifts the practiced bars to full and roughly doubles the unseen bars, but the unseen bars still sit well below half.

charts.chart_plain_title: The fixes rely on the model actually reading the worked example
charts.chart_read: Three bars for one trained model on unseen problems: given a correct worked example, given none, and given a scrambled one. Height is the share of programs fixed correctly; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-example bar is tallest and the scrambled one is lowest, showing the model reads the example rather than guessing from surface patterns.

charts.chart_plain_title: Some new problem types jump to near-perfect, others stay stuck
charts.chart_read: Six unseen problem types along the bottom, two bars each: the model without worked examples versus with 60 of them. Height is the share fixed correctly; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Several types leap from zero to near-perfect while others stay at zero or slip, showing help is very uneven across problem types.

qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.verdict_tag: Labeled strategies did not diversify retries
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.concept_primer: When the model can't crack a coding problem, you retry with more attempts. The hope here: first teach it named solution styles (loop, recursion, math) so retries explore genuinely different approaches instead of the same guess.
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.plain_question: Does teaching the model to label its solution style make its extra attempts recover more failed coding problems than plain retrying?
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.plain_answer: No. The style-labeled approach recovered 37.5% of previously-failed problems, no better than plain extra attempts at 41.7%; a scrambled-label version with meaningless tags did just as well, so the labels added nothing.
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.why_it_matters: Attaching named solution styles to a model's retries is not worth the extra training and sampling cost; plain repeated attempts already do as well, and only much larger, more expensive attempt pools recover meaningfully more.

key_numbers.label: Failed problems recovered, style-labeled retries
key_numbers.sub: the specially trained approach

key_numbers.label: Failed problems recovered, plain retries
key_numbers.sub: no special training, same attempt budget

key_numbers.label: Failed problems recovered, scrambled labels
key_numbers.sub: control with meaningless style tags

key_numbers.label: Problems solved overall
key_numbers.sub: base handful of tries vs style-labeled retries

charts.chart_plain_title: Failed problems recovered, by retry method
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one retry method; height is the share of previously-failed problems it recovered, taller is better. The style-labeled bars sit beside plain retries and a scrambled-label control; only the two big multi-batch bars on the right rise higher.
charts.chart_takeaway: The style-labeled bar is no taller than plain retries and the scrambled-label bar, meaning the named strategies bought no real recovery advantage.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of all coding problems solved, by retry method
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one retry method; height is the share of all 80 problems solved, taller is better. Bars rise from a few base tries on the left to large multi-batch pools on the right, with the style-labeled methods in the middle.
charts.chart_takeaway: The style-labeled bars land in the same 81-83% band as ordinary extra attempts, so adding labeled strategies did not lift overall problems solved.

qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.verdict_tag: The bottleneck is the steps, not the numbers
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.concept_primer: Solving these tasks is like following a recipe: you need the right sequence of steps and the right amounts to plug in. This brief asks which of the two the model actually gets stuck on.
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.plain_question: When the model fails these multi-step problems, is it choosing the wrong sequence of steps, or the right steps with the wrong numbers plugged in?
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.plain_answer: The steps. The model fails because it can't find the right sequence of steps; whenever it is handed the correct steps, filling in the numbers always succeeds, so wrong numbers are never the hidden problem.
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.why_it_matters: To crack these problems, put effort into helping the model find the right sequence of steps, for example by searching or enumerating step-orders, rather than fixing the numbers, which take care of themselves.

key_numbers.label: Solved when handed the correct steps
key_numbers.sub: filling in the numbers always finishes

key_numbers.label: Solved on its own (3-step tasks)
key_numbers.sub: it rarely finds the right steps

key_numbers.label: Extra solved from right steps but wrong numbers
key_numbers.sub: no hidden wrong-number failures to unlock

key_numbers.label: Solved by trying 200 random step-sequences
key_numbers.sub: random steps are far from enough

charts.chart_plain_title: Right steps, not right numbers, is what the model lacks
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one way of solving the tasks; taller means more solved. Left to right: the model's one try, its best of several tries, whether its steps matched the correct sequence, and the model simply handed the correct steps. Within a cluster, one bar is easier 2-step tasks, the other harder 3-step tasks.
charts.chart_takeaway: The 'steps matched' bar is exactly as tall as the model's own success, while 'handed the correct steps' jumps to full height, so misses are wrong-step, not wrong-number.

charts.chart_plain_title: Random step-sequences barely crack the hard tasks
charts.chart_read: Lines show solve rate (higher is better) as more randomly guessed step-sequences are tried, each filled in with searched numbers. The upper line is easier 2-step tasks, the lower line the harder 3-step tasks.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even after 200 random step-sequences the 3-step line reaches only about 11%, far short of full success when the correct steps are known, so random steps can't stand in for the right ones.

qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.verdict_tag: Brute-force search keeps beating the model
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.concept_primer: Think of each task as a multi-step recipe. You can either have the model write the recipe from memory, or mechanically try every possible recipe and keep the one a calculator confirms works.
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.plain_question: As tasks need more steps, does the model's own recipe-writing ever start beating a try-everything search?
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.plain_answer: No. Going from 3-step to 4-step tasks, the model's recipe-writing collapsed from 51% to 10%, while try-everything search stayed near-perfect at 97%, so the gap only widened.
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.why_it_matters: When a calculator-style tool is available, lean on mechanical search to assemble multi-step solutions. Longer tasks make the model a worse structure-proposer, not a better one, so don't expect its instincts to take over.

key_numbers.label: Model writes the recipe (3 vs 4 steps)
key_numbers.sub: collapses as tasks get longer

key_numbers.label: Try-everything search (3 vs 4 steps)
key_numbers.sub: stays near-perfect

key_numbers.label: How far the model trails search
key_numbers.sub: the gap widens, never closes

charts.chart_plain_title: As tasks get longer, the model falls further behind mechanical search
charts.chart_read: Two lines over task length (3 vs 4 steps); vertical axis is success rate, higher is better. One line is the model writing the recipe from memory, the other is trying every possible recipe with a tool.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model's line plunges while the search line stays flat near the top; longer tasks make the model worse, so the two lines spread apart rather than crossing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Number of recipes to test explodes as tasks get longer
charts.chart_read: A single rising line over task length (3 to 5 steps); vertical axis counts how many possible recipes a full search must check, which multiplies roughly 16-fold per added step.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line rockets from a few thousand to about a million by 5 steps, so search eventually gets too costly, but the model's recipe-writing has already collapsed before that point.

qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.verdict_tag: Reusable code parts did not generalize
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.concept_primer: Think of a parts kit: instead of writing each hard coding problem from scratch, you try to build it by snapping together reusable code building blocks pulled from a library of past solutions.
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.plain_question: If you build hard coding problems from a library of reusable code parts, can you solve ones that a big batch of direct attempts couldn't?
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.plain_answer: No. Reusing and adapting past solutions solved none of the 9 hard tasks, beating nothing. Parts hand-built for each specific task did solve all 9, but those were custom-carved per task, not reusable, so the reuse idea failed.
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.why_it_matters: Do not expect a shared library of reused solutions to expand what a model can solve, and never trust the small visible sample of tests as a source of correct training targets; they routinely hide wrong answers.

key_numbers.label: Hard tasks solved by reusing adapted past solutions
key_numbers.sub: none — the reuse idea failed

key_numbers.label: Hard tasks solved by parts hand-built per task
key_numbers.sub: but custom-made each time, not reusable

key_numbers.label: Passed the visible sample checks yet secretly wrong
key_numbers.sub: of answers that looked right on shown tests

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of hard tasks solved, by how the answer was built
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one way of building solutions; bar height is the share of the 9 hard tasks fully solved, taller is better. Custom-built-per-task and the combined pool reach the top; reusing adapted past solutions sits flat at the bottom.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reuse bar is flat at zero while the custom-built bar reaches full height, so reusing past solutions solved nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often answers that looked right on sample tests were secretly wrong
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a building method; height is the share of answers that passed the small visible sample of tests but failed the full checks, so lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar is high, from 41% to 100%, meaning passing the visible sample tests routinely hides a wrong answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Answers that passed sample tests versus those that were truly correct
charts.chart_read: For each building method, the taller bar counts answers passing the visible sample tests and the shorter bar counts those that also passed the full checks; a big gap means many false wins.
charts.chart_takeaway: For reusing past solutions, 8 answers passed the sample tests but zero survived the full checks.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many tries each method needed per task
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a building method; height is the average number of candidate answers generated per task, so lower is cheaper.
charts.chart_takeaway: The expanded hand-built method reached full coverage at about 15 tries per task, far cheaper than the combined pool's roughly 57.

qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.verdict_tag: Cheaper thinking, same accuracy, not higher
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.concept_primer: Think of a student who answers fast, checks the one worked example given, and only slows down to think harder if that quick answer fails the example.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.plain_question: Can a simple answer-first, think-harder-only-if-needed rule get the same accuracy as always thinking hard, but for less effort?
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.plain_answer: Yes, for cost, not for accuracy. The quick-check rule matches the accuracy of moderate fixed thinking while using a quarter to a half of the effort, but it never beats the single best fixed effort level.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.why_it_matters: Use answer-first-then-escalate to cut thinking effort sharply at almost no accuracy loss. But if peak accuracy is the goal, a well-tuned fixed effort level still wins.

key_numbers.label: Thinking effort per task
key_numbers.sub: best fixed level vs the quick-check rule

key_numbers.label: Answers correct
key_numbers.sub: quick-check rule vs best fixed level

key_numbers.label: Quick check passed a wrong answer
key_numbers.sub: why the rule can't reach the best-possible 93%

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy versus thinking effort: quick-check rule against fixed effort levels
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is average thinking effort per task, the vertical axis is share of answers correct; up-and-left is better. One line is fixed effort levels, another is the quick-check rule, and a single dot marks the best-possible pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: The quick-check line sits well to the left of the fixed-effort line at the same height, reaching the same accuracy for far less effort.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of answers correct by strategy
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one strategy; taller means more answers correct. Bars run from no thinking, through fixed and quick-check strategies, up to the best-possible pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: The quick-check bars land just below the best fixed level and the best-possible pick, giving up only a few points of accuracy.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the quick check passed a wrong answer
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one strategy; the height is how often an answer passed the shown example yet was actually wrong. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Roughly one in ten quick-check commits are wrong answers that slipped through, which is what keeps the rule below the best-possible pick.

qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.verdict_tag: Thinking mode works, but cap it
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.concept_primer: This model can work through a problem on a private scratchpad before giving its final answer, like showing your work before writing the result. The test: does more scratchpad room mean better answers?
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.plain_question: Does letting this small coding model think on a scratchpad before answering actually improve its answers, and does more thinking always help?
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.plain_answer: Yes. Turning on the scratchpad lifted the share of coding problems it solves from 76% to 91% on its committed answer, beating the older setup that had thinking switched off. But more is not always better: unlimited thinking fell back to 84%.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.why_it_matters: Turn on the model's thinking mode, but cap how long it thinks. A moderate limit beats both no thinking and unlimited thinking, and the payoff is largest on the problems it otherwise keeps failing.

key_numbers.label: Problems solved, off vs on
key_numbers.sub: its committed answer, thinking off then on

key_numbers.label: Thinking with no limit
key_numbers.sub: worse than a moderate cap

key_numbers.label: Harder problems, off vs on
key_numbers.sub: middling-difficulty coding tasks

charts.chart_plain_title: More thinking improves the committed answer, up to a limit
charts.chart_read: Each budget along the bottom is how much thinking the model may do, from none to unlimited; height is the share of coding problems solved (higher is better). Darker bars are the single answer it commits to; lighter bars are its best of eight tries.
charts.chart_takeaway: The committed-answer bars climb from left, peak at a moderate thinking cap, then dip at unlimited thinking, gaining more than the best-of-eight ceiling does.

charts.chart_plain_title: All three measures rise then fall as thinking grows
charts.chart_read: Left to right is how large a thinking budget the model is given, from none to unlimited; height is the share of problems solved (higher is better). The three lines are its committed answer, a typical single answer, and an answer picked using the given example check.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line rises to a peak at a mid-size thinking cap then flattens or declines, showing that thinking past a point stops helping and can hurt.

charts.chart_plain_title: Thinking helps most on the harder problems
charts.chart_read: Problems are grouped by how hard they were without thinking, from already-easy to never-solved; height is the share solved (higher is better). Darker bars are no thinking, lighter bars are a fixed moderate thinking budget.
charts.chart_takeaway: The gap between the two bars widens as tasks get harder, meaning thinking adds the most on the problems the model otherwise keeps failing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Scrambled thinking recovers part of the benefit
charts.chart_read: Two thinking budgets sit along the bottom; height is the share of problems solved (higher is better). Three bars each: no thinking, the model's own thinking scrambled into nonsense order, and its real thinking.
charts.chart_takeaway: The scrambled bar lands between no-thinking and real-thinking, suggesting some early gain came from the effort itself, though later tests showed real reasoning matters more than this implies.

qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.verdict_tag: The gain is real reasoning, not busywork
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.concept_primer: Before answering, the model can jot private scratch notes. We test whether the payoff comes from the act of writing anything, or from the notes actually making sense.
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.plain_question: Does the model's thinking help because it does genuine reasoning, or just because writing extra words buys more processing time?
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.plain_answer: Genuine reasoning. Real, meaningful notes lifted problems solved from 75% to 86%, while blank filler and scrambled notes added nothing, and notes copied from a different problem sent the model to the wrong answer.
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.why_it_matters: Give the model room to reason and let it write its own coherent notes; padding the output with filler or irrelevant text wastes effort and pasting in unrelated notes actively derails it.

key_numbers.label: Solved with thinking off
key_numbers.sub: baseline, no scratch notes

key_numbers.label: Solved with real reasoning
key_numbers.sub: the model's own coherent notes

key_numbers.label: Solved with another problem's notes
key_numbers.sub: wrong notes drag it to the wrong problem

key_numbers.label: Gain from blank filler
key_numbers.sub: extra processing time alone buys nothing

charts.chart_plain_title: Coding problems solved for each kind of scratch notes
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of coding problems solved (taller is better) under five setups: no notes, blank filler dots, scrambled notes, the model's own real notes, and notes copied from a different problem.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the real-notes bar rises above the no-notes baseline; the copied-from-another-problem bar collapses to almost nothing, showing the model actually follows its notes.

charts.chart_plain_title: What each ingredient of the notes actually adds
charts.chart_read: Bars show how much each added ingredient changes the share of problems solved (above zero is a gain, below is harm): extra blank compute, merely-present-but-scrambled words, coherent meaning, and wrong-problem notes.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the coherent-meaning bar is positive and it carries the whole gain, while blank compute and scrambled words sit at zero and wrong notes plunge far below.

charts.chart_plain_title: Real notes keep their edge as thinking room grows
charts.chart_read: The line shows how much better real notes do than scrambled notes (higher is a bigger edge) as the model is given more room to think, left to right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line rises rather than falling, so the benefit of coherent thinking grows with more thinking room instead of washing out.

qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.verdict_tag: Extra thinking time cannot unlock planning
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.concept_primer: Picture a 3-move puzzle. Recognizing an almost-finished puzzle (one move left) is easy; planning the very first move toward a goal still three moves away is the hard part.
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.plain_question: Can letting the model think longer out loud let it plan the first move toward a goal that is still three moves away?
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.plain_answer: No. Longer thinking leaves the model at random-guess level when it must plan the first of three moves toward a distant goal, even though the same thinking nearly doubles its accuracy when the goal is only one move away.
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.why_it_matters: For genuine multi-step planning, more thinking time won't rescue a model — that gap needs actual training. Reserve extra thinking for recognizing near-finished problems, where it clearly pays off.

key_numbers.label: First move toward a 3-away goal
key_numbers.sub: still random-guess level even with the most thinking

key_numbers.label: Move when goal is 1 away
key_numbers.sub: thinking more than doubles recognition

key_numbers.label: Move when goal is 2 away
key_numbers.sub: thinking helps once the goal is closer

key_numbers.label: Random-guess level
key_numbers.sub: what pure luck would score

charts.chart_plain_title: Thinking helps only when the goal is close, not far
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show how often the model picks the right next move (taller is better), grouped by how far the goal is (3, 2, or 1 move away) and colored by how much thinking time it got. Around 3% is random-guess level.
charts.chart_takeaway: Far-goal bars stay near random no matter the thinking time, while near-goal bars jump higher with more thinking, showing thinking sharpens recognition, not planning.

charts.chart_plain_title: Planning the first far-off move stays at random no matter the thinking time
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how much thinking time the model gets; the vertical axis is how often it plans the correct first move toward a 3-away goal (higher is better). One line is the model, the flat line marks random-guess level.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model's line hugs the random-guess line and stays flat as thinking time grows, meaning more thinking buys essentially no planning ability.

qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.verdict_tag: Scrambled thinking reads as well as real
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.concept_primer: Imagine wiring the model like a lie detector: instead of trusting what it says, you read its internal 'brain' signals to guess whether the code it just wrote is actually correct.
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.plain_question: Can you tell if the model's freshly written code is right just by reading its internal signals, and does thinking help by actually reasoning?
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.plain_answer: Partly, but not through reasoning. Internal signals reveal correctness a bit better after the model thinks, yet scrambling that thinking into gibberish works just as well, so the gain comes from the act of thinking, not its logic.
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.why_it_matters: Don't count on the content of the model's step-by-step thinking to make it 'know' its own answer; the mere presence of thinking drives the effect. The internal read-out only weakly flags hidden failures, and only after thinking.

key_numbers.label: Telling right from wrong, no thinking
key_numbers.sub: detector score out of 100; 50 = a coin flip

key_numbers.label: Same detector after thinking
key_numbers.sub: any thinking makes correctness easier to read

key_numbers.label: Scrambled vs real thinking
key_numbers.sub: gibberish thinking reads just as well

key_numbers.label: Cheating safety check
key_numbers.sub: detector drops to coin-flip on fake labels

charts.chart_plain_title: How well internal signals reveal a right answer, by condition
charts.chart_read: Five conditions run along the bottom: no thinking, then real and scrambled thinking at two lengths. Tall dark bars show how reliably the detector reads correctness; short bars are a safety check that should sit near a coin flip. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every thinking bar rises above no-thinking, but the scrambled-thinking bars stand as tall or taller than the real-thinking ones, so the lift is not from reasoning.

charts.chart_plain_title: Detector strength at each internal layer of the model
charts.chart_read: Left to right is depth inside the model, from early to late layers; height is how reliably the detector reads correctness there. Each line is one condition; higher is better, and the no-thinking line sits lowest throughout.
charts.chart_takeaway: The scrambled-thinking lines run at or above the real-thinking lines at nearly every depth, while no-thinking stays pinned to the bottom.

charts.chart_plain_title: Spotting hidden failures among answers that pass the quick check
charts.chart_read: Five conditions along the bottom; height is how well the detector flags answers that pass a quick surface test but secretly fail the full test. Higher is better, and no-thinking sits near a coin flip.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only after the model thinks does the bar clear coin-flip level, and even then the signal is too weak to rely on by itself.

qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.verdict_tag: Outside search plus practice crosses the wall, weakly
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.concept_primer: These puzzles are solved by chaining small transformations in a row, like a recipe with nested sub-steps. Each extra link makes the chain much harder; three links deep is a wall this model cannot reach on its own.
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.plain_question: If we feed the model worked-out answers to hard three-step problems, can it then solve brand-new three-step problems by itself?
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.plain_answer: Partly. Studying worked answers let it solve about 13% of new three-step problems, up from zero when it had to teach itself — a real gain. But this only shows up when it thinks things through, not in a quick single answer.
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.why_it_matters: To push a model past its natural ceiling, use an outside search to find answers it can't reach, then train on them. Expect each harder level to stick less well, stay locked behind slow thinking, and need its own fresh seeding.

key_numbers.label: New three-step problems solved (thinking)
key_numbers.sub: was flat zero; now roughly 1 in 8

key_numbers.label: Same problems, quick single answer
key_numbers.sub: the gain vanishes without slow thinking

key_numbers.label: Easier two-step problems solved (thinking)
key_numbers.sub: shallower problems absorb far more strongly

key_numbers.label: Four-step problems
key_numbers.sub: no free jump to the next level

charts.chart_plain_title: Worked answers cross the three-step wall, but only while thinking
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by difficulty (two, three, four steps); height is the share of new problems solved, taller is better. One color is the model before study, one is after study while thinking, one is after study in quick single-answer mode.
charts.chart_takeaway: At three steps the before-study bar sits flat at zero while the after-thinking bar rises to about 13% — a real crossing — yet the quick-answer bar stays at zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: More tries lift three-step success off zero; the untrained model stays flat
charts.chart_read: Left-to-right is how many attempts the model gets; height is the share of new three-step problems solved, higher is better. One line is the trained model, one is untrained, and a third shows easier two-step problems for contrast.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained three-step line climbs to about 13% with more tries while the untrained line stays pinned at zero, and the two-step line climbs roughly three times higher.

qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.verdict_tag: Visible clues let it pick the right answer
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.concept_primer: The model has two answers to each table task: a quick guess and a slower computed one. Like a student deciding whether to trust mental math or their worked-out calculation, something must pick which to submit.
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.plain_question: Can the model reliably decide when to trust its quick guess versus its slower computed answer, using only clues it can actually see?
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.plain_answer: Yes. A simple rule using visible clues picked correctly on 66% of tasks, up from 56% for always trusting the quick guess, matching the best score achievable even with perfect hindsight.
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.why_it_matters: When a model can both guess and compute, a cheap check of whether the computed answer passed a visible test is enough to know which to submit, without ever overwriting an answer that was already right.

charts.chart_read: Each bar is a strategy for choosing which answer to submit; height is the share of test tasks it got exactly right. Taller is better. The rightmost bar is the best-possible score if you knew the answers in advance.
charts.chart_takeaway: The simple clue-based rule bar reaches the same height as the best-possible bar, and clearly beats always-submitting-the-quick-answer, meaning the visible clues alone are enough to pick well.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixes made versus good answers wrecked, per strategy
charts.chart_read: For each strategy, one bar counts tasks it rescued by switching to the computed answer (good) and the other counts tasks where it wrongly overwrote an already-correct quick answer (bad), out of 50. Fewer wrecked is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The simple rule and the trained chooser rescue several tasks while wrecking zero, whereas the two control strategies wreck 10 to 21, showing the clue-based approach only switches when it helps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does the chooser actually learn from real clues or just noise
charts.chart_read: Two lines track training error as the chooser learns: one trained on real answers, one on deliberately scrambled answers. Lower means it is learning. The scrambled line is the sanity check that should stay high.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real-answer line drops to nearly zero while the scrambled line stays stuck high, confirming there is a genuine signal in the clues rather than accidental memorization.

qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.verdict_tag: Training the retry step made it worse
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.concept_primer: Give a coding model its own failed program back and ask it to fix the bug — a "retry" step. We tested whether training that retry step beats an untrained one, and whether either beats just trying fresh a few more times.
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.plain_question: If the model spends extra effort fixing its broken code, does training that fix-it step solve more problems than just trying again?
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.plain_answer: No. For the same extra effort, simply generating a few more fresh attempts solved the most previously-unsolved problems (5 of 57), beating both the untrained retry (3) and the trained retry (2); training the retry step actually made it slightly worse.
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.why_it_matters: When you have spare compute, spend it on more varied fresh attempts rather than on training a dedicated fix-it step — and watch for fixes that pass the visible checks but still fail the hidden ones.

key_numbers.label: Hard problems solved by just trying more

key_numbers.sub: base attempts vs. best use of extra effort

key_numbers.label: Fixes that looked right but were wrong

charts.chart_plain_title: Previously-unsolved problems rescued, by extra-effort strategy

charts.chart_takeaway: The 'try more' bar is tallest and the trained-retry bar is shortest, so training the fix-it step recovered fewer problems than doing nothing special.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of all problems solved, by strategy
charts.chart_read: Bars show the percentage of 150 coding tasks solved (taller is better): base attempts, untrained retry, trained retry, and just trying more. The last three each add the same extra effort.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every extra-effort bar edges above the base, but 'try more' is the tallest, so it made the best use of the added budget.

charts.chart_takeaway: The trained-retry bar is taller, meaning training made the model more likely to produce fixes that only look correct.

qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.verdict_tag: Light tuning picks every right recipe
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.concept_primer: Think of a small library of data-cleanup recipes. For each task the model is handed a menu of candidate recipes and must pick the one whose output is correct; a machine then runs the chosen recipe exactly.
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.plain_question: Can a little tuning teach the model to pick the correct data-transformation recipe from a menu of candidates?
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.plain_answer: Yes. After a light round of tuning the model produced the correct output on 100% of the 48 tasks, up from 92% untuned and matching a perfect answer key. Simple example-matching rules already reached about 90%, so the menu was fairly easy.
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.why_it_matters: When answers can be run and checked, a small amount of tuning can push a fixed model to reliably choose the exact right procedure. But easy candidate menus flatter the score, so retest on less-templated tasks.

charts.chart_read: Each bar is a way to choose the recipe; bar height is the share of 48 tasks whose output came out correct, taller is better. The rightmost bar is a perfect answer key; the simple example-matching rules sit in the middle.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tuned-model bar rises to the same full height as the perfect answer key, while the untuned model's bar stops short.

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct outputs on single-step versus multi-step tasks
charts.chart_read: Each approach shows two bars: one for single-step tasks and one for tasks needing several chained steps; height is the share solved correctly, taller is better. The tuned model reaches full height on both.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the tuned model tops out on both bar colors; the untuned model's multi-step bar dips below its single-step one.

charts.chart_takeaway: The tuned model's bar towers to full height while every other bar sits near or below half.

qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.verdict_tag: Fill-in-the-blank templates beat writing whole fixes
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.concept_primer: Instead of writing a whole program fix in one go, the model writes a fill-in-the-blank template, and a separate checker tries every valid way to fill the blanks, keeping fills that pass the visible tests.
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.plain_question: Does letting the model sketch a fill-in-the-blank template, then have a checker complete it, fix broken programs better than writing the whole fix directly?
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.plain_answer: Yes. On the hardest problems, having the model write a fill-in-the-blank template and letting a checker try valid fills raised the pass rate from 33% to 88%, far beyond the model writing the whole fix itself.
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.why_it_matters: When a small model struggles to write deep, correct code in one shot, have it sketch the overall structure and let an automated checker fill and test the fiddly details.

charts.chart_plain_title: Passing unseen tests: writing the whole fix vs filling a template
charts.chart_read: Bars group by difficulty tier (easy, medium, hardest); height is the share of unseen tests passed, taller is better. Each tier shows writing the whole fix directly, filling a template, the two combined, and the best-possible pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the hardest tier the template bars tower over the short direct bar, and the combined approach reaches 88% versus 33% for writing the fix directly.

charts.chart_plain_title: Hardest problem types solved: writing the fix directly vs filling a template
charts.chart_read: One row of bars per problem type, twelve problems each; height is how many were solved, taller is better. Two bars per type compare writing the whole fix directly against filling in a template.
charts.chart_takeaway: The template bar clears whole problem types the direct bar misses entirely, though direct still edges ahead on a couple of text-matching types.

qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.verdict_tag: Even practice beat targeting weak spots
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.concept_primer: Picture a fixed study budget of practice problems. You can spread them evenly across every topic, or cram only the topics you currently flunk. This experiment tests which spending plan trains the model better.
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.plain_question: When you can only add a fixed batch of extra practice problems, is it better to target the model's weak spots than to spread practice evenly?
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.plain_answer: No. Spreading the extra practice evenly across all task types worked best, solving 98% of unseen tasks, while concentrating practice on the model's current mistakes reached only about 85% and left some task types unsolved.
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.why_it_matters: When your training budget is fixed and the task space is broad, spread practice evenly rather than chasing the model's current mistakes. Targeting fixes a few weak spots but quietly starves others.

key_numbers.label: Evenly-spread practice
key_numbers.sub: unseen tasks solved, versus no extra practice

key_numbers.label: Cramming the mistakes
key_numbers.sub: best either targeting method reached

key_numbers.label: Worst blind spot from targeting

charts.chart_plain_title: Which practice-example strategy fixes the most broken programs
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by four training recipes (left to right: no extra practice, evenly-spread practice, two ways of cramming the model's own mistakes). Height is the share of tasks it gets fully right on unseen inputs; taller is better. Two bars per group: one single try versus the best of three tries.
charts.chart_takeaway: The evenly-spread bar towers near the top while both cram-the-mistakes bars sit well below it, so spreading practice broadly beat targeting weak spots.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much the shown worked-out steps actually help
charts.chart_read: Three bars for the same trained model, differing only in what worked-out solution steps it is shown alongside the broken program: correct steps, no steps, or deliberately jumbled steps. Height is the share of tasks solved correctly; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy falls sharply when the steps are removed and collapses when they are jumbled, showing the model genuinely reads and relies on them rather than ignoring them.

charts.chart_plain_title: On the hardest task types, targeted practice fixes some but abandons others
charts.chart_read: Five hardest task types along the bottom, with four colored bars each for the training recipes (no extra practice, evenly-spread, and two cram-the-mistakes versions). Height is share solved on unseen inputs; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The evenly-spread bars stay tall across every type, while the cram-the-mistakes bars swing to zero on at least one type, exposing the blind spots targeting creates.

qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.verdict_tag: Matched past solutions recover missed problems
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.concept_primer: The model usually cracks a coding task by trying many times. For the ones it keeps missing, we hand it code from a similar problem it already solved, like adapting a proven recipe for a new dish.
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.plain_question: When the model keeps failing certain coding problems, does handing it proven solutions to similar problems help it finally solve them?
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.plain_answer: Yes. Giving the model a closely-matched past solution to adapt cracked 3 of the 8 problems that repeated tries kept missing, lifting overall success from 67% to 79%; unrelated solutions helped none.
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.why_it_matters: Relevant past solutions are a cheap boost for stubborn problems, but the model often looks right on the shown examples while failing hidden ones, so add a checker before trusting an answer.

key_numbers.label: Problems solved overall
key_numbers.sub: with a matched past solution to adapt

key_numbers.label: Stuck problems newly cracked

key_numbers.label: Looked right but were wrong
