charts.chart_plain_title: Share of problems solved: trying more alone vs adding matched solutions
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a different way of supplying candidate solutions; height is the share of 24 problems solved, so taller is better. The leftmost bar is trying more alone; the others add various retrieved solutions.
charts.chart_takeaway: The matched-solution bar rises highest, from 67% to 79%, while the unrelated-solution bar stays flat at 67%, so the content of the retrieved solution matters.

charts.chart_plain_title: Stuck problems newly cracked by each type of retrieved solution
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one type of retrieved solution; height is how many of the 8 problems that trying-more kept missing were newly solved, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Matched solutions crack 3 stuck problems versus 0 for unrelated ones, showing the match, not just more attempts, does the work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Answers that looked right on shown examples but failed hidden checks
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one type of retrieved solution; height is the share of answers that passed the shown examples yet still failed the hidden checks, so lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar is tall, from 57% up to 100%, meaning passing the shown examples alone is not safe enough to trust an answer.

qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.verdict_tag: Small verified tweaks recover real capability
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.concept_primer: Think of a spell-checker for tiny programs: after the model writes a fix, a helper proposes small nearby edits, runs each against a few example tests, and keeps the one that passes.
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.plain_question: On the hardest brand-new repair tasks, does trying small verified edits to the model's fix beat just picking its best raw attempt?
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.plain_answer: Yes. On the toughest new tasks, adding small tested edits lifted fully-correct repairs from about 39% to about 52%, close to the 58% best-possible limit of that edit set, though not a step-change on its own.
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.why_it_matters: When a small model writes an almost-right program, cheaply testing nearby edits recovers real wins. But keep edits conservative and expect gains only where the right fix is genuinely close by.

charts.chart_read: Each bar is one repair method; taller means more of the 120 hardest new tasks fully fixed. Left bars are the model's own picks, middle bars add small tested edits, and the rightmost bar is the best-possible pick within that edit set.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tested-edit bars jump well above the model's own picks and nearly reach the best-possible bar, showing the edits recover most of the reachable gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task types the tested edits help, and which stay stuck
charts.chart_read: Each group is a task type; the three bars show the model's own picks, added tested edits, and the best-possible pick, counting tasks fully fixed out of 12. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Some task types leap from zero to nine once edits are added, but two families stay at zero even at best-possible, meaning the edit set simply lacks the needed fix there.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trade-off between tasks helped and tasks harmed by edits
charts.chart_read: Each group is an acceptance rule; the taller bar counts tasks improved and the shorter bar counts tasks made worse, out of 120. More improved and fewer harmed is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The strictest rule, accepting an edit only when it passes every example test, roughly halves the harmed tasks while keeping most of the improvements.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.verdict_tag: The cache-fit 49k rung failed; implicit-capture 61k stopped pre-receipt
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.concept_primer: Long-context batching needs two limits to agree: how long each sequence may become and how many such sequences the scheduler may keep active. A generous per-sequence allowance can still thrash if their combined block-rounded KV-cache demand exceeds the live cache.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.plain_question: Does fresh verified-macro induction terminate cleanly when vLLM concurrency is fitted to the live KV cache instead of retaining max-seqs 64 from a shorter-context setup?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.plain_answer: The fresh base-only K=4 probe at think@49,152 fit its live KV cache but still failed the content-blind termination gate. All 48 samples contacted the reasoning boundary: token-ID periodicity found 37 loops, 11 contacts remained unresolved, and 9 answers reached their limit. No decoded or scored content was inspected. The 61k attempt was stopped before a receipt after an audit found that vLLM's implicit sparse graph list covered only through width 8 rather than max-seqs 15; it left no reusable rows. Long-rung escalation moved to a separate exact-capture experiment.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.why_it_matters: This separates cache validity from graph coverage, throughput, and the research hypothesis. Max-seqs 19 fit the cache, yet the run was 19.6% slower than the predecessor's overcommitted max-seqs-64 diagnostic; the implicit capture lists also missed active widths 17-19 and 9-15. Cache-safe concurrency is necessary but not automatically throughput-optimal, and a failed termination probe remains a provisioning boundary rather than evidence against verified macros.

key_numbers.label: 49k termination
key_numbers.sub: all 48 contacted the boundary; 9 answers hit 512

key_numbers.label: Capacity-fit demand
key_numbers.sub: of 997,888 live tokens; 34,816 headroom

key_numbers.label: Capacity-fit throughput
key_numbers.sub: 19.6% below the old max-seqs-64 diagnostic

key_numbers.label: 61k implicit-capture attempt
key_numbers.sub: resolved graph maximum 8 vs active width 15; no reusable rows

charts.chart_plain_title: Capacity fitting moves worst-case demand below live KV capacity
charts.chart_read: For each scheduler setup, the dark bar is block-rounded worst-case cache demand and the comparison bar is measured live cache capacity. Demand above capacity implies eviction and recomputation risk; demand below capacity has explicit headroom.
charts.chart_takeaway: The predecessor could admit 48 long sequences demanding 2.43M cache tokens from about 995k. The completed 49k rerun capped concurrency at 19 and fit 963,072 tokens into 997,888, validating its geometry even though its termination gate failed.

charts.chart_plain_title: Capacity fitting did not clear the 49k termination boundary
charts.chart_read: Bars show the shares of the 48 fresh K=4 samples classified as unresolved boundary contacts, exact periodic loops, and answer-limit contacts. Each is compared with its registered maximum.
charts.chart_takeaway: The cache-fit run still failed every termination threshold. Its 61k successor stopped before a receipt after the implicit graph geometry proved incomplete; no decoded or scored content was inspected.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.verdict_tag: Exact runtime gates passed; the terminal context ladder still failed
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.concept_primer: CUDA graphs accelerate repeated decode batch shapes, but asking vLLM for a maximum does not guarantee its implicit sparse capture list includes that active width. This follow-up freezes and verifies the exact graph-size list alongside live KV-cache capacity.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.plain_question: Does explicitly capturing every registered active batch width repair the long-context vLLM envelope enough for fresh verified-macro induction to clear its content-blind termination gate?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.plain_answer: Exact graph and cache resolution worked at both long rungs, but neither fresh K=4 probe terminated adequately. At 49k, all 48 samples reached the boundary: 38 exact loops, 10 unresolved contacts, and 6 answer-limit contacts at 491.396 sampled tokens/s. At terminal 61k, the counts were 40, 8, and 4 at 397.688 sampled tokens/s despite FULL graph coverage and 47,488 cache tokens of headroom. The selector ended pass=false with no selected budget. No K=12 arm, semantic analysis, or macro claim was authorized, and no decoded or scored content was inspected.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.why_it_matters: Binding live cache geometry and the resolved compilation configuration repaired the inference envelope but isolated a genuine termination problem: more context shifted a small number of unresolved and answer contacts into exact loops without clearing any registered gate, while throughput fell. The next study should stop increasing context and preregister one symmetric loop-control mechanism while preserving the unresolved-contact and answer-limit gates.

key_numbers.label: Exact-capture 49k termination
key_numbers.sub: all 48 contacted the boundary; 6 answers hit 512

key_numbers.label: Exact graph resolution
key_numbers.sub: requested and resolved lists match; active width 19 covered

key_numbers.label: Exact-capture throughput
key_numbers.sub: 2,363,163 tokens in 4,809.081 seconds; descriptive +4.16%

key_numbers.label: Terminal exact-capture 61k
key_numbers.sub: 4 answers hit 512; 397.688 tokens/s; pass=false

charts.chart_plain_title: Explicit graph lists cover every registered active width
charts.chart_read: Each group compares the scheduler's active batch width with vLLM's largest resolved full-decode graph. The implicit configurations stop short; the explicit configurations match exactly. Both exact groups are completed fresh probes.
charts.chart_takeaway: The follow-up repaired the compilation-envelope defect at both long rungs, so the terminal failure cannot be attributed to missing active-width graph coverage.

charts.chart_plain_title: More context increased loops without clearing termination
charts.chart_read: For each completed fresh K=4 probe, bars show the shares of 48 samples classified as unresolved boundary contacts, exact periodic loops, and answer-limit contacts. The registered maxima are 5%, 25%, and 5% respectively.
charts.chart_takeaway: The 61k probe slightly reduced unresolved and answer-limit contacts but increased the already-dominant loop share. The terminal selector therefore remained pass=false with no selected budget.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.concept_primer: Imagine replacing a repeated two-step instruction with a short nickname. Before asking whether those nicknames help solve new puzzles, we first handed the model the full correct instruction list and checked whether it could rewrite that list faithfully.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.plain_question: Can the model reliably rewrite a supplied five-step plan using exact named shortcuts?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.plain_answer: This original run could not answer. Its reasoning-enabled attempts hit their token limits, while turning reasoning off fixed formatting but changed the computation being tested. A dedicated long-context follow-up later rewrote all 16 separate supplied plans exactly at least once, showing that this stop was a setup limit rather than a durable shortcut-interface failure.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.why_it_matters: A well-formatted tool call is not necessarily correct, but a token-capped one is not clean negative evidence either. Test a new action language on known plans, provision the real workload, and separate resource exhaustion from semantic failure before judging harder tasks.

charts.chart_takeaway: Neither original attempt reached the pass line, so this run correctly stopped; a later long-context follow-up showed the budgeted interface itself was recoverable.

charts.chart_takeaway: Perfect formatting did not make those no-think rewrites faithful, but the condition was not equivalent to adequately budgeted reasoning.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.verdict_tag: The shortcut interface works; the terminal context ladder selected nothing
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.concept_primer: A verified macro is a short nickname for an exact multi-step action. This study first separates two questions: can the model write the right nickname when the full plan is supplied, and does having those nicknames help it discover a plan for a new puzzle?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.plain_question: Once the model has enough room to reason, can exact reusable shortcuts improve new-puzzle accuracy beyond spending the same generation budget on more ordinary attempts?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.plain_answer: We still cannot answer the new-puzzle question. The corrected supplied-plan check passed on all 16 records, but fresh induction reached the reasoning boundary at every registered allowance. The terminal exact-capture 61k K=4 probe passed FULL-graph and live-KV gates yet still produced 40 exact loops, 8 unresolved contacts, and 4 answer-limit contacts among 48 samples. The selector ended pass=false with no selected budget. No K=12 matrix, semantic analysis, or macro claim was authorized, and no decoded or scored content was inspected.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.why_it_matters: Reaching a generation limit is missing evidence, and increasing the token cap without fitting scheduler concurrency can add recomputation rather than usable reasoning. This ladder now also shows that fitting cache and graph geometry does not guarantee useful termination. Stop increasing context, gate termination before reading accuracy, and test any loop intervention symmetrically in a fresh preregistered experiment while retaining the unresolved-contact and answer-limit gates.

charts.chart_read: Each group is a base-only termination run. Bars show the shares of samples left unresolved, classified as exact periodic loops, and reaching the answer limit; lower is better. The 16k and 32k groups have 144 samples each, while the diagnostic 49k probe has 48.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every sample at every allowance reached the reasoning boundary. The 49k max-seqs-64 probe had already been made diagnostic-only because its scheduler geometry overcommitted KV cache, so none of these rows is a macro-quality result.

charts.chart_read: The dark bar counts supplied plans rewritten exactly at least once across four attempts; the comparison bar is the pre-set pass requirement.

qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.verdict_tag: Confidence can judge, but cannot feed the training loop
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.concept_primer: Earlier work built a self-training loop: the model solves practice tasks, a code-runner checks which solutions are right, and the model is fine-tuned on its own verified wins. Separately, the model can score its own answers by reading the probability of a one-token 'this is correct' verdict. This experiment asks whether that self-score can replace the code-runner inside the training loop, which would let self-training work in domains where nothing can be executed.
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.plain_question: Can the model's own confidence score replace the code-runner that filters its practice solutions before fine-tuning?
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.plain_answer: No for training, yes for judging. First, the instant one-token score cannot even tell right from wrong solutions here unless the model thinks step-by-step before scoring (instant: coin-flip; with thinking: strong). Second, even with the strong thinking-based filter, fine-tuning on confidence-picked solutions helped exactly as little as fine-tuning on unfiltered ones -- only the code-runner-verified set taught anything, and even its gain was small because 70 examples is simply too few. The good news: the judge itself is not damaged by self-training -- a model fine-tuned on its own approved answers still ranks right above wrong just as well, though its raw scores drift upward on its own mistakes.
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.why_it_matters: It locates exactly where a real verifier is irreplaceable: filtering training data. For picking answers at inference time, the model's own (thinking-based) score works and even survives repeated self-training if you keep the top-ranked answers rather than everything above a fixed score cutoff, which silently rots as scores inflate.

key_numbers.label: instant vs thinking self-score, telling right from wrong within a task (AUROC)
depth-2 single-shot after fine-tuning: verified vs confidence-picked vs unfiltered data
judge quality after self-training on its own approvals (fixed candidates)
score inflation on its own wrong answers after self-training

qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.verdict_tag: Self-teaching did not improve new-problem coding
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.concept_primer: Think of a student who tries to get smarter by re-studying only the practice problems they already solved correctly, with a checker confirming each solution actually runs.
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.plain_question: Can this coding model get better at unseen problems by practicing on its own verified-correct solutions?
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.plain_answer: No. Re-studying its own correct solutions did not help the model on fresh problems; it landed at 65%, essentially unchanged, while simply having it try more times reached 69%.
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.why_it_matters: Do not spend effort retraining this model on its own successes to widen its coding reach. Simply letting it make more attempts and picking a working one is the more reliable lever.

charts.chart_takeaway: In every pair the original-model bar is at least as tall, so switching the picking rule cannot rescue the self-taught model.

charts.chart_plain_title: A tiny 20-problem preview looked promising but reversed at full scale

qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.verdict_tag: Free self-check ties the costly one
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.concept_primer: The model writes eight rough drafts of a program, then a "selector" is the rule that picks which single draft to hand in. Different rules cost different amounts of effort.
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.plain_question: When the model writes eight code attempts, what is the cheapest reliable rule for picking the one most likely to actually be correct?
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.plain_answer: A quick built-in test plus the model's own free gut-check picks a correct attempt 87% of the time, matching a checker that costs about five times more and beating a random pick's 77%.
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.why_it_matters: When your problem comes with a quick built-in test, run it and add the model's free snap judgment to catch fakes. Paying for the model to deliberate at length buys nothing extra here.

key_numbers.label: Right attempt picked
key_numbers.sub: random single pick vs the best cheap rule

key_numbers.label: Cost of the deliberate checker
key_numbers.sub: for no extra accuracy

key_numbers.label: Fakes that slip past the quick test
key_numbers.sub: the small gap a self-check can still recover

charts.chart_plain_title: Which rule picks the right code attempt?
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a rule for choosing one of eight attempts; height is how often the picked attempt actually passes every test, so taller is better. Bars climb from a random pick on the left to a perfect picker on the right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The free built-in-test-plus-gut-check bar reaches the same height as the far costlier deliberate-checker bar, nearly matching the perfect picker on the right.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much effort each picking rule costs
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a picking rule; height is the estimated effort per task, so shorter is better. The two rules that make the model deliberate at length tower over the rest.
charts.chart_takeaway: The deliberate-checker bars stand about five times taller than the free combo, spending far more effort while landing at the same accuracy.

charts.chart_plain_title: How well each self-check spots sneaky wrong attempts
charts.chart_read: Two bars for two ways the model judges its own drafts, deliberate versus quick; height is how well it sorts genuine passes above attempts that fake a pass, higher is better, with a coin-flip sitting at 50%.
charts.chart_takeaway: The quick judge's bar (70%) stands almost as tall as the deliberate one (76%), so the free check sorts real from fake nearly as well.

qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.verdict_tag: Practicing shallow chains doesn't unlock deeper ones
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.concept_primer: Each task is solved by chaining small steps: an easy one needs two steps, a harder one needs three. The question is whether getting fluent at two-step tasks carries over to three-step ones.
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.plain_question: If we drill the model on its own correct two-step solutions, does it start solving three-step tasks it never could before?
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.plain_answer: No. Drilling the model on its own two-step solutions tripled its two-step success rate, from 12% to 36%, but produced zero three-step solutions — it never learned to reach one step deeper.
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.why_it_matters: To extend a model to harder tasks, first find deeper solutions with a search tool, then drill on those. Self-practice only sharpens skills the model can already do, never adds new depth.

key_numbers.label: Two-step tasks solved
key_numbers.sub: tripled after self-practice

key_numbers.label: Three-step tasks solved
key_numbers.sub: no change, none unlocked

key_numbers.label: Four-step tasks solved
key_numbers.sub: unchanged, no leap

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-practice tripled two-step success but unlocked no three-step solutions
charts.chart_read: Three task groups: two-, three-, and four-step problems. For each, one bar is the original model and one is the self-practiced model; taller means more new tasks solved. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-practiced bar towers over the original only for two-step tasks; the three- and four-step bars stay flat at zero and near-zero, so practice added reach only where the model already had it.

charts.chart_plain_title: More attempts help two-step tasks; three-step stays stuck at zero
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many attempts the model gets; vertical is the fraction of new tasks solved, higher is better. The rising line is the self-practiced model on two-step tasks; the two flat bottom lines are three-step tasks for both models.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two-step line climbs steadily with more attempts while both three-step lines lie flat on zero, showing extra tries never surface a single three-step solution.

qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.verdict_tag: Learning from its own drafts barely helped
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.concept_primer: The model writes tiny programs to solve puzzles, and it can actually run each draft to see what it computes. The idea: let it learn from running its own drafts to pick and reuse the good ones.
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.plain_question: Can the model get better at writing correct little programs by running its own drafts and learning which ones actually work?
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.plain_answer: Partly. Learning to judge its own drafts barely beat the model's gut instinct for picking one, but reusing the best drafts still nudged its direct writing up from 18% to 23% of puzzles solved — far below the 82% it reaches when simply shown correct answers.
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.why_it_matters: Running your own drafts to reuse the good ones gives a small, real lift, but the payoff is capped by how well the model can tell a right draft from a wrong one. Improve that judgment first.

key_numbers.label: Fresh puzzles solved by writing directly
key_numbers.sub: before vs after reusing its own best drafts

key_numbers.label: Picking the right draft
key_numbers.sub: learned judgment ~ gut instinct, vs 37% if it always picked the best draft

key_numbers.label: When shown correct answers instead
key_numbers.sub: the ceiling this approach stayed well under

key_numbers.label: Hard puzzles solved when allowed to search drafts
key_numbers.sub: biggest gain from reusing its own drafts

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving puzzles by writing directly, across training methods
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a group of puzzles; taller bars mean more solved by writing a program directly, with no second tries. Bars compare the frozen starting model, two ways of reusing its own drafts, and a model simply shown correct answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reuse bars sit just above the starting model, while the shown-answers bar towers over all of them, so self-teaching helps only slightly.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving puzzles when allowed to try several drafts
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a group of puzzles; taller bars mean more solved when the model is allowed to generate and test several drafts. Bars compare the starting model against two ways of reusing its own drafts.
charts.chart_takeaway: Reusing the best drafts gives the clearest lift here, with hard puzzles rising from 35% to 52% solved.

charts.chart_plain_title: How well the model picks its best draft
charts.chart_read: Taller bars mean the chosen draft is more often correct, for practice puzzles and fresh ones. Bars compare gut instinct, learned judgment, and the score if it always grabbed its best available draft.
charts.chart_takeaway: Learned judgment barely rises above gut instinct while the best-possible bar sits far higher, showing weak judgment is the bottleneck.

charts.chart_plain_title: Being pickier about which drafts to reuse
charts.chart_read: Two puzzle groups; taller bars mean more solved by direct writing afterward. Bars compare reusing every draft versus keeping only higher-scoring drafts.
charts.chart_takeaway: Keeping only the pickier drafts raises fresh-puzzle accuracy, showing quality of reused drafts matters more than quantity.

charts.chart_plain_title: Direct puzzle-solving per group, self-teaching vs shown answers
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a puzzle group; taller bars mean more solved by writing directly. Bars compare the starting model, two self-teaching methods, and a model shown correct answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: Self-teaching bars edge only a few points above the start, dwarfed by the shown-answers bars reaching 52-82%.

qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.verdict_tag: Model labels did not build reliable rules
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.concept_primer: Imagine watching someone reformat a few names by hand, then trying to write one recipe that reformats every future name automatically, instead of redoing each one by hand.
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.plain_question: Can the model's yes/no judgments turn a handful of worked examples into one reusable recipe that handles every new case?
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.plain_answer: No. Using the model's judgments to pick a recipe worked on 25% of tasks, barely ahead of the 22.5% you get from deliberately scrambled judgments, and it never rose above the best recipe the tool could build.
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.why_it_matters: The model can reformat individual cases well, but that skill does not compile into one dependable rule. If you need a single recipe that always works, expect to hand-build better recipe options first.

key_numbers.label: Recipe picked using the model's judgments
key_numbers.sub: share of tasks where one recipe handled every new case

key_numbers.label: Recipe picked using scrambled judgments
key_numbers.sub: the same setup with judgments deliberately jumbled — nearly a tie

key_numbers.label: Model answering cases one at a time
key_numbers.sub: same model, but solving each case by hand instead of via a recipe

key_numbers.label: Recipe that worked on every case in a strict check
key_numbers.sub: on a matched set of 40 tasks, versus 37.5% for hand-solving

charts.chart_plain_title: Recipe-picking methods versus solving cases one at a time
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method; height is the share of tasks solved on unseen cases, taller is better. Bars cover the model's judgments, scrambled judgments, plain shortest-recipe picking, the best recipe available, and hand-solving one case each.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model-judgment bar sits level with the scrambled-judgment bar and the best-available ceiling, all far below the tall hand-solving bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting single cases right versus getting a whole task right
charts.chart_read: Three bars on the same 40 tasks; height is exact-match rate, taller is better. Left: model solving individual cases; middle: model getting every case in a task right; right: one recipe getting every case right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tall single-case bar collapses when every case must be right, and the recipe bar collapses furthest, showing consistency is the weak point.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where recipe-building breaks down across all tasks
charts.chart_read: Three bars, height is share of tasks, taller is better. Left: tasks where some recipe fits the shown examples; middle: tasks where a fitting recipe also handles unseen cases; right: picking the shortest fitting recipe.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every task has a recipe fitting the examples, yet only about a third of those recipes generalize, so fitting examples is easy but generalizing is not.

charts.chart_plain_title: Best-possible recipe coverage by kind of task
charts.chart_read: Task kinds along the bottom; paired bars show the best recipe available versus the plain shortest-recipe pick; taller is better. The two largest kinds, date-and-time and numbers, appear first.
charts.chart_takeaway: The date-and-time and number bars are lowest despite being the biggest groups, dragging overall recipe coverage down well below simpler task kinds.

charts.chart_plain_title: Recipe-picking results hold steady across three run sizes
charts.chart_read: Three run sizes along the bottom; grouped bars show each method's share of tasks solved on unseen cases, taller is better. Bars include the model's judgments, scrambled judgments, and hand-solving cases one at a time.
charts.chart_takeaway: Across all three sizes the model-judgment bars stay pinned near the scrambled-judgment and best-available bars, while hand-solving stays far higher.

charts.chart_plain_title: Best-possible recipe coverage shifts with the task sample
charts.chart_read: Three sample sizes along the bottom; paired bars show the best recipe available versus the plain shortest-recipe pick; taller is better. Only the largest sample gives a stable reading.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-available bar swings widely between small samples, so only the full-size run gives a trustworthy estimate of how far recipes can reach.

qwen_active_example_acquisition.verdict_tag: Smartly chosen extra examples help a little
qwen_active_example_acquisition.concept_primer: Before answering hidden test questions, the model is allowed to request a few extra worked examples. This tests whether deliberately choosing which examples to add beats grabbing random ones.
qwen_active_example_acquisition.plain_question: If the model can request a few extra worked examples before a test, does choosing them deliberately beat picking them at random?
qwen_active_example_acquisition.plain_answer: It depends. Deliberately choosing gave a small lift, solving 70% of tasks versus 67% with no extras, while random or answer-scrambled examples actually did worse, at 63% and 60%.
qwen_active_example_acquisition.why_it_matters: When you add examples to steer a small model, choose them deliberately rather than at random. Targeting the confusing cases helps modestly, but random extras can backfire, especially on formatting-heavy tasks.

key_numbers.label: Tasks fully solved with no extra examples
key_numbers.sub: starting point with the four given examples

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved after a deliberately chosen extra
key_numbers.sub: the small lift from choosing well

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved with random extra examples
key_numbers.sub: picking badly does worse than adding nothing

key_numbers.label: Best result from any pick that was tried
key_numbers.sub: the ceiling if every choice were ideal

charts.chart_plain_title: How each way of picking extra examples solves tasks
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of tasks fully solved on unseen test rows, one bar per way of choosing extra examples; taller is better. Leftmost adds nothing, rightmost is the best pick that was tried.
charts.chart_takeaway: The deliberately chosen bars sit above the no-extras baseline while random and label-scrambled bars fall below it, so how you pick matters more than how many you add.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy as more extra examples are added, by method
charts.chart_read: Each line is a strategy for choosing extra examples; the horizontal axis is how many were added (none, one, or three) and the vertical axis is the share of tasks fully solved; up is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the deliberate-choice line stays above its starting point at both amounts, while the random and diversity lines dip below, showing extra examples help only when chosen well.

charts.chart_plain_title: Date and time tasks: the hardest, largest group
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of the 13 date-and-time tasks fully solved, one bar per method, from no extras through deliberately chosen, random, and scrambled-label extras; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: One deliberately chosen example lifts this hard group the most, while random or scrambled extras push it to the lowest bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting every row right versus getting single rows right
charts.chart_read: Two bars per method: the lighter bar is the share of individual test rows answered correctly, the darker bar is the share of tasks where every row is right; the darker one is stricter and higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The whole-task bars always trail the single-row bars, meaning one wrong row sinks a task; deliberate choosing narrows that gap the most.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task types still have room to improve
charts.chart_read: Two bars per task type: starting accuracy with the four given examples versus the best pick of extras that was tried; each bar is the share of tasks fully solved, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Six of nine task types are already maxed out, so extra examples can only move the date-time and number groups, and one type stays stuck at zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: Number tasks: smart picks help, scrambled labels hurt
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of the six number-formatting tasks fully solved, one bar per method, from no extras through deliberate, diverse, scrambled-label, and best-pick extras; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Deliberate and diversity picks reach the best-achieved level, while examples with scrambled answers drag accuracy below the no-extras start.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do different self-check styles agree on which example to add?
charts.chart_read: Three bars per hard task, one for each style of self-check the model uses to decide which example to request; each bar is the share of that task's rows solved, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three self-check styles mostly agree, but on the hardest tasks one style swings the result by a noticeable chunk in either direction.

qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.verdict_tag: Answering all at once did not help
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.concept_primer: Think of a clerk given a few worked examples, then a stack of similar entries to reformat. We tested whether letting it see the whole stack at once, versus one entry at a time, makes it more consistent.
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.plain_question: Does letting the model reformat all of a task's entries together, instead of one at a time, make it get the whole task right more often?
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.plain_answer: No. Solving one entry at a time got every entry in a task right 50% of the time; doing them all together dropped to 45%. Grouping never rescued a task it had failed, and it broke two that one-at-a-time had solved.
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.why_it_matters: When you need every row of a task correct, keep reformatting entries one at a time. Bundling them into one shared answer adds a new failure mode where a single wrong item spoils the whole task.

key_numbers.label: Whole task fully correct, one at a time
key_numbers.sub: baseline of tasks with every entry right

key_numbers.label: Whole task fully correct, all together
key_numbers.sub: best grouped approach still trails

key_numbers.label: Tasks the grouping rescued
key_numbers.sub: never fixed a task it had failed

key_numbers.label: Tasks the grouping broke
key_numbers.sub: lost tasks the one-at-a-time solved

charts.chart_plain_title: Doing entries together versus one at a time, six ways
charts.chart_read: Bars group six methods along the bottom, from one-at-a-time to ever-larger bundles; taller is better. Darker bars show how often the whole task was fully correct, lighter bars how often an individual entry was correct.
charts.chart_takeaway: The leftmost one-at-a-time bar is the tallest on whole-task correctness, so no bundling method beats simply handling entries separately.

charts.chart_plain_title: Stricter grouping instructions still fall short
charts.chart_read: Four methods along the bottom, taller is better: one-at-a-time versus three ways of grouping entries with tougher instructions. Darker bars are whole-task correctness, lighter bars single-entry correctness.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every grouped bar sits at or below the one-at-a-time bar, so tighter instructions never close the gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: Task-by-task: did grouping help, tie, or hurt?
charts.chart_read: Three bars count how many of the 40 tasks grouping improved, matched, or worsened versus one-at-a-time. Taller wins bar would be good; taller loses bar is bad.
charts.chart_takeaway: The wins bar is empty and the loses bar shows two tasks, so grouping only ever hurt or made no difference.

qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.verdict_tag: Right answers generated but rarely chosen
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.concept_primer: Picture the model as a student who scribbles many candidate answers on scratch paper: the correct one is usually there, but they must circle just one, with no answer key to check against.
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.plain_question: Can a learned scoring model pick the right little program from the model's own guesses without ever peeking at the answer?
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.plain_answer: Mostly no. A learned scorer lifted accuracy only a couple of points over plain search, from 67% to 70% on new tasks, while a version allowed to peek at the answer key reached 82%. The right program is usually generated but rarely chosen.
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.why_it_matters: The bottleneck is picking, not generating. Effort should go into training a stronger judge on complete candidate programs, checked by actually running them, rather than scoring half-finished ones.

key_numbers.label: Learned picker vs plain search
key_numbers.sub: correct answers on new everyday tasks; only a tiny lift

key_numbers.label: If it could peek at the answer key
key_numbers.sub: same tasks when candidates are checked against the answer

key_numbers.label: Correct answer already on the scratch paper
key_numbers.sub: share of tasks where a right program was generated

key_numbers.label: Hardest multi-step tasks
key_numbers.sub: learned picker gives essentially no gain here

charts.chart_plain_title: Learned picker vs peeking at the answer, on two task sets
charts.chart_read: Bars show task accuracy (taller is better) for four ways of choosing a final program, grouped into everyday paired tasks and harder multi-step tasks; the rightmost bar is allowed to check candidates against the answer key.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned-picker bar sits just above plain search, but the answer-checking bar towers far above both, so the learned scorer captures little of the available gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: The correct program is usually generated but not picked
charts.chart_read: For four task sets, the short bar is how often plain search's single top pick is right and the tall bar is how often any generated candidate was right; taller is better, and the gap between them is missed opportunity.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every tall bar sits far above its short partner, meaning a correct program was generated 15-20 points more often than it was actually chosen.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much to trust the learned score before it backfires
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy (higher is better) as the learned score is given more weight when choosing the final program; the flat left region is light weight, the right edge is heavy weight.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines dip at the far right, showing that leaning too hard on the learned score makes choices worse, not better.

charts.chart_plain_title: How cleanly each kind of move-label can be told apart
charts.chart_read: Bars show how reliably a learned score separates good partial moves from bad ones (taller is better) for four training signals, from a simple exact-match label to richer graded scores.
charts.chart_takeaway: The simple exact-match bar is tall and clean while the richer graded-score bars are much shorter, so nuance is exactly what the lightweight scorer struggles to learn.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training examples let the compiler actually learn
charts.chart_read: Lines show how accurately the model turns prompts into runnable programs (higher is better) as training progresses, comparing a small-data pilot against the main run that used four times the examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: The pilot line stays flat near the floor while the main-run line climbs steadily to about 72%, showing data volume, not tuning, unlocked the compiler.

charts.chart_plain_title: Exactly rebuilding the intended program stays low everywhere
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each method reproduces the exact intended program (taller is better) across four task sets, comparing plain search, the best learned picker, and the answer-checking version.
charts.chart_takeaway: All bars stay well below half and collapse to about a third on the hardest set, so exact program recovery is hard even with answer checking.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most moves aren't the textbook move, yet still reach the answer
charts.chart_read: For three data-collection runs, the short bar is the share of partial moves matching the single textbook program and the tall bar is the share that can still reach a correct answer; taller means more workable moves.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recoverable bar towers over the textbook bar in every run, about 72% versus 4% in the main run, so textbook-only supervision misses most good moves.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training longer doesn't sharpen the learned scores
charts.chart_read: Lines track how reliably each of four learned scores separates good moves from bad (higher is better) across eight training rounds; the four lines sit at clearly different heights.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line is essentially flat from the first round onward, so the scorer's quality is capped by its design, not by how long it trains.

qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.verdict_tag: Reading candidate fixes did not help pick them
qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.concept_primer: Picture a broken program and a pile of suggested fixes, each with a receipt of what happened when you ran it. The test: can the model read those receipts and reliably pick the fix that actually works?
qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.plain_question: Can the model pick the fix that works by reading each candidate and its run receipt, instead of just leaving the program alone?
qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.plain_answer: No. A picker that read the fixes and their receipts never beat simply leaving the program alone (both about 44%), even though a working fix was usually in the pile and picking it perfectly would have reached about 90%.
qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.why_it_matters: Letting the model freely swap in these fixes is unsafe: it rewrites almost everything and breaks working code. Until a picker can tell good fixes from bad, keep a fallback that leaves already-correct programs untouched.

key_numbers.label: Fix-picker accuracy
key_numbers.sub: reading the fixes gained nothing over doing nothing

key_numbers.label: If it always picked the right fix
key_numbers.sub: a working fix was usually available

key_numbers.label: Correct programs it broke
key_numbers.sub: when the model edited freely

charts.chart_plain_title: No trained picker beat leaving the program alone
charts.chart_read: Bars show accuracy (taller is better) for each way of choosing a fix. The two left bars, doing nothing and the best trained picker, tie; the two right bars are cheat pickers that already know the right answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best trained picker's bar sits exactly level with doing-nothing, while the freely-editing pickers collapse to near the floor and only the cheat pickers rise far above.

charts.chart_plain_title: A safety fallback stops the damage but adds no gains
charts.chart_read: For each model variant, one bar is accuracy (taller better) and one is how often it broke a working program (shorter better). Left of each pair edits freely; right of each pair uses a fallback that keeps the original when unsure.
charts.chart_takeaway: The free-editing variants show sky-high breakage bars, while the fallback variants zero out the damage but their accuracy only climbs back to the do-nothing level, never past it.

charts.chart_plain_title: A working fix exists in the pile on every test set
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per test set: doing nothing versus two cheat pickers that always grab a correct fix. Taller cheat-picker bars mean more room to improve is available. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On every test set the cheat-picker bars tower over doing-nothing, proving correct fixes are present and the problem is choosing them, not lacking them.

charts.chart_plain_title: Free-editing pickers rewrite almost every program
charts.chart_read: Per approach, one bar is how often it changed the original program and one is how often that change broke a working one. Both lower is safer. Cheat pickers and the fallback variants sit low; free-editing variants sit high.
charts.chart_takeaway: The free-editing bars nearly max out on both measures, meaning they touch almost everything and wreck most working programs, unlike the cheat pickers that edit only where a fix helps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Genuine fix-readers end up no better than scrambled controls
charts.chart_read: Bars show each training approach's final usefulness score, two bars per approach for two repeat runs; higher is better. Simple baselines sit high; the real fix-reading models sit low, near controls fed scrambled or corrupted inputs.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real fix-reading bars land down near the deliberately-broken control bars on both runs, showing they learned nothing the receipts should have provided.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the base program is already right swings by data batch
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show how often the untouched program is already correct, split by data batch and test set. Each colored series is one batch of source problems; higher just means fewer things left to fix.
charts.chart_takeaway: One batch sits near the top on every test set while another drops to zero, so the room for repair is concentrated in particular batches of problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: On unfamiliar problems, the fallback is all that prevents collapse
charts.chart_read: Bars show accuracy on a batch of problems the model never trained on; taller is better. They span doing nothing, cheat picker, and free-editing versus fallback versions of the model.
charts.chart_takeaway: With doing-nothing already near the top, the free-editing bars crash to the floor while the fallback bars climb back near do-nothing but never pass it.

qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.concept_primer: Imagine a proofreader who is handed many candidate fixes for a broken calculation, reads each one's step-by-step work, and picks the best, without ever being shown the answer key.
qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.plain_question: Can a small helper pick the right fix for a faulty calculation just by reading each candidate's step-by-step work, without seeing the answer?
qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.plain_answer: Yes. Reading each candidate's step-by-step work, the helper lifted correctly solved programs from about 30% to 54%, well above the plain compiler and roughly 40% of the way toward the best a picker could possibly do.
qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.why_it_matters: Picking the right candidate, not generating it, is the bottleneck. A helper that reads intermediate work recovers real gains cheaply; comparing two wordings and re-picking jointly adds more when both are available.

key_numbers.label: Solved with no helper
key_numbers.sub: the faulty compiler's own top guess

key_numbers.label: Solved with the helper
key_numbers.sub: picks a better fix by reading the steps

key_numbers.label: Best a picker could reach
key_numbers.sub: if it always chose the right candidate

charts.chart_plain_title: Solved programs: no helper vs. step-reading helper vs. best possible
charts.chart_read: Three test sets along the bottom; for each, taller bars mean more programs solved correctly. Three bars per set: the faulty compiler alone, the step-reading helper, and a best-possible picker as the upper limit.
charts.chart_takeaway: The helper's bar sits about halfway between the low no-helper bar and the high ceiling in every set, lifting solved programs from roughly 29% to 50-56%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Four quality measures on the paired-wording test
charts.chart_read: Four quality measures along the bottom; taller is better. Four bars each: the compiler alone, the helper, the helper plus a joint re-pick across two wordings, and the best-possible picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the 'two wordings agree' measure the helper alone dips below the plain compiler, but adding the joint re-pick lifts that bar back up near the ceiling.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training programs, higher accuracy
charts.chart_read: Left-to-right shows how many programs the helper trained on; higher lines mean more programs solved correctly on fresh tests. Two lines track two different fresh test sets.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines climb steadily from left to right, so giving the helper more training programs keeps improving how many it solves.

qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.verdict_tag: A light dose of step coaching helps
qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.concept_primer: Picture teaching someone a long string of mental math by having them say each running total out loud; a little of this "show your work" coaching during practice can make the final answers more reliable.
qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.plain_question: Does coaching the model to show its running totals — and keeping the best version instead of the last — help it solve long arithmetic chains?
qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. A light amount of running-total coaching, kept on the whole way, beat giving no coaching at all — but heavy coaching that was switched off for the hardest, longest problems did worse than none.
qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.why_it_matters: Add a small, steady amount of running-total coaching rather than a heavy dose you drop later, and always keep the best-scoring version from training, not whatever the run happens to end on.

key_numbers.label: Long chains solved correctly
key_numbers.sub: no coaching vs light coaching kept on throughout

key_numbers.label: Same steps whether worded plainly or reworded
key_numbers.sub: no coaching vs light coaching kept on late

key_numbers.label: Coaching switched fully off for long problems
key_numbers.sub: worst recipe, falls below giving no coaching

charts.chart_plain_title: Light coaching kept on beats no coaching on long chains
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one coaching recipe; one bar shows how often it solves long chains correctly, the other how often plainly worded and reworded versions produce the same running totals. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two 'light coaching kept on' recipes stand tallest, while the 'coaching switched off late' recipe sinks below giving no coaching at all.

charts.chart_plain_title: The best version shows up mid-training, not at the end
charts.chart_read: Each recipe gets a pair of bars: its best-scoring saved version versus its final end-of-training version, measured on solving long chains. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every final-version bar is roughly half its best-saved counterpart, so simply ending training hands you a noticeably worse model.

charts.chart_plain_title: Coaching mainly protects the reworded problems
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by wording — plainly stated, reworded, and both-must-match — with one colored bar per coaching recipe. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Recipes barely differ on plainly worded problems but split widely on reworded ones, where light coaching stays high and 'switched off late' collapses.

qwen_compiler_multiseed_reattribution.concept_primer: Think of re-running the same training three times, each from a different random starting point ("seed"). A recipe is only trustworthy if it works no matter which starting point you happen to draw.
qwen_compiler_multiseed_reattribution.plain_question: When this small model is trained to run 24-step number programs, does it reliably learn — and does one training recipe consistently come out on top?
qwen_compiler_multiseed_reattribution.plain_answer: No. The same recipe swings from total failure to strong success depending only on the random starting point, so no recipe can be crowned a reliable winner. The best average reaches about 43% on plain wording, but that hides runs scoring anywhere from 0% to 81%.
qwen_compiler_multiseed_reattribution.why_it_matters: Never trust a single lucky training run here — judge a recipe only across several random starting points, since one good run can look impressive while a repeat of it collapses to near-zero.

key_numbers.label: Best recipe, plain wording (3-run average)
key_numbers.sub: share of 24-step programs run correctly

key_numbers.label: Same recipe, three random starts
key_numbers.sub: swings from total failure to strong

key_numbers.label: Train-everything-at-once recipe
key_numbers.sub: almost never works on plain wording

key_numbers.label: Early steps right vs whole program right
key_numbers.sub: most steps land; the full program often doesn't

charts.chart_plain_title: Which training recipe runs 24-step programs best, on average
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars: four ways of asking (plain wording, unseen wording, matched pairs, unseen pairs) along the bottom, three training recipes as colored bars, height is share of programs run correctly. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The copy-and-grow recipe is tallest on most groupings, yet even its best plain-wording bar sits below half, while training everything at once barely clears the floor.

charts.chart_plain_title: The same recipe's plain-wording score swings wildly by random start
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by random starting point (three of them), colored by recipe, height is share of plain-wording 24-step programs run correctly. Taller is better; consistent heights within a color would mean a dependable recipe.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best recipe's bar goes from zero at one starting point to 81% at another, so a single run tells you almost nothing about the recipe.

charts.chart_plain_title: The early steps are right; the whole program is where it breaks
charts.chart_read: For each recipe, two bars: how often the program's opening run of steps is correct versus how often the entire program is correct. Height is accuracy; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every recipe gets a long opening run of steps right far more often than the full program, so the failure is finishing consistently, not knowing the individual steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Programs work at length 16, then fall off a cliff at length 24
charts.chart_read: Each run (recipe and starting point) has two bars: accuracy on 16-step programs versus 24-step programs. Height is share run correctly; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Almost every run nails the 16-step programs but crashes on the 24-step ones, showing the trouble is tied to that extra length, not a general inability.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training looks like a clean success even when the final test collapses
charts.chart_read: Lines track, step by step during training, how often the model's intermediate work is right for the staged recipe; one line per random start. Rising toward the top means training is going well.
charts.chart_takeaway: All the lines climb to near-perfect and look interchangeable, yet their final test scores differ hugely, so a smooth training curve gives no warning of a bad outcome.

charts.chart_plain_title: Familiar reworded questions are far easier than the plain ones
charts.chart_read: Each run has two bars: accuracy on the plain question wording versus on reworded versions of the same programs. Height is share run correctly; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most runs handle the reworded questions far better than the plain ones — some near 100% reworded while plain wording is near zero — revealing sensitivity to phrasing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting one wording right rarely means getting both
charts.chart_read: For each run, two bars: how often a single question is answered correctly versus how often both rephrasings of the same program are answered correctly. Height is the rate; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The both-versions bar sits well below the single-question bar in nearly every run, so the model's success on a program usually doesn't hold up when the wording changes.

qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.verdict_tag: Trained picker cannot find the right solution
qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.concept_primer: Picture a multiple-choice test where a correct option is almost always present. The system builds many candidate step-by-step solutions and trains a helper to pick the best one by watching each run, never seeing the answer key.
qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.plain_question: Can a small trained helper pick better step-by-step solutions by watching them run, instead of writing every step out as text?
qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.plain_answer: No. A correct candidate is available for almost every prompt, but the helper adds only a point or two on easy problems and actually does worse than doing nothing on longer or reworded ones. The barrier is picking, not availability.
qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.why_it_matters: When a correct answer is usually reachable but hard to identify, invest in sharper training signals that reward genuinely correct reasoning, not just any candidate that lands on the right final answer.

key_numbers.label: A correct solution is available
key_numbers.sub: share of prompts where a right candidate already exists

key_numbers.label: Easy problems, before to after
key_numbers.sub: picking helper barely improves accuracy

key_numbers.label: Longer problems, before to after
key_numbers.sub: the helper does worse than doing nothing

key_numbers.label: Most of the reachable gain captured
key_numbers.sub: of the room between the system and the best possible pick

charts.chart_plain_title: Picking helper barely beats the plain system, far below the best possible pick
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars, one cluster per test set. In each cluster: the plain system, the picking helper, and the best-possible pick. Taller is better; the best-possible bar towers over the other two everywhere.
charts.chart_takeaway: The helper's bar sits almost on top of the plain system's in every cluster while the best-possible bar stays near the top, so almost all of the reachable improvement is left untouched.

charts.chart_plain_title: On longer problems the helper drops below the plain system
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy as problems get longer (more steps), left to right. One line each for the plain system, the picking helper, and the best-possible pick. Higher is better; the best-possible line stays flat near the top.
charts.chart_takeaway: Past the easy length the helper line dips under the plain-system line, even though the best-possible line stays near-perfect, showing the helper actively hurts on harder problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of available improvement the helper captured, by test set
charts.chart_read: One bar per test set showing how much of the gap between the plain system and the best-possible pick the helper closed. Above zero is good; below zero means it did worse than the plain system.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars reach at most a small positive sliver and turn negative on the paired and longer problems, so the helper captured almost none of the available headroom.

charts.chart_plain_title: Helper helps a little on most task types, hurts yes-or-no ones
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per task type: plain system, picking helper, best-possible pick. Higher is better. The best-possible bar is near the top for every type.
charts.chart_takeaway: The helper nudges four task types up slightly, leaves one flat, and drops the yes-or-no type by about 9 points, where answers collapse to few values.

charts.chart_plain_title: A calculation-scoring shortcut loses to simply trusting the system
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per test set: the plain system, a shortcut that scores candidates by simulating them, and the trained helper. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The shortcut's bar sits below the plain system on every test set except a tie on the hardest reworded one, so scoring by simulation alone underperforms doing nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded questions widen the accuracy gap as problems lengthen
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy as problems get longer, split into plainly worded and reworded versions, for both the plain system and the helper. Higher is better; reworded lines sit lower.
charts.chart_takeaway: Reworded lines fall well below plainly worded ones at every length, and the helper widens rather than narrows that reworded gap on the longer problems.

qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.verdict_tag: Practice on short recipes fixes longer ones
qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.concept_primer: Picture the model as a translator that turns a word problem into a step-by-step recipe a machine then runs. We only care whether the machine gets the right answer from that recipe.
qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.plain_question: Does teaching the translator a few short two- and three-step recipes help it get longer, unseen recipes right?
qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.plain_answer: Yes. Adding a handful of short multi-step examples raised correct answers on much longer unseen problems from 72% to 83% at six steps and 78% to 89% at eight steps, while feeding only single-step examples did not budge.
qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.why_it_matters: When a translator keeps producing valid-looking but wrong instructions on long tasks, mix a little genuine multi-step practice into its training rather than just piling on more single-step examples.

key_numbers.label: Correct on six-step problems
key_numbers.sub: single-step-only training vs. adding short multi-step practice

key_numbers.label: Correct on eight-step problems
key_numbers.sub: same before-and-after on longer unseen problems

key_numbers.label: Wrong-order mistakes on long problems
key_numbers.sub: share of programs with steps in the wrong order

charts.chart_plain_title: Longer unseen problems solved, by what the translator practiced
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of six-step and eight-step problems solved correctly, grouped by training diet; the three bars per group are single-step-only, short two-step mix, and short two-and-three-step mix. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two-and-three-step-practice bar stands clearly tallest in both groups, meaning only the richer practice diet handled longer problems better.

charts.chart_plain_title: How accuracy holds up as recipes get longer
charts.chart_read: Each line tracks one training diet's share of correct answers as problems grow from two to eight steps; higher lines are better. Watch the two-and-three-step-practice line against the single-step-only line.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the richer-practice line stays high and even climbs at eight steps, while the other two sag to about 72% by six steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: What goes wrong on the longest problems
charts.chart_read: Bars split every generated program on the longest problems into exactly correct, wrong step order, and a correct alternate route, grouped by training diet. A taller first group and shorter middle group are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The richer-practice diet has the biggest exactly-correct bar and nearly halves the wrong-order bar, showing it fixed real reasoning, not just neatness.

charts.chart_plain_title: Guided generation versus letting the translator run free
charts.chart_read: For eight-step problems, bars compare three ways of generating the recipe (guided, retry-until-valid, and free) for two training diets; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guided bars are tallest for both diets, and the single best result pairs the richer-practice diet with guided generation at 89%.

qwen_constrained_abi_parser.concept_primer: Picture asking a small AI to turn a plain-English request into a short recipe of steps a computer then runs. A "grammar guardrail" blocks any half-written step that would break the recipe's rules.
qwen_constrained_abi_parser.plain_question: Does forcing the AI to obey the recipe's rules as it writes make its steps actually run correctly more often?
qwen_constrained_abi_parser.plain_answer: Yes. On the hardest requests the guardrail got 75% of recipes running to the right answer versus 60% without it, and it beat both simply re-rolling until valid (63%) and answering with no steps at all (0%).
qwen_constrained_abi_parser.why_it_matters: When a small model writes machine-runnable steps, keeping its output well-formed as it writes buys real accuracy and holds up when requests are reworded. Simply retrying until valid barely helps.

key_numbers.label: Correct on hardest requests
key_numbers.sub: plain writing vs grammar guardrail

key_numbers.label: Just re-rolling until valid
key_numbers.sub: cheap alternative, barely helps

key_numbers.label: When the request is reworded
key_numbers.sub: plain writing vs guardrail

key_numbers.label: Answering with no steps
key_numbers.sub: gets none of them right

charts.chart_plain_title: Hardest requests: grammar guardrail gets the most recipes running right
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a different way to turn the request into steps; height is the share of runs whose steps produced the right final answer. Taller is better. The rightmost bar is the best-possible ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail bar (75%) stands clearly above plain writing (60%) and cheap re-rolling (63%); only the perfect-input ceiling is higher.

charts.chart_plain_title: The guardrail leads at every recipe length
charts.chart_read: Left-to-right is how many steps the recipe needs (longer is harder); height is the share of steps that ran to the right answer. Higher lines are better. Each line is one method of writing the steps.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail line sits on top at every length, staying above plain writing and cheap re-rolling as recipes get longer.

charts.chart_plain_title: When the request is reworded, only the guardrail holds up
charts.chart_read: Left-to-right is recipe length; height is the share running to the right answer, higher is better. Lines show methods when the request is phrased in unfamiliar wording.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail line stays high and flat near 75%, while plain writing drops to about 48% and the parse-first approach collapses below that.

charts.chart_plain_title: The guardrail's trade-off: everything well-formed, but not all correct
charts.chart_read: Three groups: how often the recipe is well-formed, how often it runs to the right answer, and how often well-formed ones are actually right. Two bars each: plain writing versus guardrail. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail forces 100% well-formed and lifts right-answers to 75%, but the last group dips to 75% too, meaning some forced-valid recipes are still wrong.

qwen_context_trace_verifier.verdict_tag: Choosing, not availability, is the bottleneck
qwen_context_trace_verifier.concept_primer: The model writes a short step-by-step recipe to compute each answer. The system generates many alternative recipes and trains a small "picker" to choose the best one, without ever seeing the answer key.
qwen_context_trace_verifier.plain_question: Can a small add-on pick a better recipe by inspecting how each one runs, instead of trusting the model's first draft?
qwen_context_trace_verifier.plain_answer: No. A correct recipe was almost always among the candidates, about 98% of the time, but the picker added only 1 to 2 points on easy prompts and lost ground on harder ones, so the wall is choosing the right recipe, not generating one.
qwen_context_trace_verifier.why_it_matters: The recipes you need usually already exist among the candidates. Effort should go into teaching the picker to tell truly-correct recipes apart from ones that only luck into the right final answer.

key_numbers.label: Easy prompts answered right
key_numbers.sub: picker barely moved the model's own choice

key_numbers.label: Hardest prompts answered right
key_numbers.sub: picker actually made harder prompts worse

key_numbers.label: A correct recipe was available
key_numbers.sub: of prompts had a right candidate to pick

charts.chart_plain_title: Picker barely beats the model, though a correct answer was almost always available
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a prompt type, from easier on the left to harder on the right. Height is the share of prompts answered correctly, so taller is better. Three bars per group: the model's own first choice, the trained picker, and a best-possible bar showing what perfect selection would reach.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-possible bar sits near 98% everywhere while the picker bar hugs the model's own bar, so correct answers exist but the picker rarely selects them.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much of the available room to improve the picker actually captured
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one prompt type. Height shows how much of the available room for improvement the picker captured; the zero line means no change, above zero is better, below zero means it made things worse.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars are only slightly positive on easy prompts and drop below zero on paraphrase-paired and longer prompts, so the picker helps a little then backfires.

charts.chart_plain_title: By task type: small gains everywhere except one, which got worse
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a task type. Height is the share answered correctly, taller is better, with bars for the model's own choice, the trained picker, and best-possible selection.
charts.chart_takeaway: The picker bar edges above the model bar for most tasks but drops sharply on true-or-false tasks, where few possible answers make competing solutions hard to tell apart.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which training signal actually changed the picker's choices
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a different way of teaching the picker what counts as a good solution. Height is the share of mixed prompts answered correctly, taller is better, with the model's own choice next to the trained picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: Four of five training signals leave the picker identical to the model; only rewarding a correct final answer nudged it above, and only slightly.

charts.chart_plain_title: A rule-based scorer trails the model at every solution length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many steps the solution takes, longer to the right. Height is the share answered correctly, taller is better. Lines track a rule-based scorer, the model's own choice, and the trained picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rule-based scorer line sits well below the other two at every length, ruling it out as a standalone way to pick the best solution.

qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.verdict_tag: Self-written recipes never beat direct answers
qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.concept_primer: Faced with a repetitive task, you can either handle each item by hand or write one reusable recipe that does them all. This test asks whether the model's self-written recipes beat handling items one at a time.
qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.plain_question: Can quizzing a model's self-written recipes on tricky made-up cases make it more reliable than answering each item one at a time?
qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.plain_answer: No. Answering each item directly solved 75% of tasks completely, while the best self-written recipe reached only 42% even with perfect recipe-picking. The recipes never beat direct answering on a single task.
qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.why_it_matters: When a model already answers items well one at a time, do not force it to distill a single reusable recipe. The recipes it writes tend to be brittle and drag whole tasks down.

key_numbers.label: Tasks fully solved, answering items one by one
key_numbers.sub: the model handling each item directly

key_numbers.label: Tasks fully solved, best self-written recipe
key_numbers.sub: even with perfect recipe-picking

key_numbers.label: Tasks the recipes improved
key_numbers.sub: none beat direct answering

charts.chart_plain_title: Answering items directly beats every self-written recipe method
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method; height is the share of tasks it got completely right, so taller is better. The leftmost direct, item-by-item bar is the reference; the recipe-based bars sit to its right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The direct bar is among the tallest while every pure-recipe bar falls below it, so even a flawless recipe-picker cannot match direct answering.

charts.chart_plain_title: Smart probe-picking matches random and scrambled controls
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a way of choosing among recipes: clever tricky-case probes, random probes, scrambled labels, and best-possible picking, with height as the share of tasks fully solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All the bars sit at the same height, so the clever probe selector adds nothing over choosing a recipe at random.

charts.chart_plain_title: Recipes reach direct accuracy only by using recipes less
charts.chart_read: For each recipe method, one bar shows the share of tasks fully solved and the other shows how often it actually used a recipe instead of falling back to direct answering; higher solved is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy climbs only as recipe usage drops toward zero, meaning the gains come from abandoning recipes, not from them.

charts.chart_plain_title: No method helped any task; recipes hurt many
charts.chart_read: Each method has two bars: tasks it improved versus tasks it worsened compared with direct answering, out of 24; more improved and fewer worsened is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The improved bars are flat zero for every method while the worsened bars rise as high as nine tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Recipes zero out whole task types that direct answering solves
charts.chart_read: Task types run along the bottom; paired bars compare direct answering against the recipe approach on the share of that type solved completely; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On several task types the recipe bar drops to zero where the direct bar is full height, so recipes erase whole categories.

charts.chart_plain_title: Requiring every row correct widens the gap between methods
charts.chart_read: Each method shows two bars: the share of individual rows correct and the stricter share of whole tasks with every row correct; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The whole-task bars sit well below the per-row bars, and that gap is widest for the recipe and batch methods.

charts.chart_plain_title: On hurt tasks, recipes rarely even fit the examples
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a task where recipes did worse than direct answering; paired bars show how many recipes were written versus how many actually fit the example rows; more fitting is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On seven of the eight tasks the fitting bar is zero, so no workable recipe existed to pick; the failure is in writing them.

charts.chart_plain_title: Probe agreement does not predict whether a recipe works
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one task, labeled solved or failed, with height showing the probe-agreement score of the chosen recipe; a useful signal would be high on solved tasks and low on failed ones.
charts.chart_takeaway: Several failed tasks score full agreement while a solved one scores zero, so the agreement signal cannot tell good recipes from bad.

qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.verdict_tag: Fixed-recipe distillation lost to direct answers
qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.concept_primer: A model can answer a table one row at a time, or you can try to boil its guesses down into a single fixed recipe applied to the whole column at once, like turning scattered hand-copied answers into one reusable rule.
qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.plain_question: Can a model's shaky row-by-row guesses be distilled into one fixed rule that transforms a whole column correctly?
qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.plain_answer: No. Forcing one fixed recipe solved just 20% of tasks completely versus 50% for answering row by row, and its supposedly informed guesses did no better than randomly shuffled ones.
qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.why_it_matters: When a task has no clean rule the model can express, forcing one throws away accuracy. Answer row by row by default, and reserve a single fixed recipe only for tasks where a valid one actually exists.

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved completely
key_numbers.sub: answering row by row versus forcing one fixed recipe

key_numbers.label: Informed clues versus shuffled clues
key_numbers.sub: no difference, so the clues added nothing

key_numbers.label: Tasks with no usable recipe
key_numbers.sub: left the recipe method nothing to select

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks fully solved: direct answers beat fixed recipes
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method; height is the share of tasks solved completely, with every row correct, so taller is better. The left three answer directly; the right three build one fixed recipe.
