charts.chart_takeaway: The three direct bars cluster near 50% while the three recipe bars sit at 20%, and real clues match shuffled ones, so the recipe approach lost 30 points.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting most rows right does not mean solving the whole task
charts.chart_read: For each method, one bar shows the share of individual rows answered correctly and a second shows the share of tasks fully solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Direct methods get about 72% of rows right but only half of tasks fully right, while the recipe method collapses on both to roughly 21% and 20%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most tasks had no usable recipe to choose from
charts.chart_read: Bars count the 40 tasks by how many valid fixed recipes were found: none, at least one, or more than one. A tall left bar means the recipe method had little to work with.
charts.chart_takeaway: 29 of 40 tasks had zero valid recipes, so on most tasks the clues had nothing at all to pick among.

charts.chart_plain_title: Recipe method collapses on the biggest task groups
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per task family show three methods side by side; height is the share of tasks fully solved, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the two largest groups, dates and numbers, the recipe bars fall to near zero while direct answers hold 56% and 30%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Row accuracy across the main task groups
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per task family show three methods side by side; height is the share of individual rows answered correctly, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe method drops to zero on several groups while direct methods stay high; combining guesses nudges dates up but drags numbers down.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where recipes existed, there were often many
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one task, and its height is how many valid fixed recipes were found; only the handful of tasks that had any recipe at all are shown.
charts.chart_takeaway: Counts ranged from about two dozen up to 400, one hitting the cap, but this covered just 11 of 40 tasks while the rest had none.

charts.chart_plain_title: The two times the safeguard fired, it never helped
charts.chart_read: For the only two tasks where the safeguard triggered, paired bars compare direct answers against the gated recipe; height is the share of rows correct, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On both tasks the gate locked in a fixed constant answer; one task got worse and the other stayed at zero.

qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.verdict_tag: Teaching the model to read examples worked
qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.concept_primer: Give the model a few solved examples in the prompt, then a fresh input. The question is whether it truly reads those examples to work out the rule, or just guesses from what looks familiar.
qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.plain_question: Can a short round of extra training teach the model to figure out a task from the examples shown, instead of guessing from what looks familiar?
qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.plain_answer: Yes. After training, the model correctly finished whole tasks 92% of the time on tricky look-alike puzzles, up from 25%, and the skill carried over to real-world formatting tasks, rising from 23% to 57%.
qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.why_it_matters: A brief, cheap training pass can make a small model actually use the examples you give it, rather than falling back on habit. Shuffling or removing those examples then breaks it, confirming it is truly reading them.

key_numbers.label: Whole puzzles solved, look-alike set
key_numbers.sub: before vs after the extra training

key_numbers.label: Whole tasks solved, real-world formats
key_numbers.sub: the skill carried over to fresh tasks

key_numbers.label: After scrambling the examples
key_numbers.sub: performance collapses, proving it reads them

charts.chart_plain_title: Whole-task success after training, and how it collapses without good examples
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of whole tasks fully solved (taller is better) across four setups, for two test sets: tricky look-alike puzzles and real-world formatting tasks. Setups run from the untrained model with examples to the trained model given scrambled or no examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained-with-examples bar towers over the untrained one on both test sets, then drops sharply when examples are scrambled or removed, showing the model genuinely leans on the examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: Same pattern when scoring each single answer instead of whole tasks
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of individual answers correct (taller is better) across the same four setups and the same two test sets. This is a more forgiving score than requiring an entire task right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained-with-examples bars stay highest and collapse again without good examples, matching the whole-task view and ruling out a memorized shortcut.

charts.chart_plain_title: The training settled quickly
charts.chart_read: The line tracks training error (lower is better) as training steps go from 1 to 120 along the bottom. A falling line means the model is learning the task.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line plunges from high to near-zero within about 40 steps and stays flat, so the model learned the skill early and the rest of training added little.

charts.chart_plain_title: More practice examples improved carry-over to real tasks
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare a smaller and a larger practice set across both test sets, showing whole-task success (taller is better) for the untrained model, the trained model with examples, and the trained model with scrambled examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: Going from the smaller to the larger practice set lifts the trained-with-examples bars on real-world tasks while the scrambled bars sink, evidence the model is truly reading the examples.

qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.verdict_tag: Real gains, plain training works just as well
qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.concept_primer: Think of giving a worker a short crash course on invented practice puzzles, each with its answer key, then checking whether they solve real puzzles better by studying a few sample answers you hand them.
qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.plain_question: Does a short extra round of training on made-up practice tasks teach the model to lean on the sample examples in a prompt, and does that carry over to real tasks?
qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.plain_answer: It depends. The extra training roughly tripled accuracy on real tasks and the model clearly leans on the sample examples, but two plain control versions did just as well, so the special twist in the training was not what caused the gain.
qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.why_it_matters: A cheap round of practice-only training reliably sharpens a small model on example-driven tasks, but do not credit a clever training twist for it. A plain version works just as well, so keep the recipe simple.

key_numbers.label: Real-task score: untrained then trained
key_numbers.sub: getting every line of a task right

key_numbers.label: Score with sample examples removed
key_numbers.sub: the trained model needs the examples

key_numbers.label: Special-twist vs plain training
key_numbers.sub: plain training matched the twist

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra training triples real-task accuracy, but every recipe does it
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one training version; height is how often it gets a whole real task right, and taller is better. The leftmost untrained bar is the starting point; the other three are different training recipes.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained bar is about a third the height of the three trained bars, which stand nearly level, so training helps but no recipe stands out.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trained models really do read the sample examples
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by how the sample examples were provided at test time: intact, scrambled, or removed. Each color is a different trained version, and a taller bar means the model still succeeds.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars shrink sharply from intact to scrambled to removed, showing the models truly rely on the examples, except the scrambled-trained version stays tall even when examples are scrambled.

charts.chart_plain_title: Gains from practice tasks carry over to unseen real tasks
charts.chart_read: Two task groups sit side by side: the made-up practice tasks and the real public tasks. Each has an untrained bar and a trained bar, and taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained bar towers over the untrained one on both groups, so the boost from practice tasks transfers to real tasks, not just the practice generator.

charts.chart_plain_title: The benefit shows up in every repeat run
charts.chart_read: For each of three repeat runs, one bar counts real tasks the training improved and another counts tasks it made worse, out of 45. More helped and fewer hurt is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: In every run about 20 tasks improve while only 1 gets worse, so the benefit is consistent rather than luck.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which kinds of real tasks the training fixes
charts.chart_read: Each pair of bars is a type of real task; one bar is the untrained model and the other the trained one. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained bar jumps to full marks on many structured types like codes and file paths, while dates-and-times stay hardest and one address task drops to zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: One recipe shrugs off scrambled examples, hinting at a formatting shortcut
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by training version on the practice tasks; one color uses intact examples, the other scrambled examples. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The scrambled-trained version alone stays near-perfect even with scrambled examples, suggesting the gains may lean on formatting rather than truly reading the examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: Demanding a perfect whole task costs everyone, the beginner most
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by training version; one color scores each line on its own, the other demands every line of a task be right. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Requiring a flawless whole task costs the trained versions about ten points but nearly halves the untrained model's score.

qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.verdict_tag: Judging attempts without answers stays too noisy
qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.concept_primer: The system turns each question into a tiny program it can actually run. This test asks whether it can learn to pick its best attempt just by watching how the attempts run, without ever being shown the correct answer.
qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.plain_question: Can the system learn to choose its best attempt by running the options, without being told which final answer is correct?
qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.plain_answer: Partly. It found a faint real signal and nudged accuracy up slightly on the hardest questions, but its picks stayed too noisy to beat simply trusting the model's first attempt.
qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.why_it_matters: Teaching a model to grade its own attempts without an answer key is appealing but fragile. Filter its picks by confidence, or give it richer clues, before trusting them as training material.

key_numbers.label: Picks that were actually correct
key_numbers.sub: when choosing with no answer key; 100% when given one

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on the hardest questions
key_numbers.sub: small nudge from the new self-picked targets

key_numbers.label: Best pick available in the batch
key_numbers.sub: headroom the picker left untouched

key_numbers.label: Accuracy when given the answer key
key_numbers.sub: shows the system can master the task when told answers

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-picked targets give a small bump but stay far below full training
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by the two hardest question sets. Within each group, they compare the starting system, two ways of choosing training examples, and the answer-key ceiling; taller means more accurate.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-picked bars edge just above the starting system, yet all three huddle near the floor while the answer-key bar towers above them.

charts.chart_plain_title: Letting the picker choose does worse than trusting the first attempt
charts.chart_read: For each question set, three bars: the model's first attempt, the picker's choice, and the best attempt available in the batch. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The picker's bar sits at or below the first-attempt bar everywhere, while the best-available bar stands far higher, so the picker leaves the headroom untouched.

charts.chart_plain_title: Examples the picker chose for training are mostly wrong
charts.chart_read: One bar per way of choosing training examples, showing the share that were actually correct; taller means cleaner training material.
charts.chart_takeaway: The picker-chosen bar reaches only about a tenth as high as the two answer-key methods, which are fully correct by construction.

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-picked targets drop accuracy below the untrained starting point
charts.chart_read: One bar per training stage on the paired question set, showing accuracy when the system is allowed several tries; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-picked bar dips below even the starting bar, while the answer-key ceiling stands far above both.

charts.chart_plain_title: With an answer key the system nearly masters every question set
charts.chart_read: For each question set, four bars: full training with one try or with several tries, and the starting system with one try or several. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-training bars cluster near the top across all sets while the starting bars stay near the floor, showing the task itself is very learnable.

charts.chart_plain_title: Repair training rarely reproduces the exact intended program
charts.chart_read: For each question set, bars for the starting system, the two repair methods, and full training, showing how often the exact target program is recovered; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every repair bar hugs zero while the full-training bar reaches half or more, so repair barely changes the program the system writes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra clues helped the small pilot but the gain vanished at scale
charts.chart_read: For a validation set and the two hardest sets, bars compare a bare pilot, a pilot given extra clues, and the full-size run; taller means the picker chose better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The extra-clue pilot bar roughly doubles the bare pilot, but the full-size run bar slips back toward the bare level on the hardest sets.

qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.verdict_tag: Showing work barely helps, costs far more
qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.concept_primer: Imagine teaching someone a recipe two ways: hand them only the finished dish, or make them write out every step. This tests whether writing the steps teaches the model better.
qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.plain_question: Does making a small model write out its step-by-step work teach it procedures better than just giving it the final answer?
qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.plain_answer: It depends, but mostly no. On familiar problems, writing out steps beat answer-only by only about 2 percentage points while using 3 to 6 times more words, and on genuinely new step combinations every teaching style collapsed together.
qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.why_it_matters: For simple, familiar tasks, teaching a small model to just give the answer is cheaper and nearly as accurate. Verbose step-writing does not buy real generalization to new problem types.

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on familiar problems
key_numbers.sub: answer-only versus best step-writing style

key_numbers.label: Words used to answer
key_numbers.sub: answer-only versus the step-writing styles

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on new step combinations
key_numbers.sub: the best style; all styles fell here

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy of each teaching style across three kinds of test problems
charts.chart_read: Bars show percent correct (taller is better) for five teaching styles, grouped by test type: familiar problems, reworded problems, and brand-new step combinations. The untrained model is the flat zero baseline.
charts.chart_takeaway: On familiar and reworded problems the trained styles cluster high near 85-94%, but all of them crash to roughly a third correct on brand-new step combinations.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many words each teaching style needs to answer
charts.chart_read: Bars show the average number of words the model writes to produce an answer for each output style; shorter is cheaper. Answer-only is far left, the verbose structured style far right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-writing styles use 3 to 6 times more words than answer-only, a large cost for at most a 2-point accuracy gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each style produces a usable answer
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of responses that yield a readable final answer (taller is better), for each style across the three test types. Simple line-based styles versus the verbose structured style.
charts.chart_takeaway: The simple styles always produce a usable answer, while the verbose structured style fails to give one about a quarter of the time.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task types break on brand-new step combinations
charts.chart_read: Bars show percent correct (taller is better) for the step-writing style on new step combinations, broken out by task type: text cleanup, unit conversion, table lookup, and date math.
charts.chart_takeaway: Unit conversion stays fully correct while text cleanup and date math drop to zero, showing failures cluster where a new combination of steps is required.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded-problem accuracy by task type and teaching style
charts.chart_read: Bars show percent correct (taller is better) on reworded problems, grouped by task type, with one bar per teaching style. The verbose structured style is one of the four.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most styles stay above 83% on most task types, but the verbose structured style drops to zero on table lookup and date math, failing whole categories.

qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.verdict_tag: Step-by-step editing helps but falls short
qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.concept_primer: Instead of writing a whole answer at once, the model works like someone fixing a program one small edit at a time in a scratch machine, running it, seeing the result, then editing again.
qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.plain_question: Can teaching the model to solve tasks with small, checkable edits beat having it answer in one shot?
qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.plain_answer: It depends. The trained editing agent beat the model's one-shot answers on four of five task types, including the hardest ones, but stayed far below a flawless reference solver, so it is useful, not a replacement.
qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.why_it_matters: Letting a small model work in small, checkable steps can unlock harder compositional tasks than answering directly, but the payoff caps out unless you also teach it to plan whole edit sequences, not just single moves.

key_numbers.label: Mixed tasks solved
key_numbers.sub: answering directly vs the step-by-step agent

key_numbers.label: Flawless reference solver
key_numbers.sub: ceiling the agent stays far below

key_numbers.label: Task types improved
key_numbers.sub: editing agent beat direct answers

charts.chart_plain_title: Trained editing agent vs direct answers and a flawless solver
charts.chart_read: Bars group each task type by four solvers: an empty-machine baseline, the model answering directly, the trained step-by-step agent, and a flawless reference solver. Taller means more tasks solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained agent's bars top the direct-answer bars on four of five task types, yet sit far under the flawless reference solver.

charts.chart_plain_title: How accuracy changes with the number of editing steps allowed
charts.chart_read: Each line is one task type; the horizontal axis is how many editing steps the agent may take, the vertical axis is how many tasks it solves. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line dips near zero at a few steps then jumps up around eight steps, meaning half-finished edits hurt while enough steps pay off.

charts.chart_plain_title: Three editing skills improve across training stages
charts.chart_read: Bars track three skills across training stages: choosing the right edit, choosing the right value to fill in, and knowing when to stop. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three skills climb across stages, but the fill-in-the-value bars stay shortest throughout, flagging it as the weakest skill.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra practice rounds mostly help the hardest tasks
charts.chart_read: Bars group each task type by training stage: after basic imitation, then two rounds of extra practice on the agent's own attempts. Taller means more tasks solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The extra practice rounds steadily lift the hardest composition tasks from about 19% to 31%, while other task types just shuffle up and down.

charts.chart_plain_title: Quitting too early gets worse with more steps allowed
charts.chart_read: Each line is one task type; the horizontal axis is how many steps the agent may take, the vertical axis is how often it quits before finishing. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All lines rise steeply as steps increase, so the more room it is given, the more often the agent stops early, capping the benefit of longer runs.

charts.chart_plain_title: Two stopping rules trade wins across task types
charts.chart_read: Bars compare two stopping rules for each task type: letting the agent decide when to stop versus a confidence check before it stops. Taller means more tasks solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two rules trade wins across task types, adding a little on some and losing a little on the hardest, so neither is clearly better.

charts.chart_plain_title: Full-scale agent far outperforms early prototypes
charts.chart_read: Bars group each task type by version: two small early prototypes and the full-scale trained agent. Taller means more tasks solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-scale agent's bars tower over both prototypes, and the earliest text-based prototype sits near zero, showing the design changes and extra training were both essential.

qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.verdict_tag: Tie-breaker quiz added no selection power
qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.concept_primer: When the model writes several small programs to solve a whole task, you must pick the best one. This test tries a "tie-breaker quiz": invent tricky inputs where the programs disagree and ask the model which answer is right.
qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.plain_question: Does a tie-breaker quiz help the model pick a better program than just checking the visible examples?
qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.plain_answer: No. The tie-breaker quiz picked programs no better than a random version of itself; both solved 64% of tasks, exactly matching a simpler safety-net rule that skips the quiz entirely. The quiz added no selection power.
qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.why_it_matters: The real win came from caution: use a generated program only when it looks trustworthy, otherwise answer by hand. Skip the elaborate disagreement quiz, which costs effort but changed no decisions.

key_numbers.label: Solved by hand, item by item
key_numbers.sub: plain answering, the baseline to beat

key_numbers.label: Solved with a cautious safety-net gate
key_numbers.sub: use a program only when it looks trustworthy

key_numbers.label: Extra lift from the tie-breaker quiz
key_numbers.sub: quiz gate also 64%, same as a random quiz

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks fully solved by each approach
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of tasks solved perfectly by one approach, from plain by-hand answering, to a raw program, to a safety-net gate and three tie-breaker-quiz versions. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three gate bars sit at exactly the same height, so the tie-breaker quiz matches both the no-quiz and random-quiz gates and adds nothing beyond the simple safety net.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks made better vs made worse than plain answering
charts.chart_read: For each approach, one bar counts tasks it improved over plain answering and another counts tasks it broke, out of 25. More improvements and fewer breakages is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Raw generated programs break six or seven tasks, while the safety-net gates keep the two wins and break none, showing the gate's whole value is caution.

charts.chart_plain_title: Success by task type: plain answering vs the quiz gate
charts.chart_read: Bars pair plain by-hand answering against the tie-breaker-quiz gate for each type of task, such as dates, names, and numbers. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every task type shows two equal bars except numbers, so all of the gate's gain comes from number-formatting tasks and nowhere else.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of individual items answered correctly by approach
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of individual items answered correctly by one approach, counting single items rather than whole tasks. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All gated versions tie near the top while raw programs roughly halve accuracy and scrambled-answer programs collapse to near nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often a program that fits the examples is trusted
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows the share of tasks where a generated program fit the visible examples and was actually used by that approach. Here the bars just describe behavior, not quality.
charts.chart_takeaway: Programs fit the examples on about half of tasks, but the cautious gates only trust them on 40 to 44 percent, and scrambled-answer quizzes trust none.

charts.chart_plain_title: By task type: plain answering vs raw programs
charts.chart_read: Bars pair plain by-hand answering against raw generated programs for each task type, counting individual items. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Raw programs match plain answering on several types but collapse on dates and file paths, which is why they are unsafe to use without the gate.

qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.verdict_tag: Custom warm-up hint did not beat plain prompting
qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.concept_primer: Think of giving the model a short custom warm-up note, tuned from a handful of a task's own examples, before it works fresh items of that same task.
qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.plain_question: Does tuning a small custom warm-up note from each task's examples make the model more consistent on new items of that task?
qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.plain_answer: No. The tuned warm-up note matched plain prompting exactly on fully-solved tasks (both 52.5%) and never beat it, though it did beat a scrambled-example version, showing the tuning itself worked.
qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.why_it_matters: Spending effort to tune a per-task warm-up note buys nothing over just prompting the model directly. For these text-cleanup tasks, skip it and use straightforward prompting.

key_numbers.label: Tasks fully solved, plain vs tuned note
key_numbers.sub: identical; no gain from the tuned warm-up

key_numbers.label: Tasks the note helped vs hurt
key_numbers.sub: out of 40; the other 38 unchanged

key_numbers.label: Single-item accuracy, plain vs tuned
key_numbers.sub: a tiny drift, not a real improvement

key_numbers.label: Tuning difficulty, start to finish
key_numbers.sub: note learned cleanly, so the null is not a training failure

charts.chart_plain_title: How each method does on new items, plain prompting vs tuned note
charts.chart_read: Bars show accuracy for six methods; taller is better. Two measures per method: whole tasks fully solved and individual items correct. The tuned warm-up note sits level with plain row-by-row prompting.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tuned-note bar is the same height as plain prompting on fully-solved tasks, meaning the extra tuning added nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Change in fully-solved tasks versus plain prompting
charts.chart_read: Bars show each method's gap from plain row-by-row prompting on fully-solved tasks; zero is the baseline and bars above zero would mean improvement. Every bar is at or below zero.
charts.chart_takeaway: No bar rises above zero and the tuned note sits exactly at zero, so nothing beat plain prompting.

charts.chart_plain_title: The warm-up note learned cleanly during tuning
charts.chart_read: Lines show tuning difficulty falling over six steps; lower is better. One line uses the task's real examples, the other uses scrambled examples. The real-example line drops much lower.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real-example line falls smoothly to a low value, confirming the note trained properly, so the flat result is not a tuning failure.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many of the 40 tasks each method helped, hurt, or left unchanged
charts.chart_read: Stacked bars count tasks helped, hurt, or unchanged versus plain prompting; more helped and fewer hurt is better. Almost every task is unchanged for the tuned note.
charts.chart_takeaway: For the tuned note, 38 of 40 tasks are unchanged with one helped and one hurt, a wash; the control versions only ever hurt.

charts.chart_plain_title: Change in single-item accuracy versus plain prompting
charts.chart_read: Bars show each method's shift in per-item accuracy from plain prompting; above zero is better. The tuned note and an untrained blank note sit at nearly the same tiny positive height.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained blank note gains as much as the tuned one, meaning any prefix at all, not the tuning, drives the sliver of change.

charts.chart_plain_title: Item accuracy holds up across small and larger test sets
charts.chart_read: Paired bars show per-item accuracy for each method on a 20-task set and a 40-task set; taller is better. The method ordering barely shifts between the two sizes.
charts.chart_takeaway: The ranking stays roughly the same across both test sizes, with the tuned note's edge over plain prompting staying tiny throughout.

qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.verdict_tag: Short-recipe training carries all the way
qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.concept_primer: Think of teaching someone to follow recipes: the model turns an instruction into a short step-by-step program a calculator then runs. We trained it only on short recipes and tested it on much longer ones.
qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.plain_question: If you only teach the model to build short 3-step recipes, can it still build correct 12- and 16-step ones?
qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.plain_answer: Yes. Training on recipes of at most three steps already produced nearly perfect 12- and 16-step programs, jumping from 61% correct with single-step-only training to 100%; adding longer training recipes bought nothing, and one deeper mix even slipped back.
qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.why_it_matters: You can keep the teaching material short and cheap rather than hand-building long example recipes. A little compositional practice generalizes far; piling on ever-longer examples is wasted effort and can even backfire.

key_numbers.label: 16-step tasks solved, single-step training
key_numbers.sub: model taught only one-step recipes

key_numbers.label: 16-step tasks solved, short-recipe training
key_numbers.sub: taught recipes up to three steps

key_numbers.label: 12-step tasks: before to after
key_numbers.sub: single-step vs short-recipe training

key_numbers.label: Deeper training at 16 steps
key_numbers.sub: longest training mix did worse, not better

charts.chart_plain_title: Long-task success by how deep the training recipes went
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of long programs the model got right; the four groups along the bottom are training diets from single-step-only up to eight-step recipes, with separate bars for 12-step and 16-step tests. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The jump happens between single-step and up-to-three-step training, where bars leap to near the top; the two deeper diets are no taller, so extra depth added nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy as the test recipes get longer
charts.chart_read: Each line is one training diet; the horizontal axis is how many steps the test recipe has and the vertical axis is share solved correctly. Higher and flatter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the single-step-trained line droops as tasks lengthen; every line that saw even short recipes stays flat and high out to sixteen steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Letting the model write freely versus adding formatting guardrails
charts.chart_read: On 16-step tasks, paired bars per training diet compare writing freely against writing with output-format guardrails. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail bars tower over the free-writing bars everywhere, showing the correct steps were already known and messy formatting, not reasoning, was the bottleneck.

charts.chart_plain_title: What the long-task answers looked like, right and wrong
charts.chart_read: For long tasks, bars split every answer into exactly right, a valid alternative that still works, wrong step order or choice, and wrong number used. Taller 'correct' bars and shorter error bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Single-step training produced wrong-step-order mistakes about a third of the time, while any short-recipe training shrank that error to a sliver and pushed most answers to exactly right.

qwen_fastweight_hook.verdict_tag: Extra silent thinking steps did not help
qwen_fastweight_hook.concept_primer: Imagine giving the model a private mental scratchpad and letting it loop through extra silent thinking steps before answering, instead of thinking out loud in words. More loops should mean more careful reasoning.
qwen_fastweight_hook.plain_question: Does giving a fixed small model extra silent thinking steps on a private scratchpad make it better at multi-step arithmetic?
qwen_fastweight_hook.plain_answer: No. Extra silent thinking steps did not reliably improve accuracy on multi-step arithmetic quizzes. A few early gains looked promising on small tests but disappeared on larger rechecks, matching what a do-nothing control also produced by chance.
qwen_fastweight_hook.why_it_matters: Small apparent gains from a new add-on can be pure sampling noise. Always recheck promising results on larger tests and against a do-nothing control before believing an internal mechanism actually learned to compute.

key_numbers.label: Random-guess score
key_numbers.sub: five answer choices, so blind guessing lands here

key_numbers.label: No steps vs. extra steps
key_numbers.sub: bigger recheck: extra thinking did not help

key_numbers.label: Small-test promise, then rechecked
key_numbers.sub: the early gain evaporated on more questions

charts.chart_plain_title: More private thinking steps did not raise accuracy
charts.chart_read: Bars show accuracy on two quiz sets (everyday problems and harder longer-chain problems) as the model runs 0 up to 8 extra invisible thinking steps before answering. Taller is better; the leftmost zero-step bar is the reference.
charts.chart_takeaway: The zero-step bar is the tallest on both quiz sets, so adding private thinking steps left accuracy flat or slightly lower rather than climbing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Early improvements vanished on a bigger test
charts.chart_read: Bars compare no extra thinking steps against the best extra-step setting, on two quiz sets each scored first on 100 questions then rechecked on 250. Taller is better; the pairs show whether the early lead survives.
charts.chart_takeaway: The extra-step bars lead on the 100-question checks but fall level or behind on the 250-question rechecks, showing the early edge was just luck of a small sample.

charts.chart_plain_title: The step advantage flickered and a do-nothing control bumped too
charts.chart_read: Lines track how much the best extra-step setting beat zero steps across training, in points, for the real system and for a control that never actually trained the thinking loop. The flat zero line means no advantage; above it is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines wobble above and below zero and the untrained control bumps up just as much, so the occasional gains reflect noise, not real learned computation.

qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.verdict_tag: Smart picker didn't beat plain decoding
qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.concept_primer: Imagine asking the model to fill in a table several times, then having a trained "picker" read all the drafts and choose the one complete table it thinks is fully correct.
qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.plain_question: Can a trained picker choose the fully-correct completed table more often than just taking the model's first answer?
qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.plain_answer: No. The trained picker solved 50% of tasks completely, exactly the same as taking the model's first answer, even though a perfect table was available among the drafts on 62% of tasks.
qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.why_it_matters: Generating extra drafts only helps if you can reliably pick the winner. Here the winning table exists but the picker can't find it, so the bottleneck is selection, not generation.

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved completely, first answer
key_numbers.sub: every row in the table correct

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved completely, trained picker
key_numbers.sub: no improvement over first answer

key_numbers.label: Tasks where a perfect table was available
key_numbers.sub: the ceiling the picker could have reached

key_numbers.label: Winnable tasks the picker captured
key_numbers.sub: perfect table existed but was not chosen

charts.chart_plain_title: Fully-correct tables: each picking method versus the best-possible
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks solved completely (every row right) for each method; taller is better. The leftmost bar is the best-possible table available among drafts; the rest are real selection methods including the trained picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-possible bar stands well above the rest, while the trained picker sits level with plain first-answer decoding, so the extra machinery added nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting single rows right versus getting the whole table right
charts.chart_read: For each method, one bar shows the share of individual rows correct and another the share of tasks where every row is correct; taller is better. The gap between them shows how strict all-rows-correct scoring is.
charts.chart_takeaway: Rows are about 72% correct but the whole-table bars drop to 50% for every method except the best-possible, showing one wrong row sinks the task.

charts.chart_plain_title: Can the picker tell good tables from bad: real versus scrambled labels
charts.chart_read: For each of four data splits, one bar is the picker's ability to rank correct tables above wrong ones and the other is the same picker trained on scrambled labels; higher is better, and around 50% means random guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real-label bars tower over the scrambled ones near 90%, proving the picker ranks tables well yet still fails to solve any extra tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which data types leave room to improve on complete tables
charts.chart_read: For five data types, one bar is first-answer complete-table accuracy and the other is the best-possible complete-table accuracy; taller is better and a gap between them means untapped room.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the Number type shows a real gap (30% versus 70%), meaning almost all the missed opportunity lives in numeric tables.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which data types get more rows right from extra drafts
charts.chart_read: For five data types, one bar is first-answer row accuracy and the other is best-possible row accuracy; taller is better and a gap means extra drafts recovered rows.
charts.chart_takeaway: Number rows jump from about 62% to 89% with better drafts while Address and City rows never move, showing draft variety helps only some types.

charts.chart_plain_title: Five winnable tasks the picker still got wrong
charts.chart_read: For five tasks where a perfect table existed, three bars per task show first-answer, trained-picker, and best-possible row accuracy; the best-possible reaches full accuracy while higher is better for the others.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first-answer and picker bars are identical and short on every task, meaning the picker chose the same imperfect table and captured none of the five wins.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much the mix of correct tables shifts between data splits
charts.chart_read: For four data splits, one bar is the share of correct tables in the training portion and another in the testing portion; the point is how unequal the two are, not which is taller.
charts.chart_takeaway: Test-side correct-table share swings from 7% to 48% across splits, an instability that helps explain why a good ranker still can't reliably pick winners.

qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.verdict_tag: Reward coaching made the controller worse
qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.concept_primer: Picture the model as an apprentice building a tiny program one edit at a time: it first learns by copying finished examples, then gets a round of trial-and-error coaching that rewards correct final answers.
qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.plain_question: Does a round of reward-based coaching sharpen a small model that builds programs step by step, beyond just copying worked examples?
qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.plain_answer: No. Both reward-coaching methods left the model less accurate than plain imitation did. The coaching produced encouraging training signals, but it scrambled the model's sense of which edit to make and when to stop.
qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.why_it_matters: Before letting reward feedback rewrite a step-by-step controller, use it only to score or rank candidate moves. A single coaching round can quietly break the model's timing for editing and stopping.

key_numbers.sub: imitation policy vs after one reward round

charts.chart_plain_title: Reward tuning lowered accuracy for both approaches
charts.chart_read: Each pair of bars is one coaching approach; the left bar is accuracy after learning by imitation, the right bar after one reward-tuning round. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both right-hand bars sit below their left partner, so the tuning round cost accuracy rather than adding any.

charts.chart_plain_title: Best-of-eight accuracy fell after tuning
charts.chart_read: Two coaching approaches, each with a before-tuning and after-tuning bar; height is the share of tasks solved when the model gets eight attempts. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: One approach's after-tuning bar drops all the way to zero, wiping out its earlier best-of-eight wins.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tuning disturbed when the model decides it is finished
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often the model wrongly declares the task done, before versus after tuning, for each approach. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The preference approach's after-bar roughly doubles, a sign the tuning scrambled the model's stopping decisions.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model's sense of when to stop dropped sharply
charts.chart_read: Left group is how often it picks the right edit, right group is how often it correctly decides to stop; three bars each for after imitation and after each tuning round. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The stop-decision bars fall from high to under a third after either tuning round, while edit-picking barely moves.

charts.chart_plain_title: Imitation policy barely beats an empty program
charts.chart_read: For each task type, three bars: leaving the program blank, the imitation-trained model, and an exhaustive search that tries many attempts. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model's bars hug the blank-program bars while exhaustive search towers far above, showing the tasks are solvable.

charts.chart_plain_title: More attempts let exhaustive search solve far more tasks
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many attempts are allowed, vertical is share of tasks solved by exhaustive search, one line per task type. Up and to the right is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line climbs steeply toward most tasks solved, so the ceiling is the model's decisions, not the task itself.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trial-and-error tuning broke edit legality; preference tuning kept edits legal
charts.chart_read: For each task type, three bars: after imitation, after the trial-and-error round, after the preference round; height is the share of edits that are legal moves. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trial-and-error bars roughly halve while the preference bars stay at full height, yet accuracy still fell in both.

charts.chart_plain_title: Per-part training scores stayed near baseline while real accuracy collapsed
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by training stage; each colored bar is how well one part of the model's move choice fits its training targets. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tuning-stage bars stay near the imitation levels, so the deployment collapse was not a training-fit failure.

qwen_hidden_vm_curriculum_repair.concept_primer: Instead of blurting an answer, the model writes a hidden worksheet of steps that a calculator then runs. We tried two coaching styles to make those worksheets hold up on longer problems.
qwen_hidden_vm_curriculum_repair.plain_question: Does coaching the model with corrected worksheets make its hidden step-by-step working more reliable, especially on longer and harder problems?
qwen_hidden_vm_curriculum_repair.plain_answer: No. Coaching with corrected worksheets made things worse: it roughly halved accuracy on matched problems versus plain step-by-step training, and collapsed on longer ones. The plain training itself, however, held up well.
qwen_hidden_vm_curriculum_repair.why_it_matters: Handing a model patched-up examples can quietly unlearn good habits. A correct worksheet almost always sits nearby, so build a checker that picks it at answer time rather than retraining on patches.

key_numbers.label: Matched-problem accuracy
key_numbers.sub: plain step-by-step training vs corrected-worksheet coaching

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on the longest problems
key_numbers.sub: plain training held; coached model collapsed

key_numbers.label: A correct worksheet sits nearby

charts.chart_plain_title: Corrected-worksheet coaching lags plain training
charts.chart_read: Bars show accuracy on matched fresh problems for four methods, taller is better: the model's blurted answer, the coached hidden worksheet, the plain-trained worksheet, and the best possible if worksheets were hand-checked.
charts.chart_takeaway: The plain-trained bar (72%) towers over the coached bar (35%), so coaching roughly halved accuracy even though a correct worksheet nearly always exists.

charts.chart_plain_title: Plain training degrades gracefully, coaching collapses on long problems
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is problem length (both models were trained only up to length 6); vertical axis is worksheet accuracy, higher is better. Two lines compare plain training against corrected-worksheet coaching.
charts.chart_takeaway: The plain line stays high and slopes gently to 30% at length 10, while the coached line drops to near-baseline 9%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Hidden worksheet beats the blurted answer in almost every domain
charts.chart_read: For each task domain, three bars compare the blurted answer, the hidden worksheet, and the best possible with hand-checking; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hidden worksheet beats the blurted answer in every domain except boolean, and hand-checking reaches near 100% everywhere.

charts.chart_plain_title: The coaching phase itself causes the drop
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is training step, vertical axis is accuracy on matched problems, higher is better. Both runs are identical until step 700, then only one gets the extra coaching phase.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both runs hit 72% at step 700, then the 220-step coaching phase alone drags accuracy down to 35%.

charts.chart_plain_title: A correct nearby worksheet exists even where the model fails
charts.chart_read: Evaluation splits run from easy to long-and-hard; each has two bars, the model's own guess versus the best correct worksheet found nearby by checking. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even on the longest problems where the model's guess falls under 10%, a correct nearby worksheet exists 88-96% of the time.

charts.chart_plain_title: Coaching erodes exactly-correct worksheets
charts.chart_read: Four training runs, each with two bars: the fraction of exactly-correct worksheets and the fraction with correct early steps. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Plain training keeps 56% exactly-correct worksheets, while coaching cuts that to 18%.

qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains.concept_primer: Instead of blurting out an answer, the model secretly writes a tiny step-by-step recipe that a built-in calculator then runs exactly, and the calculator's result becomes the answer.
qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains.plain_question: Can a small model be taught to solve many kinds of word problems by writing a hidden recipe a calculator runs, instead of guessing the answer?
qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains.plain_answer: Yes. When the model wrote a hidden recipe for a calculator to run, it was right about 78% of the time on fresh problems, versus only 15% when it answered directly.
qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains.why_it_matters: Teaching the model to write out an executable recipe, and showing it the full worked steps rather than just final answers, both raise accuracy sharply. More training examples help too; longer-than-trained problems remain the weak spot.

key_numbers.label: Using a hidden recipe

charts.chart_plain_title: Hidden recipe beats plain guessing on fresh mixed problems
charts.chart_read: Each bar is accuracy on fresh problems for one method: guessing directly, a hidden recipe learned from final answers only, and a hidden recipe learned from full worked steps. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-worked-steps bar is tallest at 78%, far above the 15% for direct guessing, showing the hidden recipe is what does the work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy by task type: guessing versus hidden recipe
charts.chart_read: Six kinds of problems sit along the bottom. For each, one bar is plain guessing and a second is the hidden recipe; bar height is accuracy, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hidden-recipe bar is taller in every task type, with four types jumping from near zero to between 67% and 88%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Showing worked steps produces cleaner recipes
charts.chart_read: Three quality measures across the bottom, each with two bars: training on full worked steps versus training on final answers only. Taller bars mean better, cleaner recipes.
charts.chart_takeaway: Worked-step bars are taller on all three measures, nearly doubling the rate of recovering the exact recipe, 64% versus 34%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Problems longer than the training examples lose accuracy
charts.chart_read: Two prompt styles, plainly worded and reworded, each with a bar for problems of the trained length and a bar for longer problems. Height is hidden-recipe accuracy, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The longer-problem bars are shorter, dropping to 36% on reworded prompts, so the recipe stretches only partway beyond its training length.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training examples sharply improve the hidden recipe
charts.chart_read: Three runs ordered by how many training examples they saw (160, 256, 512), each with bars for three quality measures. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar rises as examples increase; accuracy climbs from 16% at 160 examples to 78% at 512, so the method is still hungry for data.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training lifts the hidden recipe, not plain guessing
charts.chart_read: Two phases, before and after training, each with bars for plain guessing, the hidden recipe result, and recovering the exact recipe. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: After training the hidden recipe jumps from 29% to 78% and exact recipes from 0% to 64%, while plain guessing barely moves.

qwen_hidden_vm_onpolicy_canonical_repair.concept_primer: Instead of blurting an answer, the model quietly writes out a step-by-step recipe that a fixed calculator then runs and checks — like showing your work so a machine can execute it.
qwen_hidden_vm_onpolicy_canonical_repair.plain_question: Does teaching the model from auto-fixed versions of its own recipes make it better than plain training?
qwen_hidden_vm_onpolicy_canonical_repair.plain_answer: No. Writing hidden recipes lifted accuracy from 9% to 61% versus answering directly, but training on the auto-fixed recipes matched simpler training almost exactly (a 2-point gap that is not a real change) and made long tasks no better.
qwen_hidden_vm_onpolicy_canonical_repair.why_it_matters: Use the checker to pick or rerank good recipes at answer time, not to retrain the writer — folding fixes back in just reproduces plain training and can hurt on longer tasks.

charts.chart_takeaway: The hidden-recipe bars tower over direct answering, but the two middle bars are nearly the same height, so the extra training barely moved anything.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy as tasks get longer than trained
charts.chart_read: Accuracy (higher is better) going left to right toward longer tasks; training only covered the shortest. Three lines: the extra-training model, the plainly-trained model, and the best case if a checker patches the recipe.
charts.chart_takeaway: Past the trained length the extra-training line sits at or below the plain line, showing the added training gives no robustness on longer tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Four training recipes land on the same score

charts.chart_takeaway: All four approaches produce essentially identical heights, meaning distilling the auto-fixed recipes buys nothing over plain training.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy by task type on new tasks
charts.chart_read: Six task types across the bottom; taller is better. For each, three bars: answering directly, running the hidden recipe, and the best case if a checker patches the recipe.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hidden-recipe bar beats direct answering in every task type, and the checker's best-case bar recovers most of what the recipe still missed.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded tasks hurt more as tasks get longer
charts.chart_read: Three length tiers left to right; taller is better. Bars pair plain wording against reworded wording, both for the raw recipe and after a checker patches it.
charts.chart_takeaway: The gap between plain and reworded bars widens sharply at longer lengths, and patching cannot close it, so rewording bites hardest where tasks are hard.

charts.chart_plain_title: Repair-heavy recipes trade off writer quality
charts.chart_read: Four training recipes across the bottom; taller is better. Three bars each: the raw recipe score, the best case after a checker patches it, and how often the recipe was written exactly right.
charts.chart_takeaway: Recipes trained heavily on fixes raise the patched-best-case bar but drop the raw-recipe bar, trading first-try quality for how much the checker can salvage.

charts.chart_plain_title: The checker itself fails on hard reworded tasks
charts.chart_read: Seven task groups left to right, getting harder and more reworded; taller is better. One bar is how often the checker found any fix, the other how often that fix was exactly right.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars fall off a cliff on the hardest reworded group, so the checker's best-case help vanishes exactly where the model needs it most.

charts.chart_plain_title: What training adds over the untrained model
charts.chart_read: Two bars, before and after training; taller is better. Three measures each: running the hidden recipe, the best case after a checker patches it, and how often the recipe was written exactly right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained model never writes a perfect recipe, while training lifts the checker-patched best case from 28% to 95% on new tasks.

qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.verdict_tag: Watching its own programs helps only modestly
qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.concept_primer: Picture a student who solves puzzles by writing tiny programs, running each one, and watching what happens. This experiment asks whether watching those outcomes teaches better puzzle-solving than just being told which answers were right.
qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.plain_question: Can the model get better at solving puzzles by watching what its own attempted programs actually do, beyond just learning which answers turned out correct?
qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.plain_answer: It depends. Watching its own programs taught the model to predict how they behave and slightly raised its best-of-many success, but its first-attempt accuracy did not move and stayed far below what complete answer keys reach.
qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.why_it_matters: Watching outcomes teaches a model the structure of what its programs do, but not the harder judgment of which attempt is actually right. Sharper training would compare near-identical attempts that differ only in correctness.

qwen_iterative_repair_policy.verdict_tag: Step-by-step fixer nearly doubled correct recipes
qwen_iterative_repair_policy.concept_primer: Think of the model writing a 24-step recipe that mostly works but has a few wrong steps. A small helper checks the result and nudges one step at a time until the whole recipe runs correctly.
qwen_iterative_repair_policy.plain_question: Can a small helper fix a mostly-right recipe from the model by nudging one step at a time, instead of just guessing more?
qwen_iterative_repair_policy.plain_answer: Yes. Nudging one step at a time lifted fully-correct recipes from about 30% to 53% on fresh problems, nearly doubling the model's own rate and slightly beating a helper that rewrote everything at once.
qwen_iterative_repair_policy.why_it_matters: When a model's output is close but not exact, a small learned fixer that edits one piece at a time can rescue near-misses cheaply, without making the model write out its reasoning or generate more full attempts.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixing steps one at a time doubles how often the whole recipe runs correctly
charts.chart_read: Four bars on fresh problems; height is how often the full step-by-step recipe runs exactly right (taller is better). Left is the model alone, then a one-shot fixer, then the step-by-step fixer, then the best a perfect fixer could reach.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-by-step fixer bar rises far above the model-alone bar, roughly doubling correct recipes, and edges past the one-shot fixer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most of the improvement comes from the very first repair step
charts.chart_read: Lines show how often the recipe runs exactly right as more repair rounds are allowed, from zero rounds up to three (higher is better); each line is a different fresh problem set. Rising left-to-right means each round helps.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line jumps sharply after the first round then flattens, showing one fix does most of the work and extra rounds add nothing.

qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.verdict_tag: Small-branch practice transfers to wider branches
qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.concept_primer: Think of the model as writing a short recipe of steps that a fixed machine then runs; it only counts as a success if that machine reaches the correct final answer.
qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.plain_question: Does a much bigger toolbox of steps, or recipes that split into several branches, break the model's ability to write recipes that actually run correctly?
qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.plain_answer: Yes. Showing the model just a few small branching recipes taught it to handle much wider branching, jumping from near-random to about 87% correct, while models shown only straight-line recipes stayed near the floor. A four-times larger toolbox did not hurt straight-line recipes.
qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.why_it_matters: You don't need to train on every branch size; a little practice on small branching cases carries over to wider ones. But reworded prompts still trip up branching recipes, so test wording robustness before trusting it.

charts.chart_takeaway: The best model keeps most of its score on reworded straight-line recipes but drops sharply on reworded branching ones, so wording robustness is unsolved for branches.

qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.verdict_tag: Works for short programs, brittle on long ones
qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.concept_primer: Instead of writing out the math step by step in words, the model fills a few blank slots on a punch card that a fixed built-in calculator then runs to get the answer.
qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.plain_question: Can the model solve math by filling in a hidden calculator's slots instead of writing out every step, and does offering several candidate fills help?
qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. Directly teaching the model to fill one punch card worked cleanly for short and medium programs, beating the random-level result from generating several candidates, but the longest programs stayed unreliable and wording-sensitive.
qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.why_it_matters: Letting a model configure a built-in calculator is a promising shortcut for short tasks, but do not expect it to scale to long chains yet, and adding more candidate attempts will not rescue it.
