charts.chart_takeaway: The edited-pick bar sits just above the original on plainly-worded problems but far below the best-possible bar, and gains nothing on reworded problems.

charts.chart_takeaway: Even where a good fix existed, the picker captured only about a quarter of the possible gain on plain problems and none on reworded ones.

qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.concept_primer: Picture the model as a machine operator that solves a task by making one small edit to a program, checking the result, then editing again. The test: does a smarter training routine make it edit better?
qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.plain_question: Does letting the model practice and then retraining it on automatically-found fixes make its step-by-step editing solver more accurate?
qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.plain_answer: No. The fix-retraining did not improve the step-by-step solver: it matched or trailed the simpler training on four of five test sets, and both stayed far below what a perfect edit sequence can reach.
qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.why_it_matters: Collecting verified fixes is the easy part; turning them into better whole-solution choices is the real bottleneck. Future work should reward complete solution attempts rather than scoring single edits in isolation.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rates by test set: fix-retraining versus simpler training
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one test set. Within it, bars compare the plain model, the simply-trained editor, the fix-retrained editor, and an ideal solver. Taller bars mean more tasks solved, so higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fix-retrained bars sit at or below the simply-trained bars on most test sets, while the ideal-solver bars tower far above every trained version.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does allowing more edit steps help the retrained solver?
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many edits the model is allowed; the vertical axis is the share of tasks solved. Each line is one test set, and higher lines mean more solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only one line climbs as edits increase; the rest stay flat or drop, so more editing does not reliably improve results.

charts.chart_takeaway: Choosing the right edit fell from the basic stage's peak to the fix-retraining stage, and stronger per-step scores never produced better full solutions.

qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.verdict_tag: Reachability scoring fails to beat plain search
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.concept_primer: Imagine guiding a builder assembling a calculation step by step, but you are never shown the right answer. You must score each half-finished attempt using only a hunch about whether it is on track.
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.plain_question: Can scoring half-finished programs by whether they could still reach a correct answer guide the search better than just following the model's own confidence?
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.plain_answer: No. Scoring half-finished attempts by whether they could still reach a correct answer did no better than plain confidence-based search. Only a stricter scorer, which checks each step against the one known correct program, added a small edge.
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.why_it_matters: The bottleneck is picking the right candidate from those already generated, not generating it. A coarse could-this-still-work signal is too blunt; a scorer that rates how promising each path is looks like the better bet.

charts.chart_takeaway: Only the strict exact-match scorer nudges the everyday-task bar upward; the reachability scorer sits level with plain search, far below the answer-peeking bar.

charts.chart_takeaway: The best-in-shortlist bar towers over the first-pick bar, showing the right program is usually generated but ranked below wrong ones.

charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of program steps each rule labels as good; higher means a broader, looser target. Exact matching is strict, raw reachability is loose, and filtered reachability sits between them.
charts.chart_takeaway: The raw-reachability bar is roughly seven times the exact bar, marking far more steps as good and giving the scorer a much looser target to learn.

charts.chart_plain_title: Both scorers learn their target well
charts.chart_read: Bars rate each scorer's ability to sort good steps from bad on fresh data, from about 0.5 (coin-flip) up to 1.0 (perfect); taller is better. Left is the exact-match scorer, right the reachability scorer.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars sit far above coin-flip, and the exact-match scorer is cleaner, yet even it turns into only a small real-task gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tightening the reachability labels across trial runs
charts.chart_read: Across four successive runs, two bars per run show the share of steps marked good by exact matching versus reachability. Lower reachability bars mean a tighter, less permissive target.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reachability bars shrink run over run while the exact bars stay low and flat, showing the target was progressively tightened.

qwen_shared_parser_compiler.verdict_tag: A trained reader works but stays brittle
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.concept_primer: Think of the frozen model's internal notes as a foreign transcript. A small add-on "reader" is trained to translate that transcript, step by step, into a math recipe a calculator can run exactly.
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.plain_question: Can a tiny add-on read a frozen model's internal notes and turn a word problem into exact runnable math steps?
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. On the exact wording it was trained on, the reader turns short recipes into correct answers most of the time, far beating the do-nothing baselines. But it falls apart on longer recipes and on reworded problems.
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.why_it_matters: Structured step-by-step teaching can install a real skill that plain answer-only training never finds. But the skill stays glued to one exact phrasing and short problems, so it is not yet a general capability.

charts.chart_takeaway: Only the trained reader's line rises high on short recipes and then slides toward the flat, near-zero baselines as recipes lengthen, meaning the skill works but does not scale to long recipes.

charts.chart_takeaway: The follow-up-training bars are much shorter than the original bars except at the shortest recipe, meaning answer-only practice damages the skill it was supposed to reinforce.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tiny per-step slips pile up into whole-recipe failure

charts.chart_takeaway: The per-step lines stay high while the whole-recipe line drops steeply, showing small slips on each number multiply into total failure on long recipes.

charts.chart_takeaway: The deepest, most-trained design's line stays highest across recipe lengths while the shallow probe collapses after four steps, showing design choices extend how far recipes can run.

charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is recipe length; the vertical axis is how much probability the system places on the correct answer, higher is better. One line is the reader-plus-calculator, the other a do-nothing baseline.
charts.chart_takeaway: Beyond four steps the reader's line drops onto the flat baseline, meaning its confidence in the right answer becomes no better than a random guess.

qwen_slot_repair_distillation.verdict_tag: Trained fixer did not transfer to new prompts
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.concept_primer: A tool writes a short step-by-step math program to answer each question, but often gets a step wrong. Think of a proofreader trained to patch just the buggy steps in a draft.
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.plain_question: Can we train a small fixer to patch the buggy steps in an auto-written math program so more answers come out right?
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.plain_answer: No. A proofreader trained to patch the buggy math steps helped only on the practice questions it was tuned against; on new and reworded questions it matched or slightly hurt accuracy, even though about 86% were actually fixable.
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.why_it_matters: A patch learned on one batch of questions can overfit that batch and fail elsewhere. Since most errors were fixable in principle, the bottleneck is reliably choosing which steps to edit, not the lack of a nearby correct answer.

key_numbers.label: Correct answers on new questions
key_numbers.sub: the trained fixer left new-question accuracy essentially unchanged

key_numbers.label: Correct on reworded questions
key_numbers.sub: the fixer actually lowered accuracy here

key_numbers.label: Share that were fixable
key_numbers.sub: a best-possible repair could correct most of them

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixer helps only on practice questions, not new ones
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one set of questions. Within it, three bars: the original auto-written program, the trained fixer's version, and the best-possible repair. Taller means more correct answers; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fixer bar tops the original only on the practice set and sits far below the best-possible bar everywhere, showing the gains never reach new questions.

charts.chart_plain_title: The fixer helps on practice sets but hurts on new ones
charts.chart_read: One bar per question set showing how much of the fixable room the fixer actually closed. Bars above the zero line mean it helped; bars below zero mean it made things worse.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars sit above zero on the two practice sets but drop below zero on every new-prompt set, so the learned repair does not carry over.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixer barely beats the original, only on the last round
charts.chart_read: The jagged line is the fixer's share of correct practice answers after each training round; the flat line is the original program's fixed level. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fixer line bounces around and only pokes above the flat baseline on the final round, a fragile edge rather than steady skill.

charts.chart_plain_title: Editing makes two phrasings of a question disagree more
charts.chart_read: Bars group by program source: original, best-possible repair, and the fixer. Height shows how often two wordings of the same question give the same answer (and both correct). Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fixer nearly halves answer agreement across wordings versus the original, while the best-possible repair raises it, so editing adds inconsistency.

charts.chart_plain_title: No fixer setup wins on both practice and new questions
charts.chart_read: Each configuration has two bars: correct answers on the practice set and on a fresh set. Taller is better on each.
charts.chart_takeaway: The setup that tops the practice bars is not the one that tops the fresh bars, so no single design is best on both.

charts.chart_plain_title: The fixer never settles on which steps to edit
charts.chart_read: Two lines track, over training rounds, how accurately the fixer picks which steps need editing versus how many needed steps it catches. Higher is better for both.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-picking accuracy line swings widely round to round and never settles, so the edit decisions stay unreliable.

qwen_slot_stability_compiler.verdict_tag: Recipe method works, sameness nudge mostly doesn't
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.concept_primer: Instead of guessing a math answer, the model writes a step-by-step recipe that a hidden calculator runs exactly. We test whether forcing it to write identical recipes for reworded versions of the same problem helps.
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.plain_question: Does forcing the model to write the same step-by-step recipe for reworded versions of a math problem make its exact answers more reliable?
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. Writing a recipe a calculator runs exactly crushes plain answer-guessing, which never learned the task at all. But the extra rule forcing identical recipes across rewordings helped only sometimes, tying or trailing a simpler version on most lengths.
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.why_it_matters: For exact multi-step arithmetic on a small model, have it emit a checkable recipe rather than guess the answer. The sameness-across-rewordings rule isn't worth it; instead target the error buildup on long chains.

key_numbers.label: Guess-the-answer method
key_numbers.sub: never solved the task at any length

key_numbers.label: Recipe method, short problems
key_numbers.sub: exact answers on 4-step problems

key_numbers.label: Longest reworded problems
key_numbers.sub: the sameness nudge's one clear gain

key_numbers.label: Longest problems, exact match
key_numbers.sub: small per-step slips pile up

charts.chart_plain_title: Exact-answer accuracy as problems get longer
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is problem length in steps; vertical axis is how often the exact final answer is right, higher is better. Two lines are recipe-writing versions; the bottom flat line is plain answer-guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both recipe lines start high and fall as problems lengthen, while the guessing line hugs the bottom, never rising above random-guess level.

charts.chart_plain_title: Exact-answer accuracy on reworded problems
charts.chart_read: Same layout but for reworded problems: horizontal axis is problem length, vertical axis is exact-answer accuracy, higher is better. Two recipe versions versus the flat answer-guessing line.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the longest reworded problems the sameness-nudged recipe pulls clearly ahead (23% vs 16%); elsewhere the two recipe versions overlap.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do matching recipes make reworded answers agree more?
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by problem length; height is how often two rewordings of one problem give the same answer, higher is better. One bar is the sameness-nudged version, the other the plain recipe.
charts.chart_takeaway: The nudge wins on short and longest problems but loses badly at length 8 and ties at 12, so it's a mixed help, not a reliable one.

charts.chart_plain_title: Mid-training lead on reworded problems vanishes by the end
charts.chart_read: Bars compare the two recipe versions at an early and a late training point, on medium-length problems; taller is better, for both exact answers and rewording agreement.
charts.chart_takeaway: Early on, the nudged version leads sharply on agreement, but by the end the plain version fully catches up, erasing the advantage.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where recipes break: single steps versus whole program
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is problem length; vertical axis is accuracy, higher is better. Top lines are accuracy of individual recipe pieces; the bottom line is getting the entire program exactly right.
charts.chart_takeaway: Individual pieces stay near-perfect, but tiny per-step slips compound so the whole-program line collapses to zero on the longest chains.

charts.chart_plain_title: Second half of training does most of the work
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by reworded-problem length; height is exact-answer accuracy, higher is better. Four bars per group compare each recipe version at an early and a late training point.
charts.chart_takeaway: Late-training bars tower over early ones on longer problems, and the plain version catches up to the nudged one by the end.

qwen_span_free_compiler.verdict_tag: The add-on helps only where it drilled
qwen_span_free_compiler.concept_primer: Think of the frozen model as a clerk who silently reads a math word-problem, and a small add-on translator that turns the clerk's private notes into the exact steps a calculator can run.
qwen_span_free_compiler.plain_question: Can a small add-on read a frozen model's private notes and write out the exact math steps to solve the problem, without being handed the numbers?
qwen_span_free_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. On the exact problem style it practiced, the add-on nailed the full sequence of steps 100% of the time, versus random-guess level for every shortcut method. But it fell apart on longer chains or reworded problems.
qwen_span_free_compiler.why_it_matters: A tiny translator can decode a frozen model's internal notes into runnable steps, but only if you teach it where to look. Expect it to memorize one narrow format, not to generalize.

key_numbers.label: Full sequence solved, practiced style
key_numbers.sub: shortcut methods vs the guided translator

key_numbers.label: Full sequence solved, reworded style
key_numbers.sub: random-guess level once wording changes

key_numbers.label: Full sequence solved, longer chains
key_numbers.sub: collapses past the practiced length

charts.chart_plain_title: Practiced problems: guided translator versus shortcut methods
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method; height is how often the whole step-sequence came out exactly right on the practiced problem style. Taller is better. The three shortcut methods sit near the floor; the guided translator stands near the top.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the guided translator reaches the top of the chart, hitting nearly 100%, while every shortcut method stays at random-guess level.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy falls off a cliff past the practiced chain length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many math steps the problem has; the vertical axis is how often the whole sequence is solved exactly. Higher is better. Each line is one method. All lines are high only at the practiced short length and drop to the floor beyond it.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line plunges from near-perfect at the practiced length to near-zero at longer lengths, showing the skill is locked to one chain length.

charts.chart_plain_title: Rewording the problem breaks the translator's number-reading
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by what the translator had to read out: the starting number, each operation, each amount, and the whole sequence. Height is accuracy; taller is better. Trained wording bars are near the top; reworded-prompt bars are much shorter except for the starting number.
charts.chart_takeaway: Reworded bars stay tall only for the starting number and roughly halve for the operations and amounts, dragging the full-sequence bar down to almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Good step-by-step accuracy still fails the whole chain
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is chain length; vertical axis is accuracy; higher is better. Two lines track how often each single operation and amount is right; the third tracks solving the whole sequence. The per-step lines stay fairly high while the whole-sequence line sinks fast.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even with each step around 90% right at longer chains, the whole-sequence line drops below 6%, because small per-step errors pile up.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only teaching the translator where to look works
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a different way of building the translator; height is how often it solved the whole practiced sequence exactly. Taller is better. The three simpler pooling designs sit at the floor; the two that learn where to focus stand near the top.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy jumps from random-guess level to near-100% only for the two designs that learn where to point, identifying focus as the key ingredient.

charts.chart_plain_title: No training schedule escapes the longer-chain cliff
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is chain length; vertical axis is how often the whole sequence is solved; higher is better. Each line is a different training schedule. All lines collapse toward the floor by the twelve-step mark, and some start low even at short chains.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every schedule's line falls to near-zero by twelve steps, and the fixed and mixed schedules give up much of their short-chain accuracy too.

charts.chart_plain_title: Robustness to rewording trades against raw accuracy
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by training schedule, with one bar for trained wording and one for reworded wording; height is full-sequence accuracy, taller is better. High-accuracy schedules show a tall trained bar next to a floor-level reworded bar; the milder schedules show two similar mid-height bars.
charts.chart_takeaway: The most accurate schedules drop from near-perfect to random-guess level under rewording, while milder schedules keep similar accuracy both ways but never climb as high.

charts.chart_plain_title: Translator locks onto trained wording early, never learns rewording
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is training progress; vertical axis is how often the whole sequence is solved; higher is better. One line is trained wording, the other is reworded wording. The trained line shoots up early and flattens near the top; the reworded line stays pinned at the bottom throughout.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained-wording line reaches near-perfect within the first stretch and holds, while the reworded line never lifts off random-guess level.

qwen_state_ladder_compiler.verdict_tag: Gradual practice helped, step-checking did not
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.concept_primer: Picture the model working through a recipe of many arithmetic steps in a row, keeping a running total; one slip anywhere ruins the final answer, so staying exact over a long chain is the whole challenge.
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.plain_question: Does forcing the model to show and check its running total after every single step help it follow long arithmetic chains correctly?
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.plain_answer: No. Checking the running total after every step never beat a matched model trained without it, and full-strength checking made things worse. The real gain came from a separate trick, gradually working up to longer chains, which lifted the hardest problems from random-guess level to about 21%.
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.why_it_matters: To get a small model to nail long chains of exact steps, invest in a gradual difficulty ramp rather than forcing it to justify every intermediate step; heavy step-checking can even make it fragile on reworded problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: Gradual practice lifts long chains; step-checking never beats the control
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for three kinds of 24-step problems: standard wording, reworded, and both. Taller is better. Compares an ordinary model, a gradual-practice model, and two step-checking variants.
charts.chart_takeaway: The ordinary-model bars sit at random-guess level while the gradual-practice bars tower over them, and no step-checking bar tops the gradual-practice control.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy falls as the programs get longer
charts.chart_read: Lines show how often the whole program is worked exactly right as it grows from 4 to 24 steps, one line per training method; higher and flatter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All lines start together near the top and slide down as chains lengthen, with the full step-checking line dropping fastest to the lowest point at 24 steps.

charts.chart_read: One bar per method showing accuracy on the hardest 24-step problems at each method's peak moment during training, not the final saved model; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At its best moment the lighter step-checking bar is tallest, but that peak was never saved, so it doesn't count as a real win.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does the same problem, worded two ways, get worked the same?
charts.chart_read: One bar per method showing how often the same 24-step problem, written two different ways, produced matching step-by-step work; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full step-checking bar nearly vanishes near zero while the no-checking control stands highest, showing heavy checking made the model inconsistent.

charts.chart_plain_title: Step-checking's early lead vanishes with more training
charts.chart_read: Two pairs of bars compare the step-checking model and the control on the hardest 24-step problems, first after short training then after long training; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Step-checking leads at the short budget but slips below the control after longer training, so its early edge evaporates.

charts.chart_plain_title: Full step-checking fails completely on reworded problems
charts.chart_read: Bars compare accuracy on standard versus reworded 24-step problems for three methods; taller is better, and a small gap between the two means the method handles new wording.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every method dips on reworded problems, but the full step-checking bar collapses all the way to zero while the others hold near one-in-seven.

charts.chart_plain_title: Long-chain skill appears only in the final training stage
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy on the hardest 24-step problems across training steps for each method; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line hugs zero until the final long-chain stage, spikes near step 800, then dips, so the last saved model is not the best one.

charts.chart_plain_title: Near-perfect parts still add up to failed whole programs

charts.chart_takeaway: The per-part bars sit near perfect while whole-program bars sink to about one in five, as tiny scattered slips compound over 24 steps.

qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.verdict_tag: Copying steps was not the cause
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.concept_primer: Picture the model writing a short step-by-step program to reach an answer, then running it. The test is whether that program still works when it grows to 24 steps instead of just a few.
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.plain_question: When a model writes longer step-by-step programs, does copying its earlier steps into the new ones explain why it handles the longest 24-step programs?
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.plain_answer: No. Copying earlier steps was not the reason. What actually worked was training on full-length problems from the start while gradually raising difficulty, which reached 97% on the longest programs versus about 2% for the copying approach.
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.why_it_matters: To get a model handling longer step-by-step problems, train on the full length from the start while gradually raising difficulty, rather than stitching longer programs together from copied earlier pieces.

charts.chart_takeaway: The full-length-plus-rising-difficulty approach towers over the rest on standard wording and twin-prompts; copying steps only helps on reworded prompts.

charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines stay low, then jump sharply in the final training stretch, so the skill emerges late rather than building steadily.

charts.chart_takeaway: The copying-steps approach scores 75% on reworded prompts but 2% on standard ones, so wording, not just the approach, drives the failures.

charts.chart_plain_title: Failing approaches get most steps right but botch the final answer

charts.chart_takeaway: Losing approaches keep 62-86% of intermediate steps correct while final answers sink near zero, so errors cluster in the last steps.

qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.verdict_tag: Stretched to longer problems without breaking
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.concept_primer: Picture the model with a built-in calculator that follows a hidden, step-by-step recipe in its head instead of writing the steps out. Here we try to stretch that recipe to more steps.
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.plain_question: Can the model's hidden step-by-step recipe be stretched to handle much longer problems without losing accuracy on the shorter ones it already solved?
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.plain_answer: Yes. Growing the hidden recipe from short to long problems kept the shorter ones perfectly solved while the longest reached 83 to 100 percent, all without writing steps out or trying many guesses.
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.why_it_matters: You can extend a model's internal step-by-step solving to harder, longer tasks by growing it in stages, rather than retraining from scratch or forcing it to spell every step out.

charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show how often the grown solver reached the right answer for short (8-step), medium (16-step), and long (24-step) problems, split by three prompt styles. Taller bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars stay at full height for short and medium problems and dip only slightly for the longest, so growing the recipe cost almost no accuracy on what it already handled.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy as longer problems were added in stages

charts.chart_takeaway: Consistency holds at full height for short and medium problems and eases only to about seven-eighths for the longest, so reworded prompts mostly compile to the same internal steps.

qwen_structured_bridge.verdict_tag: Translate to steps, then a calculator runs them
qwen_structured_bridge.concept_primer: Instead of asking the frozen model to blurt out a final answer, a small add-on translates its reading of the words into a list of button-presses that a separate calculator then executes exactly.
qwen_structured_bridge.plain_question: Can a frozen model's reading of a word problem be turned into steps a hidden calculator runs, beating just asking for the answer?
qwen_structured_bridge.plain_answer: Yes. Translating the words into calculator steps hit 96-100% on chains up to 12 operations, while asking the same model to name the final answer stayed at random-guess level. The win requires showing the translator the correct steps during training.
qwen_structured_bridge.why_it_matters: When a task is a chain of exact operations, wire the model to emit the steps and let a reliable calculator do the math, rather than trusting it to compute the answer in its head.

charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare three approaches at 4, 8, and 12-operation chains (left to right); height is share of answers correct, taller is better. Only the step-translation bars rise near the top; the other two sit at the floor.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trace-taught translator towers near 100% across all chain lengths while both the direct-answer and no-guidance versions flatline at random-guess level.

charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy as chains grow from 4 to 24 operations, well past the 1-4 seen in training; higher and flatter is better. The lines drift down only slightly toward the right.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy slopes down gradually to about 88% at 24 operations, six times the training depth, showing graceful decline rather than a collapse.

charts.chart_takeaway: Numbers are read essentially perfectly, so the 12% of failed chains trace to the occasional wrong operation, whose errors pile up over many steps.

qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.verdict_tag: Model truly reads examples but trades some accuracy
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.concept_primer: Think of a student handed a few worked examples on an exam. You want them actually reading those examples to solve new problems, not just guessing from the topic. This training method rewards reading the examples.
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.plain_question: Does this training method make the model both more accurate and genuinely reliant on the worked examples it is shown?
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.plain_answer: It depends. The method makes the model genuinely rely on the examples (accuracy collapses to about 7% when they are scrambled) and nearly triples accuracy over the untrained model, but a simpler training recipe scored a few points higher.
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.why_it_matters: If you need a model that actually uses the examples you feed it rather than pattern-matching the task type, this method delivers that reliably, but expect to give up a little raw accuracy for it.

charts.chart_read: Bars group five approaches (untrained baseline plus four training recipes) across three conditions: real examples, scrambled examples, no examples. Height is the share of tasks solved completely; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: This method's real-examples bar is tall near 50% but its scrambled bar collapses to about 7%, unlike other recipes whose scrambled bars stay high, proving it truly reads the examples.

charts.chart_read: Bars show, for each recipe, the accuracy drop when examples are scrambled instead of real, on two test sets. A taller bar means the model leans more heavily on the actual examples, which is the goal.
charts.chart_takeaway: This method's bars are the tallest on both test sets, meaning its answers hinge on the real examples far more than any other recipe's do.

charts.chart_takeaway: With real examples this method scores about 84%, but its scrambled bar drops to about 7% while other recipes stay at 37 to 83%, again showing it depends on the examples.

charts.chart_takeaway: This method lifts most categories, several from zero to 78-100%, with only one category slipping backward and one staying stuck at zero.

charts.chart_takeaway: The main test set stays steady near 49-51% across all three runs, so the gain is repeatable rather than a one-off lucky run.

charts.chart_takeaway: Every run helps 17-18 tasks and hurts exactly 3, so the improvement is broad and consistent rather than a handful of lucky tasks.

qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.concept_primer: Think of a proofreader who is handed a mostly-correct recipe with a buggy ending and several candidate corrections, and must pick the right fix without ever seeing the answer key.
qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.plain_question: Can a learned proofreader fix the buggy endings of mostly-correct programs by picking the right candidate, without peeking at the answer?
qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.plain_answer: No. Both learned proofreaders left the share of correct programs exactly where it started, near 45%, even though a picker allowed to peek could reach 91% from the very same candidate fixes.
qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.why_it_matters: Choosing the fix, not having one, is the wall. A picker that only reads summary statistics fails; it must actually compare each candidate program against the original prompt to be trusted.

key_numbers.label: Programs that run correctly
key_numbers.sub: unchanged after the learned proofreader

key_numbers.label: If allowed to peek at the answer
key_numbers.sub: a perfect picker over the same fixes

key_numbers.label: Weakest program source, with peeking
key_numbers.sub: where nearly all missed gains sit

key_numbers.label: Programs the proofreader edited
key_numbers.sub: it chose to change nothing

charts.chart_plain_title: Learned repair picker never beats leaving the program alone
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one way of choosing a fix; height is the share of 24-step programs that run correctly start to finish, so taller is better. Bars cover no repair, two learned proofreaders, a scrambled-label control, and two peek-allowed pickers.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two learned-proofreader bars sit exactly on the no-repair bar while the peek-allowed answer bar towers far above, so a good fix exists but is never chosen.

charts.chart_plain_title: The repair gap stays wide across every batch of programs
charts.chart_read: Four batches of programs, three bars each: leave the program as-is, the learned proofreader, and a peek-allowed picker. Height is the share that run correctly, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned-proofreader bar is identical to leave-as-is on every batch, always sitting far below the peek-allowed bar that could reach it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct fixes are plentiful, so having a fix is not the problem
charts.chart_read: For each batch, one bar is the share of programs where a correct ending-fix already exists among the tried edits, the other is the share already correct with no fix. Taller means more fixable.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fix-exists bar sits near ninety percent everywhere, roughly double the already-correct bar, so the raw material for repair is abundant.

charts.chart_plain_title: Peeking would rescue mostly the weakest program source
charts.chart_read: Three source generators, two bars each: leaving programs as-is versus a peek-allowed picker. Height is the share that run correctly, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The peek-allowed picker lifts the weakest source from near-zero to about 78%, so nearly all the unclaimed gains sit on that weak source.

charts.chart_plain_title: Scrambled control edits and harms programs; the real picker touches nothing
charts.chart_read: Four batches of programs. Bars show how often a scrambled-label control edits programs and how often it harms them, next to how often the real proofreader edits. Lower editing by the real picker means it stayed hands-off.
charts.chart_takeaway: The scrambled control changes and damages roughly a third of programs while the real proofreader edits zero, showing its stillness is a deliberate retreat, not a broken pipeline.

qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.verdict_tag: Extra copying hints did not help
qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.concept_primer: Picture a student transcribing a long chain of arithmetic into a calculator one step at a time; a single mis-copied number ruins the final answer. The extra training aid pointed out exactly where each number sat in the wording.
qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.plain_question: Does pointing the model to exactly where each number sits in the wording help it copy and run long multi-step math problems more accurately?
qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.plain_answer: No. Adding location hints did not improve exact answers over the plain version (about 28% versus 27%, essentially tied), and the hints actually made the model less consistent when the same problem was reworded.
qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.why_it_matters: The model already finds the right numbers; the real problem is that tiny copy slips pile up across many steps. Future work should check and repair the running calculation, not point out where numbers sit.

key_numbers.label: Exact answers, plain vs hinted
key_numbers.sub: essentially tied, no real improvement

key_numbers.label: Agreement when reworded
key_numbers.sub: heavier hints made rewording consistency worse

key_numbers.label: Reworded accuracy, fragile hint version
key_numbers.sub: one hint setup collapses when wording changes

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra hints do not beat the plain version
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one training setup: the plain version and two hint-added versions. In each cluster one bar shows how often the final answer is exactly right, the other how often two rewordings of the same problem agree. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The plain version's agreement bar towers over both hint versions while the exact-answer bars are all roughly equal, so the hints added nothing and cost consistency.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy falls as problems get longer, for every setup
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many steps the problem has; the vertical axis is how often the final answer comes out exactly right. Each line is one training setup, and higher and flatter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines drop steeply from short to long problems and sit almost on top of each other, showing length, not the missing hints, drives the failures.

charts.chart_plain_title: One hint version breaks when problems are reworded
charts.chart_read: Two groups compare original wording against reworded wording. Inside each group one bar per training setup shows how often the final answer is exactly right; taller is better, and a big drop from left to right means the setup is fragile to rewording.
charts.chart_takeaway: The soft-position hint bar collapses from about a quarter correct to roughly one in nine when reworded, while the other two setups barely move.

qwen_trace_bootstrap_retention.concept_primer: Think of teaching a student a method with worked examples that show every step, then switching to only grading their final answers. The question is whether the method survives once you stop showing the steps.
qwen_trace_bootstrap_retention.plain_question: Once a helper has learned a step-by-step method from worked examples, can it keep that method while you only grade its final answers?
qwen_trace_bootstrap_retention.plain_answer: Yes, it holds. A helper first taught with worked steps keeps solving about 97% of the hardest problems even after you switch to grading only final answers, while a helper given only final answers from the start stays near 1%, no better than guessing.
qwen_trace_bootstrap_retention.why_it_matters: Give a small model the method through worked examples first; afterward, cheap final-answer feedback is enough to preserve and even sharpen it. Final-answer feedback alone never teaches the method from scratch.

key_numbers.label: Hardest problems solved, worked-steps helper
key_numbers.sub: after switching to final-answer grading only

key_numbers.label: Hardest problems solved, final-answers-only helper
key_numbers.sub: no better than random guessing

key_numbers.label: Hardest problems solved, plain answer-guesser
key_numbers.sub: same information, no step method

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each teaching recipe solves the hardest problems
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one teaching recipe; bar height is the share of the longest 24-step problems the helper solves exactly, so taller is better. The two right-hand bars that used worked examples tower over the near-flat bars that never did.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two recipes that first showed worked steps reach near the top of the chart, while every recipe trained only on final answers sits almost flat on the floor.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy as problems get longer, by teaching recipe
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is problem length in steps and the vertical axis is the share solved exactly, so lines that stay high are better. The worked-example recipes hold near the top even far past the lengths they trained on, while the final-answer-only lines lie flat along the bottom.
charts.chart_takeaway: The worked-example lines stay near the ceiling even at the longest 24-step problems, while the answer-only lines never lift off the floor at any length.

charts.chart_plain_title: What happens right after worked examples are switched off
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training progress; worked-example coaching is switched off after the halfway point. The vertical axis is accuracy on the longest problems, so higher is better. One line dips sharply right after the switch, another barely moves, and both climb back up by the end.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-speed line dips right after coaching stops but then recovers to finish highest, showing the helper keeps and even sharpens the method on its own.

qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.verdict_tag: Writing the recipe beats doing the math
qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.concept_primer: Think of a recipe versus cooking: the model can write out a correct step-by-step recipe that a machine then follows exactly, even when the model would botch the cooking (the arithmetic) if it tried to do it in its head.
qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.plain_question: If the model only ever practiced single steps, can it still write correct recipes for much longer multi-step tasks?
qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.plain_answer: Yes. Trained only on one-step examples, its written six-step recipes ran correctly about 63% of the time, while just asking it for the answer directly got 0% right at that length.
qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.why_it_matters: Have the model emit an explicit step list for a machine to run, rather than trusting its head math. The recipe holds up on long tasks; the weak spot now is understanding reworded instructions, not running the steps.

key_numbers.label: Six-step tasks solved, direct answer vs written recipe
key_numbers.sub: answering in-head collapses; the machine-run recipe holds up

key_numbers.label: Two-step tasks solved by the recipe
key_numbers.sub: best result, drifts down only gradually as tasks get longer

key_numbers.label: Six-step recipe when the task is reworded
key_numbers.sub: same task, different wording, so wording is the weak spot

charts.chart_plain_title: Written recipes hold up as tasks get longer; direct answers collapse
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many steps the task needs; vertical axis is how often it was solved, higher is better. Each line is one method; three lines are machine-run recipes, the bottom line is the model answering directly in its head.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe lines slope down gently and stay high while the direct-answer line falls all the way to the floor by six steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: The recipe runs right even when the model's own answer is wrong
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many steps the task needs; vertical axis is how often it was solved, higher is better. The top line is the model's written recipe run by a machine; the bottom line is the answer the model wrote itself.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two lines split wide apart: at six steps the machine-run recipe is often right while the model's own written answer is essentially never right.

charts.chart_plain_title: Rewording the same task lowers success at every length
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many steps the task needs; vertical axis is how often the machine-run recipe solved it, higher is better. The upper line is the familiar wording, the lower line is the same tasks reworded.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reworded line sits below the familiar-wording line across all lengths, showing the wording of the request is the remaining hurdle.

charts.chart_plain_title: When a recipe fails, it usually fails to run at all
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one type of outcome for the written recipes across all task lengths; taller means a bigger share of recipes fall in that group. The first bar is fully correct; the rest are kinds of mistakes.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tallest failure bar is recipes that won't run at all, while picking the wrong steps is a tiny sliver, so parsing not reasoning is the main breakdown.

qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.verdict_tag: Self-teaching helps, but worked examples win
qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.concept_primer: The model turns word problems into a short recipe of steps a calculator runs exactly. You can teach it with full worked-out recipes, or let it guess-and-check toward the right answer and learn from its wins.
qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.plain_question: Can letting the model guess-and-check its way to correct answers make it as good as showing it full worked-out recipes?
qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.plain_answer: It depends. Learning from its own guess-and-check wins raised first-try accuracy from 62% to 73%, beating the untrained starting point, but full worked-out recipes reached nearly 100%, so self-teaching helped without closing the gap.
qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.why_it_matters: When you can score final answers but lack worked examples, self-teaching gives a real lift for free, yet a modest stock of full worked examples stays far more powerful and worth collecting.

key_numbers.label: First-try accuracy, before to after self-teaching
key_numbers.sub: getting the problem right unaided

key_numbers.label: First-try accuracy with full worked recipes
key_numbers.sub: the teaching ceiling to beat

key_numbers.label: Same method on a real 4B model's reading
key_numbers.sub: self-teaching nearly tripled its accuracy

key_numbers.label: Fresh problems where guess-and-check found a correct recipe
key_numbers.sub: but almost all just confirmed known answers

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-teaching lifts accuracy, worked recipes nearly max it out
charts.chart_read: Each training method has two bars on fresh problems; taller is better. The lighter bar is the model answering unaided, the darker is it allowed to guess-and-check. Bars go left to right: starting point, self-taught, full worked recipes.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-taught bar climbs from 62% to 73% unaided, but the full-recipe bars tower near 100%, dwarfing the self-teaching gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Almost all the self-teaching gain arrives in the first round
charts.chart_read: Two rising lines track unaided accuracy across rounds of self-teaching, starting at round zero; higher is better. One line is ordinary fresh problems, the other is harder multi-step problems.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines jump at round one then flatten, and the harder-problems line stays well below, showing self-teaching stalls early and struggles with longer recipes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Same pattern when reading comes from a real 4B model
charts.chart_read: Bars for three training methods on fresh problems; taller is better. The light bar is answering unaided, the dark bar is with guess-and-check. Left to right: starting point, self-taught, full worked recipes.
charts.chart_takeaway: Self-teaching nearly triples unaided accuracy from 18% to 50%, but the full-recipe bar near 95% again far outreaches it.

charts.chart_plain_title: The self-teaching loop runs out of fresh lessons
charts.chart_read: Two lines across rounds; higher means more. One is the share of problems where guess-and-check found a correct recipe, the other is the share of those that were genuinely new corrections rather than already-known answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: The found line climbs to 93% while the new-correction line collapses toward 5%, so later rounds mostly re-confirm what the model already knew.

charts.chart_plain_title: Guess-and-check stays ahead of unaided answering every round
charts.chart_read: Two lines across self-teaching rounds on fresh problems; higher is better. The lower line is the model answering unaided, the upper line is it allowed to guess-and-check toward the right answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guess-and-check line sits a steady 14-16 points above the unaided line throughout, meaning fixable mistakes remain in the model's first answers.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even full worked recipes cannot fully crack harder problems
charts.chart_read: Four groups of problem types, each with two bars for the two model front ends after training on full worked recipes; taller is better. The first three groups are ordinary problems, the last is harder multi-step ones.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both front ends near-max the first three groups but drop 15-20 points on the harder multi-step group, marking longer recipes as the stubborn weak spot.

charts.chart_plain_title: The 4B-model version finds fewer, messier self-taught lessons
charts.chart_read: Lines across rounds; higher means more. Two lines are the 4B-model version's share of problems solved and share needing heavy rewriting, one line is the simpler main version's solved share for comparison.
charts.chart_takeaway: The 4B version starts far lower and rewrites more heavily, but its solved line climbs toward the main version's, showing a slower version of the same trend.

qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.verdict_tag: Recipe cards from memory did not help
qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.concept_primer: Imagine a cook facing a new dish who is first handed a recipe card for a similar dish they nailed before. Here the model gets a verified how-to card for an analogous text-cleanup task, hoping it copies the recipe.
qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.plain_question: Does handing the model a recipe card from a library of past solved tasks make it clean up text more reliably?
qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.plain_answer: No. Giving the model a matching recipe card left it slightly worse (47% of tasks fully correct) than just working through rows plainly with no card (50%), and no better than handing it a random or scrambled card.
qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.why_it_matters: Pulling in a related worked example is not a free win for consistency. Before adding a retrieval library, check it beats plain step-by-step prompting and both a random and a scrambled-card control.

key_numbers.label: Tasks fully correct, plain vs with recipe card
key_numbers.sub: share of tasks where every row was exactly right; the card slightly hurt

key_numbers.label: Card matched the task type
key_numbers.sub: how often the right-family card was fetched, versus a random card

key_numbers.label: Tasks the card improved
key_numbers.sub: it helped none and made 1 worse

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of tasks solved perfectly, by method
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks where every row came out exactly right; taller is better. Plain row-by-row work is leftmost; the recipe-card method and its scrambled, batched, and random-card comparisons follow.
charts.chart_takeaway: The plain no-card bar is the tallest, and the recipe-card bar sits just below it, near the random and scrambled controls, so the card gave no gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of individual rows correct, by method
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of individual rows cleaned up exactly right; taller is better. Same five methods as before, from plain row-by-row work to the recipe-card and control versions.
charts.chart_takeaway: Plain row-by-row work again has the tallest bar, and every card-based or batched version loses rows, confirming the card does not help even row by row.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks the card helped versus hurt
charts.chart_read: For each method, one bar counts tasks it improved and one counts tasks it worsened, versus plain row-by-row work, out of 40; more helped and fewer hurt is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe-card method shows zero tasks helped and one hurt, and the controls hurt even more, so no variant produced a net win.

charts.chart_plain_title: Did the library fetch a truly similar card?
charts.chart_read: Two bars show how often the fetched card came from the same task family as the target: the real retriever versus picking a random card; taller means better-matched cards.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real retriever's bar is far taller (right family most of the time) than random, proving the null result came from useless cards, not bad fetching.

charts.chart_plain_title: Small pilot runs swung wildly before the main test
charts.chart_read: Across three tiny six-task warm-up runs, grouped bars show how often rows came out exactly right for each method; taller is better. The recipe-card method appears among plain and control methods.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe-card method jumps from worst to best across the three tiny runs, an unstable swing that motivated the larger 40-task test where its edge disappeared.

charts.chart_plain_title: On the hardest task type, cards still do not rescue accuracy
charts.chart_read: For ten tasks in the toughest (number-formatting) family, paired bars compare plain row-by-row work against the recipe-card version on share of rows correct; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On most of these hard tasks the recipe-card bar is shorter than or equal to the plain bar, showing the numeric-rounding failures are not fixed by fetching a card.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which method produced readable output
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks whose output could be read back cleanly; taller is better. The prompt-based methods sit beside the all-at-once batched method.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every method reads back cleanly except the batched all-at-once approach, which garbles output on a small slice of tasks, part of why it trails.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fetched cards get less similar past the closest match
charts.chart_read: Lines track, by fetch position (closest match first), how similar the fetched card is and how often it is the right family; the closest match is at left and higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines slope downward from the first fetch onward, showing cards past the closest match are weaker and off-topic, which is why only the top match was used.

qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.verdict_tag: Small fixes recover most failed runs
qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.concept_primer: The model builds a 24-step arithmetic recipe from memory, and one wrong step ruins the final answer. A checker that knows the correct running total at each step can hunt down and swap the few bad steps.
qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.plain_question: If the model gets a long 24-step calculation almost right, can a checker that knows the correct running total fix the few bad steps and recover the answer?
qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.plain_answer: Yes, mostly. Swapping just one or two wrong steps lifted the share of exactly-correct 24-step runs from about 27% to 91%, versus staying at 27% with no fixing at all.
qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.why_it_matters: When a model nearly solves a long problem, its mistakes are usually small and local, so a reliable checker can repair them in place rather than needing a bigger model or many more attempts. The catch: this test used a perfect answer key, so the next job is building a checker that works without one.

key_numbers.label: Exactly-correct 24-step runs
key_numbers.sub: before vs after fixing one or two bad steps

key_numbers.label: Fixed by a single swap
key_numbers.sub: changing just one step already recovers most failures

key_numbers.label: True recipe recovered
key_numbers.sub: the real program is rebuilt, not just a lucky final answer

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixing one or two steps recovers most failed 24-step runs
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by how many steps the checker is allowed to swap: none, one, or two. In each group, one bar counts runs with the right final answer and one counts runs with the fully correct recipe. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars climb from about 27% with no fixing to 91% with two swaps, and the two heights stay matched, meaning it recovers the real recipe, not just a lucky answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Longer calculations fail far more, but fixing recovers most of them
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many steps the calculation has; the vertical axis is the share of runs that come out exactly right. One line is before fixing, one is after. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The before-fixing line drops from about 86% down to 25% as runs reach 24 steps, while the after-fixing line stays near 82%, so repair holds up even on the longest chains.

charts.chart_plain_title: The repair gain holds across different prompt wordings
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by prompt style: plain, reworded, and matched pairs. In each group one bar is before fixing and one is after. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every style jumps from roughly a quarter of runs correct before fixing to around 86 to 91% after, so the gain is not a quirk of one phrasing.

qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.verdict_tag: Edit-and-run loop helped, the add-on tweak didn't
qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.concept_primer: Instead of guessing an answer in one shot, the model works like a coder: it makes one small edit to a tiny program, runs it, sees the result, and edits again over several rounds.
qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.plain_question: Does letting the model edit-run-repeat beat a single guess, and does teaching it to also predict the machine's output help even more?
qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.plain_answer: Mixed. The edit-and-run loop lifted accuracy from 10% to 43%, beating a plain one-shot answer, but the extra tweak of teaching the model to predict the machine's output actually hurt, dropping it to 36%.
qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.why_it_matters: Letting a small model act in a loop and check its own work pays off, but teaching it to narrate what the machine will show is a distraction; reward finishing the right program instead.

key_numbers.label: One-shot guess vs edit-and-run loop
key_numbers.sub: average tasks solved when the model works in a loop

key_numbers.label: Extra output-predicting tweak
key_numbers.sub: lower than the plain loop's 43%

key_numbers.label: Best possible with a perfect guide
key_numbers.sub: how far a flawless step-by-step teacher got, showing the room left

charts.chart_plain_title: The edit-and-run loop beats one-shot; the extra tweak does not help
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show the share of tasks solved across five test sets plus their average; taller is better. Each group compares a plain one-shot answer, the edit-and-run loop, the same loop with the extra output-predicting tweak, and a perfect step-by-step teacher.
charts.chart_takeaway: The loop's bars sit above the one-shot bars on average, while the tweak's bars are no taller and often shorter, and the teacher's bars tower over all, showing plenty of unclaimed room.

qwen_vm_echo_trace_distillation.concept_primer: Think of a helper that turns a word problem into a tiny program a computer runs. Here we also asked it to predict what happens when that program runs, like guessing a recipe's taste before cooking.
qwen_vm_echo_trace_distillation.plain_question: If we also teach the program-writing helper to predict what its programs do when run, does it write better programs?
qwen_vm_echo_trace_distillation.plain_answer: It depends. The helper learned to predict run outcomes very well, jumping from near-zero to about 43%, and its answer-checking retries improved slightly. But its first-try program writing did not broadly improve, and on one everyday test it dropped a few points.
qwen_vm_echo_trace_distillation.why_it_matters: Teaching a model to foresee consequences is a real, attachable skill, but predicting outcomes of a given answer barely helps it pick better answers. Next, have it predict outcomes of its own attempts, not the ideal one.

key_numbers.label: Predicting what a program does
key_numbers.sub: new outcome-guessing skill the helper gained

key_numbers.label: Success after retry attempts
key_numbers.sub: small gain on the paired everyday test

key_numbers.label: First-try success, standard test
key_numbers.sub: a slight drop, not an improvement

charts.chart_plain_title: What the outcome-prediction training changed
charts.chart_read: Three paired bars compare the original helper against the outcome-predicting one on first-try success, success-after-retry, and guessing a program's result; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The outcome-prediction bar leaps from almost nothing to about 43%, retry success edges up, but first-try success is identical, so the new skill barely spills over.

charts.chart_plain_title: First-try success across four kinds of test problems
charts.chart_read: Four paired bars, one per problem style, comparing the original helper with the outcome-predicting one on first-try success; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two helpers sit nearly level everywhere, and on the standard style the outcome-predicting bar is actually shorter, confirming no broad first-try gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Useful new examples gathered during self-practice
charts.chart_read: Paired bars for two practice rounds show the share of problems where each helper found a working program to learn from; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bars stay within a couple of points across both rounds, showing the extra training did not shrink the pool of examples the helper could learn from.

real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.verdict_tag: Fixed toolbox fits clean data, not messy
real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.concept_primer: Think of a fixed toolbox of ready-made data-cleanup tools, tested by asking whether one tool can correctly do each job, including deliberate trick jobs designed to fool a lazy match.
real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.plain_question: Can one fixed toolbox of ready-made data transformations handle both tidy spreadsheet cleanup and messy date, ID, and text jobs?
real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.plain_answer: It depends on the job. The fixed toolbox handled every tidy spreadsheet task, but slipped on messy date and ID work, covering 85% there versus 100% on clean pipelines once trick tests weeded out lucky-looking wrong answers.
real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.why_it_matters: Build a compiler onto this toolbox for tidy pipeline work now, but expand the toolbox and add tougher trick tests before trusting it on messy extraction and formatting jobs.
