charts.chart_plain_title: Jobs solved per group, before and after trick tests
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks solved for tidy pipelines, messy date/ID work, and overall; each group pairs coverage from easy tests (lighter) against coverage after tougher trick tests (darker). Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tidy-pipeline bars stay at full height after trick tests while the messy group drops, showing the fixed toolbox holds up only on clean work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trick jobs caught by tougher tests
charts.chart_read: Two bars for the same set of deliberately deceptive tasks: coverage under easy tests versus coverage after trick tests. A drop to zero is the intended, better outcome here.
charts.chart_takeaway: Coverage falls from full to nothing, confirming trick tests removed every lucky-looking wrong answer that the easy tests had let through.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many solved jobs used one tool versus two
charts.chart_read: Bars count solved tasks that needed a single tool versus two tools chained together; lighter bars are before trick tests, darker after. Height just shows how common each pattern is.
charts.chart_takeaway: The single-tool bar towers over the two-tool bar, meaning the toolbox mostly proves it can pick the right one tool, not chain several.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of easy-test winners that were actually wrong
charts.chart_read: One bar per group showing how often a candidate that passed the shown examples still failed hidden checks, for tidy, messy, and overall work. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three bars sit near a third, showing thin visible examples routinely let wrong answers slip through and why the tougher tests are needed.

rule_family_diversity_scaling.verdict_tag: Wider training variety unlocks some new-type transfer
rule_family_diversity_scaling.concept_primer: Think of the bug-fixer as a mechanic. Instead of more cars of the same make, you give it the same number spread across more makes, hoping it can then fix a make it has never touched.
rule_family_diversity_scaling.plain_question: If we train a bug-fixer on a wider variety of problem types without adding any examples, can it fix problem types it never saw during training?
rule_family_diversity_scaling.plain_answer: Yes, but modestly. Spreading the same examples across 12 problem types instead of 3 lifted fixes on never-seen types from none to about 29%, while fixes on familiar types stayed high. Nearly all the gain came from one lucky new type.
rule_family_diversity_scaling.why_it_matters: When you cannot add more training data, broadening the variety of problem types can buy some transfer to unfamiliar cases. But check whether it generalizes broadly or just happens to crack one lucky case.

key_numbers.label: Never-seen problem types fixed
key_numbers.sub: as training variety grew from 3 to 12 types

key_numbers.label: Total training examples
key_numbers.sub: held fixed at every variety level

key_numbers.label: Fixes without the step-by-step hint
key_numbers.sub: the transfer gain vanishes if the hint is removed or scrambled

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixes on new problem types rise only at the widest training variety
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many problem-type varieties were in training (3, 6, 12); the vertical axis is the share of bugs fixed, higher is better. One line tracks familiar problems, one the same problems in new formats, and one brand-new problem types.
charts.chart_takeaway: The brand-new-type line stays flat near zero until 12 varieties, where it jumps to about 29%, while familiar-problem fixes stay high throughout.

charts.chart_plain_title: The new-type fixes need a genuine step-by-step hint at both training and test
charts.chart_read: Bars compare how many never-seen-type bugs each setup fixes; taller is better. The first bar is the full trained helper with a real hint, then variants that drop or scramble the hint at test or training time, then the untrained base.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the full setup with a real hint reaches about 29%; every stripped or scrambled variant collapses to near zero, so the hint is doing the work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Almost all transfer comes from a single new problem type
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one never-seen problem type (12 tasks each); height is the share fixed, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: One type is nearly solved at 92% and another partly at 25%, while two types get zero fixes, so the gain is narrow, not broad.

charts.chart_plain_title: On familiar problems, only one bug family takes a hit as variety grows
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of familiar bugs fixed for three bug families; grouped by 3, 6, and 12 training varieties; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Two families stay perfectly fixed at every variety level, and all the wobble lands on the affine bug family.

charts.chart_plain_title: Robustness to new formats depends heavily on which bug family
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of same-problems-in-new-formats bugs fixed for three bug families, grouped by 3, 6, and 12 training varieties; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The threshold family stays near-perfect across formats, the affine family is stuck near the bottom, and the slug family slips at the widest variety.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting the right label early climbs before full fixes catch up
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is training variety (3, 6, 12); vertical axis is a share on new-type problems, higher is better. One line is how often the correct rule label appears, the other how often the bug is fully fixed.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-label line rises steadily while full fixes stay flat until 12 varieties, suggesting partial progress builds before complete repairs arrive.

charts.chart_plain_title: The transfer gain is not bought with extra thinking time
charts.chart_read: Bars show median seconds to produce one attempt on new-type problems for each setup; there is no better direction, this checks whether some setups simply ran longer.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every trained setup takes about eight seconds regardless of variety, so the widest-variety gain is not explained by spending more time per attempt.

sampled_query_filter_executor.verdict_tag: Single sampled answers teach step-by-step reasoning
sampled_query_filter_executor.concept_primer: Think of the model as a detective handed a scrambled case: it is only ever graded on one guessed answer, yet to keep scoring well across many different questions it must keep a full mental map of every possible suspect.
sampled_query_filter_executor.plain_question: If we only ever tell the model one final answer per example, can it still learn to reason step by step and track every possibility?
sampled_query_filter_executor.plain_answer: Yes. Given enough internal thinking steps, the model answered over 95% correctly even on programs three times longer than any it trained on, while two simpler designs collapsed below 12%.
sampled_query_filter_executor.why_it_matters: You need not hand a model the full internal picture during training; a single sampled answer is enough for it to build that picture itself, provided the design lets it take one thinking step per instruction.

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on long unseen programs
key_numbers.sub: full model at three times its training length

key_numbers.label: Best the simpler designs manage
key_numbers.sub: same long programs

key_numbers.label: Internal picture the model was never taught
key_numbers.sub: built on its own over training

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy on longer programs: full model vs simpler versions
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by program length, from 4 up to 24 instructions; taller is better. Each group has three bars: the full step-by-step model and two stripped-down versions. Bar height is the share of answers landing on a correct value.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full model's bars stay near the top above 95% at every length, while both simpler versions shrink toward the floor, so only the full model keeps working on long programs.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy jumps once thinking steps reach the program length
charts.chart_read: Each line is a program of a fixed length; the horizontal axis is how many internal thinking steps the model takes, and the vertical axis is the share of answers correct, where higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line stays flat and low until the thinking steps reach that program's length, then snaps up to roughly 95% or more, showing the model needs one step per instruction.

charts.chart_plain_title: Model rebuilds the full picture it was never taught
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by program length; taller is better. They compare the full model against two simpler versions on how much of the correct internal picture of all possibilities each one holds, a thing never shown during training.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full model's bars stand near 94 to 98% while the two simpler versions barely clear the floor under 6%, meaning only it reconstructed the untaught internal picture.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every kind of question is answered equally well
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by program length, with one bar per kind of question asked, including two single-value questions and two combined-value questions; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All four question types sit together above 94% at every length, so combined questions are answered just as well as the simple single-value ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: The internal picture forms on its own during training
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training progress; two lines are shown, the answer the model is graded on and its untaught internal picture, and higher is better for both.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untaught internal-picture line starts near zero but climbs to track the graded-answer line, both finishing near 98%, showing the picture builds itself without being taught.

charts.chart_plain_title: A smaller repeat of the task shows the same result
charts.chart_read: This is a separate, smaller version of the task; bars are grouped by program length, comparing the full model against two simpler versions, and taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full model again holds near 99% at every length while both simpler versions fall below 31%, confirming the main finding repeats in an independent run.

sparse_support_memory_executor.verdict_tag: One memory slot per possibility restores perfect accuracy
sparse_support_memory_executor.concept_primer: Imagine tracking a puzzle where several answers are still possible; you keep one sticky note per candidate. If you have enough notes, you never lose the true one.
sparse_support_memory_executor.plain_question: Does a small working memory need a separate slot for every possible answer to solve step-by-step reasoning puzzles exactly?
sparse_support_memory_executor.plain_answer: Yes, when the memory has one slot for every candidate answer, it solves every puzzle perfectly at all lengths; with fewer slots, accuracy falls steadily the longer the puzzle runs.
sparse_support_memory_executor.why_it_matters: Reasoning that keeps multiple options alive breaks not from too little raw memory size but from too few addressable slots; give one slot per live possibility.

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on longest puzzles, too few slots
key_numbers.sub: half the needed slots, hardest length

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on longest puzzles, full slots
key_numbers.sub: one slot per possibility

key_numbers.label: Puzzles solved exactly at full capacity
key_numbers.sub: every length, no errors

charts.chart_plain_title: Fewer memory slots lose accuracy as puzzles get longer
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is puzzle length; vertical axis is how often the exact answer is kept, higher is better. Each line is a memory size; the top line has one slot per possibility.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-capacity line stays flat at the top across all lengths, while every smaller-memory line slopes downward, meaning missing slots cost accuracy on longer puzzles.

charts.chart_plain_title: The same pattern repeats with far more possibilities
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is puzzle length; vertical axis is how often the exact answer is kept, higher is better. Each line is a memory size, with the top line holding one slot per possibility.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the full-capacity line stays perfectly flat at the top, confirming that even a large-but-incomplete memory leaks accuracy as puzzles lengthen.

structured_slot_initializer_ladder.verdict_tag: Constrained slot setup beats a bigger network
structured_slot_initializer_ladder.concept_primer: The job splits in two: first place each unknown value into its own labeled slot, then a flawless calculator runs the steps. Any error can only come from a sloppy setup.
structured_slot_initializer_ladder.plain_question: When a flawless calculator handles the math, what kind of setup best places each value into its own slot?
structured_slot_initializer_ladder.plain_answer: Yes, a specific structure won: a setup that forces each value into its own distinct slot landed 98.1% of answers on the exact right target, versus 55.5% for a bigger general-purpose network.
structured_slot_initializer_ladder.why_it_matters: When a task has a fixed, exact backend, invest in a setup that guarantees every value claims its own slot rather than scaling up a generic network. The constraint, not size, closes the gap.

key_numbers.label: Answer landing on the exact right target
key_numbers.sub: generic network vs constrained setup

key_numbers.label: Values that got their own slot
key_numbers.sub: generic network vs constrained setup

key_numbers.label: Gap to a perfect setup
key_numbers.sub: constrained setup trails the ideal ceiling

charts.chart_plain_title: How well each setup style places the answer
charts.chart_read: Each group along the bottom is a setup style, from a perfect reference on the left to a plain general-purpose network on the right; taller bars mean more of the answer landed on the exact right target, which is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars climb steadily as more structure is added, with the constrained setup nearly reaching the perfect reference while the plain network sits far below it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Answer quality stays flat as the task runs longer
charts.chart_read: The bottom axis is how many steps the calculator runs; the height is how much of the answer stays on the exact target, higher being better; each line is one setup style, with the perfect reference on top.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line runs nearly flat across all lengths, showing the calculator neither repairs nor worsens the setup, so final quality is locked in at the start.

charts.chart_plain_title: Learning the rule is not enough without slot coverage
charts.chart_read: Each group is a setup style; one bar shows whether it learned the value relationship, the other whether every value got its own slot; taller is better on both.
charts.chart_takeaway: Some setups score high on learning the rule yet leave many slots doubled up, and only the constrained setup reaches full marks on giving each value its own slot.

charts.chart_plain_title: Constrained setup tracks the ideal across problem sizes
charts.chart_read: The bottom axis is increasing problem size, from small to largest; the height is how much of the answer lands on target, higher being better; one bar is the learned constrained setup, the other the ideal reference.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned setup's bars stay within about two points of the ideal reference at every size, holding near-perfect even at the largest problem.

targeted_bridge_allocation.verdict_tag: Targeting hard cases wins only narrowly
targeted_bridge_allocation.concept_primer: Think of a fixed study budget: the model gets a set number of worked examples showing how to combine two skills it never saw combined, and you decide which combinations to spend them on.
targeted_bridge_allocation.plain_question: When you can only give a fixed set of worked examples, does concentrating them on the hardest skill-combinations beat spreading them evenly?
targeted_bridge_allocation.plain_answer: It depends. Concentrating the worked examples on the hardest skill-combinations edged out spreading them evenly (33% vs 28% of new combinations repaired), but the gap was small; where examples went, and whether each came with a correct step-by-step solution, mattered far more than how many there were.
targeted_bridge_allocation.why_it_matters: When you cannot add more training data, spend your limited worked examples on the combinations the model currently fails, and always pair each one with a correct step-by-step solution, since that pairing is what actually makes them work.

key_numbers.label: Even spread vs concentrated on hard cases
key_numbers.sub: share of never-seen skill-combinations repaired

key_numbers.label: Untrained starting point
key_numbers.sub: the frozen model repairs none of them

key_numbers.label: With correct solution vs without
key_numbers.sub: removing the step-by-step solution collapses repair

charts.chart_plain_title: Where you spend a few worked examples changes repair
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of never-seen skill-combinations a trained model fixes; the leftmost frozen bar is the untrained starting point; bars move from spreading examples evenly to concentrating them on the hardest cases. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The concentrated hard-case bar is tallest at 33%, only 5 points above even spreading, yet every trained bar towers over the frozen model's 0%.

charts.chart_plain_title: A correct worked-out solution in training is what makes it work
charts.chart_read: Three groups of bars, one per test type; colors are training with a correct step-by-step solution, with none, or with a scrambled one. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-solution bars dwarf the others on every test, dropping from 78% to under 20% even on familiar problems once the solution is removed or scrambled.

charts.chart_plain_title: Concentrating examples opens skill-combinations even spreading leaves stuck
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by unseen skill-combination family; colors are even spread, concentrated on hard cases, and a control that concentrates on easy cases. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Concentrating on hard cases lifts two families off 0% that even spreading never cracks, while the already-easy families stay maxed out.

charts.chart_plain_title: Flooding one skill with examples is not the lever
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows repair on never-seen combinations for the even-spread baseline versus pouring sixteen examples into a single skill family. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Overloading one family helps modestly at best (34% for modulo) and actually lowers overall repair for length, so sheer volume is not what helps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Concentrating examples costs nothing on familiar problems
charts.chart_read: Bars per training condition, two colors for familiar problems and reworded versions of them; the frozen bar is the untrained baseline. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every trained condition clusters in a 69-83% band, so concentrating the worked examples on hard cases sacrifices nothing on familiar or reworded problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: At test time the model still needs the worked-out solution shown
charts.chart_read: Three test types along the bottom; colors are giving the model a correct step-by-step solution, none, or a scrambled one in the prompt. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Stripping or scrambling the solution from the prompt crashes even familiar-problem repair from 78% to under 20%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Repair depends heavily on the kind of task
charts.chart_read: One bar per task mechanism, sorted from most to least repaired, for the concentrated hard-case model on never-seen combinations. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Repair splits sharply: step-through and text-cleanup tasks reach 100%, while remainder-arithmetic tasks stay flat at 0%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Total number of examples does not predict repair
charts.chart_read: Each dot is a training condition; left-to-right is the total worked examples used, up is repair on never-seen combinations. A rising trend would mean more examples help.
charts.chart_takeaway: Conditions with the exact same example count scatter from 20% to 33%, so how examples are allocated matters more than how many.

trace_keyed_symbol_repair.verdict_tag: Reading the error output enables repair
trace_keyed_symbol_repair.concept_primer: When a test fails, its error message prints the correct value the code should have used. This task asks whether the model can learn to pull that value out of the error and paste it into a fix.
trace_keyed_symbol_repair.plain_question: Can the model learn to fix a broken value by copying the right one out of the failing test's error message?
trace_keyed_symbol_repair.plain_answer: Yes. After training to use the error message, the model fixed all 60 test cases on both familiar and unseen value styles, while every version that lacked the error message or was trained to ignore it fixed none.
trace_keyed_symbol_repair.why_it_matters: A failing test's output is a usable source of answers, not just a red light. Training a model to actually read it, rather than assuming it will on its own, is what unlocks the fix.

key_numbers.label: Cases fixed after training to use the error message
key_numbers.sub: none fixed before, all 60 fixed after

key_numbers.label: Same trained model when the error message is taken away
key_numbers.sub: the message content, not the format, carries the answer

key_numbers.label: Untrained model copying the right value from the message
key_numbers.sub: rarely does it on its own

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the model trained to read the error message fixes the code
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of tasks fixed for one setup; two bars per setup show familiar value styles and brand-new value styles. Taller is better. Only the rightmost setup, trained to use the error message and given it, rises.
charts.chart_takeaway: Four setups sit flat at zero and the trained-with-message one hits the top on both styles, so reading the error message is what does the work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Take the error message away and the same model stops fixing anything
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks fixed by the one trained model under three inference conditions: correct message, no message, and a mismatched message. Two bars per condition for familiar and new value styles. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the correct-message bars reach the top; no-message and mismatched-message drop to zero, showing the answer lives in the message content itself.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the right value gets copied out of the error message
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each setup pastes the exact correct value into its fix, split into familiar and new value styles. Taller is better. The untrained model shows a short bar; the trained model reaches the top.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained model copies the right value only about 18% of the time and near-never on new styles, while training makes copying it reliable every time.

charts.chart_plain_title: Deleting the wrong value is easy; writing the right one is not
charts.chart_read: For each setup, one bar is how often it removes the wrong value and another is how often the code is actually fixed, on familiar-style tasks. Taller is better. Most setups remove the wrong value but leave the fix bar flat.
charts.chart_takeaway: Nearly every trained variant deletes the wrong value, but only the one that reads the correct message writes the right replacement and passes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Well-formed edits are cheap; correct edits need the error message
charts.chart_read: For each setup, bars show how often the edit is well-formed, the code still runs, and the tests actually pass, on familiar-style tasks. Taller is better. The first two measures fill up for every trained setup; only the last separates them.
charts.chart_takeaway: All trained setups produce clean, runnable edits, but only the model that reads the error message produces edits that pass the tests.

charts.chart_plain_title: Give the model enough room to write the whole fix
charts.chart_read: Two setups differ only in how much output length the model is allowed. Bars show whether the edit is usable and whether it fixes the code, on a small pilot. Taller is better. The tight-limit setup is flat; the roomier one fills up.
charts.chart_takeaway: With too little output room the model cannot finish a usable edit at all, but doubling the room flips it to fully fixing the pilot cases.

qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.verdict_tag: Surgical token-preference training backfired without its precondition
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.concept_primer: When the model 'thinks' before answering, some single word choices early in its reasoning appear to steer it toward success or failure. We sampled sixteen reasoning attempts per task, found the exact fork points where attempts that went one way usually succeeded and attempts that went the other way usually failed, and nudged the model's preference at exactly those forks — using a published technique that had previously cured runaway repetition by demoting a handful of overconfident tokens.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.plain_question: Can we make the model a better agent by nudging single word-choices at the reasoning forks where its own successful and failed attempts diverge?
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.plain_answer: No — at this dose it made things slightly worse, and a decisive control explains why. A twin model trained on the same forks with the success labels scrambled degraded by the same amount, so the harm came from the nudging procedure itself, not from bad steering directions. The published technique works when demoting a token that towers over its alternatives; our fork tokens sat at near-parity with their siblings, so the trainer had to force apart tokens that started as equals, which subtly disrupted the flow of thinking (the model finished its reasoning naturally only half as often). Bonus findings: the repetition-loop problem the technique was famous for fixing essentially does not exist at everyday thinking budgets (about 0.1% of attempts), and the technique's built-in safety tether did prevent the catastrophic collapse an earlier preference-training experiment suffered.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.why_it_matters: This closes off the naive version of a tempting idea — steering thought at its fork points — and pins down the precondition any future attempt must satisfy: only demote tokens the model is confidently over-choosing. It also saved the held-out benchmark from being spent on a broken recipe (a pre-registered gate cancelled that evaluation automatically), and banked a free measurement showing reasoning loops live only at very long thinking budgets, not everyday ones.

qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.verdict_tag: One cheap round buys the jump; nothing buys the next one
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.concept_primer: After our training playground lifted the held-out benchmark dramatically, we asked what makes it work and whether pushing harder helps: we trained on just one task world, made all worlds much harder, taught the model to recover when its thinking gets cut off, and matched training to the exact thinking budget used at test time.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.plain_question: Can we push past the score plateau by training harder, broader, or smarter — and which ingredient caused the original jump?
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.plain_answer: No push moved the benchmark: nine careful paired tests all land the trained model in the same 0.38-0.45 band (from a ~0.12 baseline). Surprisingly, practicing on a single rich task world works almost as well as practicing on ten, so the jump comes from learning a general habit — finish thinking, commit to a short answer — not from broad content. Harder practice levels did teach the model to solve harder playground tasks, but the benchmark band still refused to move.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.why_it_matters: It draws a clean map: one cheap training round buys a huge general improvement, and after that this whole style of self-training is spent. The remaining gap needs a different kind of lever — external tools finding solutions the model can't sample itself, or reinforcement-style training aimed at the specific failures — which is exactly where the program goes next.

qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.verdict_tag: Pull-up preserved some signal, but non-local weight changes erased capability
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.concept_primer: The first thought-steering run nudged ordinary coin-flip-like word choices and made reasoning less likely to finish. This follow-up measured entropy and varentropy at each fork, kept only cases where a failed word clearly dominated but a successful alternative remained plausible, then compared lowering the failed word with gently raising the successful one. A scrambled-label twin tested whether any benefit came from the real outcome direction or merely from training.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.plain_question: Can we unlock better reasoning by changing only overconfident wrong turns, and is lifting a fruitful alternative safer than suppressing the wrong turn?
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.plain_answer: No capability unlock at this dose. Gently lifting the successful word was clearly safer than pushing the failed word down, and real outcome labels beat scrambled labels by 6.25 points on the parent-style gym and 13.89 points on the coding agent. But the edit was not truly local: nearby non-target logits still moved beyond the frozen safety bar. On 72 hidden-tested repository repairs, the unchanged model solved 43, pull-up solved 39, push-down 34, and scrambled pull-up 29. Fresh reasoning tests were flat at the short budget and negative at the long one, so the benchmark gate correctly stopped before any blackbox run.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.why_it_matters: The experiment separates useful steering information from a useful steering mechanism. The outcome-conditioned fork directions contain some real signal, but editing one token in the loss does not create a context-local change in a shared neural network. Entropy and varentropy are helpful routing diagnostics, not monotone correctness scores: the lowest-varentropy quartile was actually safest. The next experiment should first earn logit locality with a smaller or context-gated intervention; simply collecting more pivots or pushing harder is not justified.
